Typical maximum centrifugal advance for a 4-stroke petrol engine: **20°–28° crankshaft**.

### 2.5 Vacuum Advance Mechanism — Constructional Architecture

**Major Components:**

1. **Advance Diaphragm** — A flexible elastomeric or metal diaphragm clamped at its periphery, dividing the housing into two chambers.
2. **Spring-Loaded Chamber (Atmospheric side)** — Connects to atmosphere via a small vent or filter; contains a calibrated **return spring** pushing the diaphragm toward the manifold side.
3. **Vacuum Chamber (Manifold side)** — Connected via a vacuum pipe to the **carburetor venturi** or throttle body, just above the throttle plate.
4. **Vacuum Pickup Point** — The location in the intake tract where vacuum is tapped — this is critical and differs between **ported vacuum** (above throttle plate) and **manifold vacuum** (below).
5. **Diaphragm Link Rod** — Connects the diaphragm center to the **movable breaker plate** (the same plate that houses the contact breaker points).
6. **Breaker Plate** — A circular plate inside the distributor carrying the **contact breaker points**, capacitor, and cam-following rubbing block. It rotates on a fixed bearing to advance/retard timing.

### 2.6 Vacuum Advance — Operating Principle

At **closed throttle (idle)**:
- Manifold vacuum is high but **above-throttle venturi vacuum is near zero** (because airflow is minimal).
- If tapped at venturi → vacuum advance is **0°**.
- If tapped at manifold → vacuum is high, advance may be **maximum** (this is why modern systems use **ported** pickup).

At **part throttle (cruising)**:
- Venturi vacuum rises → diaphragm pulls inward → linkage rotates breaker plate against distributor rotation → **advance increases**.

At **wide-open throttle (WOT)**:
- Venturi vacuum collapses to nearly atmospheric → diaphragm pushed back by spring → advance returns to **0°**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Advance at Part-Throttle?** At part throttle, the throttle plate restricts airflow, creating high venturi vacuum. This corresponds to **light load, lean mixture**, which burns *slowly* → needs *more* advance. At WOT, rich mixture burns fast → needs *less* advance.

### 2.7 Vacuum Advance — Pressure Differential Equation

The net force on the diaphragm is:

$$F_{net} = (P_{atm} - P_{manifold}) \cdot A_d - k_v \cdot y$$

where:
- $A_d$ = effective diaphragm area
- $k_v$ = vacuum spring stiffness
- $y$ = diaphragm displacement
- $P_{atm} - P_{manifold}$ = vacuum pressure differential

At equilibrium:

$$\boxed{\Delta P \cdot A_d = k_v \cdot y \implies y = \frac{\Delta P \cdot A_d}{k_v}}$$

The advance angle is linearly proportional to diaphragm displacement via a mechanical linkage:

$$\theta_v = \lambda \cdot y \quad \text{(where } \lambda \text{ is linkage ratio)}$$

Thus vacuum advance is **linearly proportional to manifold vacuum drop** below a threshold (often ~100 mmHg for ported systems).

### 2.8 Total Ignition Advance

$$\boxed{\theta_{total} = \theta_{initial} + \theta_{centrifugal}(N) + \theta_{vacuum}(\Delta P)}$$

where:
- $\theta_{initial}$ = static base timing (typically 5°–10° BTDC)
- $\theta_{centrifugal}$ = function of engine RPM
- $\theta_{vacuum}$ = function of manifold vacuum / load

### 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| Centrifugal force on flyweight | $F_c = m \omega^2 r$ | Newtons (N) | $m$ = kg, $\omega$ = rad/s, $r$ = m |
| Spring restoring force | $F_s = kx$ | N | Linear regime |
| Flyweight equilibrium | $m\omega^2(r+x) = kx$ | — | Used to derive advance vs RPM curve |
| Total advance | $\theta_T = \theta_0 + \theta_c + \theta_v$ | degrees (crank) | Algebraic sum |
| Vacuum advance linkage | $\theta_v = \dfrac{\lambda A_d \Delta P}{k_v}$ | degrees | Linear approximation |
| Spark energy (typical) | $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ | milli-Joules | $C \approx 0.2$–$1 \, \mu$F, $V \approx 20$–$40$ kV |
| Spark plug gap | $g = 0.6$–$1.2$ mm | mm | Varies by engine type |
| Breakdown voltage (Paschen) | $V_b = f(pd)$ | kV | Empirical curve; ~10 kV/mm at atmospheric |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Centrifugal Advance Angle vs RPM

**Objective:** Derive an explicit expression relating centrifugal advance angle $\theta_c$ to engine RPM.

**Step 1 — Flyweight Geometry Setup**

Consider a single flyweight pivoted at point $P$ on the breaker plate. The center of mass $G$ is located at distance $L$ from pivot $P$. When at rest (or at low RPM), $G$ lies at radial distance $r_0$ from the distributor shaft axis.

**Step 2 — Free Body Diagram**

Two forces act on the flyweight in the rotating frame:
- Centrifugal force acting radially outward through $G$: $F_c = m\omega^2 (r_0 + \Delta r)$
- Spring tension (and gravity, neglected if spring is dominant): $F_s = k \Delta r$ opposing outward motion

where $\Delta r$ is the additional radial displacement of $G$ from its rest position.

**Step 3 — Moment Equilibrium about Pivot $P$**

Let the spring act at distance $L_s$ from pivot $P$. Taking moments:

$$F_c \cdot L_c = F_s \cdot L_s$$

$$m \omega^2 (r_0 + \Delta r) \cdot L_c = k \Delta r \cdot L_s$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\omega^2$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega^2 &= \frac{k \Delta r \, L_s}{m L_c (r_0 + \Delta r)} \\[6pt]
\omega^2 &= \frac{k L_s}{m L_c} \cdot \frac{\Delta r}{r_0 + \Delta r}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Relate $\Delta r$ to Advance Angle $\theta_c$**

The breaker plate rotates about the distributor shaft axis. Geometrically:

$$\Delta r = R \cdot \tan(\theta_c)$$

where $R$ is the effective moment arm of the linkage.

**Step 6 — Substituting Back**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega^2 &= \frac{k L_s}{m L_c} \cdot \frac{R \tan\theta_c}{r_0 + R \tan\theta_c} \\[6pt]
\omega^2 \left[r_0 + R\tan\theta_c\right] &= \frac{k L_s R}{m L_c} \tan\theta_c \\[6pt]
\omega^2 r_0 &= \tan\theta_c \left[\frac{k L_s R}{m L_c} - \omega^2 R\right] \\[6pt]
\tan\theta_c &= \frac{\omega^2 r_0}{R \left[\dfrac{k L_s}{m L_c} - \omega^2\right]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert $\omega$ to RPM**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

where $N$ = engine RPM.

**Step 8 — Final Expression (Crank Degrees)**

Because the distributor rotates at half crankshaft speed, the advance angle measured at the crank is $2\theta_c$:

$$\boxed{\theta_{c,crank} = 2 \arctan\left[\dfrac{r_0 \left(\dfrac{\pi N}{30}\right)^2}{R \left(\dfrac{k L_s}{m L_c} - \left(\dfrac{\pi N}{30}\right)^2\right)}\right]}$$

This produces a characteristic **rising, asymptotic curve** matching the empirical centrifugal advance curve.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Centrifugal Advance

**Given:**
- Flyweight mass $m = 50$ g $= 0.05$ kg
- Initial radius $r_0 = 0.025$ m
- Spring stiffness $k = 800$ N/m
- Pivot geometry ratio $\dfrac{L_s}{L_c} = 0.6$
- Linkage radius $R = 0.020$ m

**Find:** Centrifugal advance at $N = 3000$ RPM.

**Step 1 — Compute $\omega$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} \\[6pt]
\omega &= 100\pi = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\omega^2$**

$$\omega^2 = (314.16)^2 = 98{,}696 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Plug into the formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\theta_c &= \frac{(98696)(0.025)}{(0.020)\left[\dfrac{(800)(0.6)}{0.05} - 98696\right]} \\[6pt]
\tan\theta_c &= \frac{2467.4}{0.02 \times [9600 - 98696]} \\[6pt]
\tan\theta_c &= \frac{2467.4}{0.02 \times (-89096)} \\[6pt]
\tan\theta_c &= \frac{2467.4}{-1781.9} \approx -1.385
\end{aligned}
$$

**Negative sign indicates** our parameter values yield a critical RPM below 3000 — the flyweights are already at their mechanical stop. **Interpretation:** The mechanism has reached maximum advance before 3000 RPM — a design reality where further RPM rise adds no advance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note for KTU:** The exact formula isn't tested numerically — but students *must* understand that centrifugal advance is **non-linear** in RPM and **asymptotically saturates** at a maximum set by mechanical stops.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Both Advance Mechanisms

| Parameter | Centrifugal Advance | Vacuum Advance |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Input Variable** | Engine RPM (N) | Manifold Vacuum ($\Delta P$) |
| **Sensor / Transducer** | Mechanical flyweights | Diaphragm (pressure-to-displacement) |
| **Output** | Rotation of breaker plate (advance) | Rotation of breaker plate (advance) |
| **Effect at Idle** | Zero advance | Zero advance (with ported vacuum) |
| **Effect at Cruise** | Moderate advance | Maximum advance |
| **Effect at WOT** | Maximum advance | Zero advance |
| **Operating Range** | 0° – 28° (crank) | 0° – 15° (crank) |
| **System Type** | Open-loop mechanical | Open-loop mechanical |
| **Failure Mode** | Spring fatigue → erratic advance | Diaphragm leak → no advance |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Centrifugal Advance Mechanism — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine RPM Rise] --> B[Distributor Shaft Speed Increase]
    B --> C[Centrifugal Force on Flyweights: m omega squared r]
    C --> D[Flyweights Pivot Outward Against Spring Force]
    D --> E[Linkage Translates Radial Motion to Angular Motion]
    E --> F[Breaker Plate Rotates Opposite to Shaft]
    F --> G[Contact Breaker Opens Earlier in Cycle]
    G --> H[Spark Occurs Earlier = Ignition Advance]

    I[Return Spring Force: kx] -. opposes .-> D
    J[Mechanical Stops] -. limits max .-> D
```

### 4.2 Vacuum Advance Mechanism — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Throttle Position Change] --> B[Intake Manifold Pressure Change]
    B --> C[Venturi Vacuum Deltap Variation]
    C --> D[Vacuum Line Transmits to Diaphragm Chamber]
    D --> E[Diaphragm Deflects Against Atmospheric Spring]
    E --> F[Link Rod Pushes Breaker Plate]
    F --> G[Contact Breaker Rotates for Advance]
    G --> H[Spark Timing Advances Earlier]

    P[Atmospheric Pressure Side] -. counteracts .-> E
    Q[Return Spring: kv times y] -. opposes .-> E
```

### 4.3 Spark Plug — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[High Voltage from Ignition Coil Secondary] --> B[Centre Terminal]
    B --> C[Centre Electrode]
    C --> D{Spark Gap}
    D --> E[Ground Electrode Strap]
    E --> F[Metal Shell Threaded into Cylinder Head]
    F --> G[Engine Ground Return Path]
    D --> H[Arc Plasma Initiates Flame Kernel]
    H --> I[Combustion of Air Fuel Mixture]
```

### 4.4 Spark Plug Internal Construction — Cross-Sectional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP[Spark Plug Assembly]
        CT[Centre Terminal Brass Stud]
        R1[Ceramic Insulator Al2O3]
        R2[Conductive Glass Seal]
        CE[Centre Electrode Nickel or Platinum or Iridium]
        CE2[Copper Core Heat Conductor]
        SP1[Steel Shell with Thread]
        GE[Ground Electrode Side]
        G[Gap 0.6 to 1.2 mm]
        GS[Gasket Ring Seal]
    end
    CT --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> CE
    CE --> G
    G --> GE
    CE --- CE2
    SP1 --> GS
```

### 4.5 Complete Ignition Advance Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSORS[Inputs]
        RPM[Engine Speed Sensor]
        LOAD[Manifold Vacuum Sensor]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[Mechanical Controller]
        CAM[Centrifugal Governor]
        DIAPH[Vacuum Diaphragm]
    end

    subgraph ACT[Actuator]
        PLATE[Breaker Plate Rotation]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output]
        SPARK[Spark Timing Advance]
    end

    RPM --> CAM --> PLATE
    LOAD --> DIAPH --> PLATE
    PLATE --> SPARK
    SPARK -.->|Combustion feedback| RPM
    SPARK -.->|Vacuum feedback| LOAD
```

### 4.6 Spark Plug Heat Range Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Spark Plug Selection] --> B{Operating Temperature Requirement}
    B -->|High Temp Long Burn| C[Hot Plug]
    B -->|Low Temp Quick Burn| D[Cold Plug]
    C --> E[Long Insulator Nose + Small Contact Area]
    D --> F[Short Insulator Nose + Large Contact Area]
    E --> G[Heat Path Length INCREASES]
    F --> H[Heat Path Length DECREASES]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the working principle of the centrifugal advance mechanism with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The centrifugal advance mechanism is a speed-sensitive device fitted on the **distributor shaft** that advances the ignition timing as engine RPM increases.

- Two **flyweights** are pivoted on the breaker plate. At low RPM, springs hold them inward → no advance. **(1 mark)**
- As RPM rises, centrifugal force $F_c = m\omega^2 r$ overcomes spring tension and the flyweights swing outward. **(1 mark)**
- A linkage converts this outward motion into angular rotation of the breaker plate opposite to shaft rotation, advancing the spark instant. Maximum advance is limited by mechanical stops. **(1 mark)**

---

**Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is the function of a spark plug? List the major components of a standard spark plug.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Function:** A spark plug delivers high-voltage current from the ignition coil into the combustion chamber, creating an electric arc across an air gap to ignite the air-fuel mixture. **(1 mark)**
- **Major Components:** Centre terminal, ceramic insulator, conductive glass seal, centre electrode, copper core (heat conductor), ground electrode, steel shell with threads, gasket ring. **(2 marks — list any four)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a centrifugal advance mechanism with a sketch. Discuss the effect of spring stiffness on the advance curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a vacuum advance mechanism. Discuss how it differs from the centrifugal advance mechanism. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A(a) — Centrifugal Advance Mechanism (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**

The centrifugal advance mechanism is housed inside the ignition distributor. Its components are:

- **Distributor shaft** — rotates at half engine speed.
- **Breaker plate** — a circular plate carrying the contact breaker points; it can rotate about the distributor shaft axis through a small angle.
- **Two flyweights** — pivot-mounted on the breaker plate by pivot pins; their center of mass lies at radius $r$ from the shaft axis.
- **Two return springs** — each connects a flyweight to a stationary point on the breaker plate, providing inward restoring force.
- **Linkage lever** — converts outward radial motion of flyweights into tangential rotation of the breaker plate.

**Working (3 marks):**

At low RPM, centrifugal force is small; springs keep flyweights inwards → **zero advance** at idle.

As RPM increases, $F_c = m\omega^2 r$ grows quadratically. Flyweights pivot outward against spring tension. The link rotates the breaker plate opposite to shaft rotation by an angle $\theta_c$. Since the contact points open earlier in the cycle, the spark fires **earlier** (advanced).

Maximum advance (~20°–28° crank) is reached when flyweights hit **mechanical stops**.

**Effect of Spring Stiffness (1 mark):**

$$F_s = kx \implies \omega^2_{critical} = \frac{k \cdot x}{m \cdot r}$$

- **Higher $k$** → higher RPM needed to begin advancing → **delayed** advance curve (less aggressive).
- **Lower $k$** → advance begins at lower RPM → more aggressive advance.

---

### Model Solution for Question A(b) — Vacuum Advance Mechanism (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**

- **Diaphragm** — clamped at edges, divides housing into atmospheric and vacuum chambers.
- **Atmospheric chamber** — contains the return spring pushing diaphragm outward.
- **Vacuum chamber** — connected via hose to the carburetor venturi (ported vacuum).
- **Link rod** — connects diaphragm to the breaker plate.

**Working (3 marks):**

At **idle** (throttle nearly closed, ported venturi vacuum ≈ 0), diaphragm is pushed outward by spring → **zero vacuum advance**.

At **part throttle** (cruising), venturi vacuum rises → atmospheric pressure pushes diaphragm inward against the spring → link rod pulls breaker plate → **advance increases**.

At **WOT**, venturi vacuum collapses → diaphragm returns → **zero vacuum advance**.

**Difference from Centrifugal (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Centrifugal | Vacuum |
|--------|-------------|--------|
| Input | RPM | Manifold Vacuum |
| Sensor | Flyweights | Diaphragm |
| Primary Need | Compensate finite flame speed at high RPM | Compensate slow burning of lean mixtures at part throttle |

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the constructional details of a spark plug. Mention the function of the copper core in the centre electrode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the different types of spark plugs based on (i) insulator nose length (heat range), and (ii) electrode material. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B(a) — Spark Plug Construction (7 marks)

**Sketch and Components (5 marks):**

A spark plug consists of:

1. **Centre terminal** — brass stud connected to the coil HT lead.
2. **Ceramic insulator** — high-purity **alumina (Al₂O₃)** providing >30 kV dielectric strength and thermal resistance.
3. **Conductive glass seal** — bonds insulator to centre electrode; provides gas-tight seal.
4. **Centre electrode** — typically nickel alloy, with an embedded **copper core**.
5. **Copper core** (heat extractor) — runs through the centre electrode, dramatically increasing heat conduction.
6. **Steel shell** — hex-headed, threaded (M14×1.25 typical) for screwing into the cylinder head.
7. **Ground electrode** — welded to the shell, forming the spark gap with the centre electrode.
8. **Spark gap** — typically 0.6–1.2 mm; determines breakdown voltage.
9. **Crush gasket** — seals combustion pressure.

**Function of Copper Core (2 marks):**

- Nickel has poor thermal conductivity (~90 W/m·K); copper has high conductivity (~400 W/m·K).
- The copper core rapidly **transfers heat from the insulator tip** to the metal shell, which is in turn cooled by the cylinder head water jacket.
- This prevents the centre electrode from overheating → avoids **pre-ignition** and **insulator tip erosion**.
- Allows a longer insulator nose (hot plug) without thermal runaway.

---

### Model Solution for Question B(b) — Spark Plug Types (7 marks)

**(i) Based on Insulator Nose Length — Heat Range (4 marks):**

- **Hot Plugs** — Long insulator nose, small contact area with metal shell. Heat path is long → tip runs hotter (~850°C). Used in **low-power, low-RPM engines** where heat must be retained to burn off deposits.
- **Cold Plugs** — Short insulator nose, large contact area. Heat path short → tip runs cooler (~500°C). Used in **high-performance, high-RPM, high-compression engines** to prevent pre-ignition.
- **Standard/Medium** — Intermediate nose length for general-purpose engines.

> **Heat Range Order:** Hot ← Medium → Cold

**(ii) Based on Electrode Material (3 marks):**

| Type | Material | Service Life | Cost |
|------|----------|--------------|------|
| **Copper-core Nickel** | Ni-alloy with Cu core | ~20,000 km | Low |
| **Platinum** | Pt tip welded or sintered | ~100,000 km | Medium |
| **Iridium** | Ir tip (0.4 mm) | ~120,000 km + | High |
| **Double Platinum / Iridium** | Both electrodes | ~150,000 km | Very High |
| **Silver** | Ag centre electrode | ~50,000 km | Medium |

Finer electrode tip → smaller spark gap → higher voltage concentration → easier arc initiation → better idle and lean-burn performance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse "centrifugal advance" with "vacuum advance" triggers.** Centrifugal = RPM; Vacuum = Manifold Vacuum. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Always state the maximum advance in CRANK degrees** (not distributor degrees). Distributor rotates at half engine speed, so multiply by 2.
> 3. **For spark plugs**, students often forget the **copper core** function. This is a guaranteed 2-mark question in KTU exams — write at least two points: (i) high thermal conductivity, (ii) prevents pre-ignition and tip erosion.
> 4. **Hot vs Cold plug**: students often reverse these. **Long nose = Hot**, **Short nose = Cold**. Write it explicitly.
> 5. Always draw a **labeled sketch** — even a rough block diagram fetches partial marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Total Advance = Initial + Centrifugal + Vacuum**
- [x] **Centrifugal Advance** = RPM-driven, flyweight mechanism, **quadratic** with $\omega$
- [x] **Vacuum Advance** = Load-driven, diaphragm mechanism, **linear** with $\Delta P$
- [x] Centrifugal **maximum ≈ 20°–28°** crank; Vacuum **maximum ≈ 10°–15°** crank
- [x] **Ported vacuum** pickup is preferred over manifold vacuum to ensure **zero advance at idle**
- [x] Spark plug **gap** = 0.6–1.2 mm; breakdown voltage ~10 kV/mm
- [x] **Copper core** = heat extractor inside centre electrode → prevents overheating
- [x] **Ceramic insulator** = high-purity **alumina (Al₂O₃)**, dielectric strength >30 kV
- [x] **Hot plug** = **long** insulator nose → retains heat → low-power engines
- [x] **Cold plug** = **short** insulator nose → dissipates heat → high-performance engines
- [x] Electrode materials ranked by life: **Ni < Pt < Ir < Double-Ir/PT**
- [x] Centrifugal spring stiffness $k$: higher $k$ → delayed advance start
- [x] Vacuum diaphragm: atmospheric pressure vs manifold vacuum differential drives advance

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Fuel Injection System Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT501 - AUTOMOBILE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electronic Fuel Injection System Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 – Electronic Fuel Injection System Overview

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (as per 2024 Scheme syllabus):**
> An **Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI) System** is an electronically controlled, microprocessor-based fuel delivery system in which an **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** precisely meters and injects fuel into the engine's intake manifold or directly into the combustion chamber, based on real-time inputs from multiple sensors, in order to maintain an optimal **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** under all operating conditions.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a highly skilled chef preparing a dish. Instead of blindly throwing ingredients (fuel) into a pan (engine), the chef constantly *tastes* the mixture (sensors), adjusts the flame (throttle), and adds precise pinches of salt (fuel) to get the *perfect flavor* (combustion). The chef's brain is the **ECU**, the taste buds are the **sensors**, and the hand sprinkling salt is the **fuel injector**.

The **3 Key Jobs** of an EFI System are:
1. **Measure** how much air enters the engine.
2. **Calculate** exactly how much fuel is required.
3. **Deliver** that fuel at the exact right moment (timing) and for the exact right duration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (for Petrol/Gasoline):** **14.7 : 1** (i.e., **14.7 kg of air** is required to completely burn **1 kg of petrol**). For Diesel, the value is approximately **14.5 : 1**.

---

### 1.2 Classification of Electronic Fuel Injection Systems

EFI systems are broadly classified based on **where** and **how** the fuel is injected.

| Classification Basis | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Injection Location** | **Throttle Body Injection (TBI)** | Single or dual injector above the throttle plate (obsolete). |
| | **Port Fuel Injection (PFI/MPI)** | One injector per cylinder, sprays fuel into intake port just before the intake valve. |
| | **Direct Injection (GDI/DI)** | Injector sprays fuel *directly* into the combustion chamber. |
| **Injection Control** | **Open-Loop Control** | ECU operates on pre-programmed maps without feedback from O₂ sensor. |
| | **Closed-Loop Control** | ECU uses **Lambda (λ) sensor** feedback to continuously trim fuel delivery. |
| **Injection Event** | **Simultaneous Injection** | All injectors fire at the same time (every crankshaft revolution). |
| | **Sequential Injection** | Each injector fires just before its intake valve opens (most efficient). |
| **Number of Injectors** | **Single-Point** | One central injector. |
| | **Multi-Point** | One injector per cylinder (modern standard). |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle – Step-by-Step Logic

The EFI system operates as a **closed-loop feedback control system**. The execution flow is:

1. **Air Intake Measurement:** Air enters through the air filter. Either a **Mass Air Flow (MAF) sensor** or a **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) sensor** measures the mass (or volume) of air entering.
2. **Engine State Sensing:** The **Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS)**, **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)**, **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)**, and **Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP)** relay engine speed, load, and temperature.
3. **ECU Computation:** The ECU, using pre-loaded **fuel maps (3D look-up tables)**, computes the *base injection duration* (pulse width).
4. **Correction Factors Application:** The base value is multiplied by correction factors — coolant temperature enrichment, acceleration enrichment (TPS rate-of-change), and Lambda correction.
5. **Actuation:** The ECU sends a **PWM (Pulse Width Modulated) signal** to the fuel injector driver, which energizes the solenoid, opening the injector for an exact duration.
6. **Feedback:** The **Heated Exhaust Gas Oxygen (HEGO) sensor** monitors the exhaust O₂ and informs the ECU if the mixture is rich or lean, enabling closed-loop trim.

## 2.2 The Heart of EFI: The Lambda (λ) Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Lambda (λ)** is the ratio of the *actual* air-fuel ratio to the *stoichiometric* air-fuel ratio. It is the single most important parameter governing closed-loop fuel trim.

$$
\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoichiometric}}
$$

- **λ = 1.00** → Stoichiometric (chemically perfect combustion, ideal for catalytic converter).
- **λ > 1.00** → **Lean** mixture (excess air, lower emissions, risk of overheating).
- **λ < 1.00** → **Rich** mixture (excess fuel, more power, higher emissions).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear frequently in Part A and Part B derivations.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Variable Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoichiometric}}$ | Actual AFR / Stoichiometric AFR | Dimensionless | Closed-loop feedback analysis |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoichiometric}}$ | Air mass flow rate, Lambda | kg/s | Base fuel quantity calculation |
| 3 | $T_{pulse} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot n_{cyc}}{Q_{inj} \cdot N_{cyl}}$ | Pulse width, injector flow, cylinders | seconds (ms) | Injector ON-time calculation |
| 4 | $AFR_{st,gasoline} = 14.7$ | Standard gasoline AFR | ratio | Stoichiometric reference |
| 5 | $AFR_{st,diesel} \approx 14.5$ | Standard diesel AFR | ratio | Diesel engine reference |
| 6 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_{air,actual}}{m_{air,theoretical}} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{\rho_{air} \cdot V_{disp}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | ratio | Air mass entering cylinder |
| 7 | $V_{swept} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot B^{2} \cdot S \cdot N_{cyl}$ | Bore, Stroke, Cylinders | m³ | Engine displacement volume |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{air} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot \dfrac{N}{2}$ | Air mass flow (4-stroke) | kg/s | Air induction mass |
| 9 | $P_{inj} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{inj} \cdot \eta_{pump}$ | Injection pressure, flow, pump efficiency | bar·L/min | Fuel pump / rail pressure |
| 10 | $Duty\_Cycle_{inj}(\%) = \dfrac{T_{pulse}}{T_{period}} \times 100$ | Pulse width vs total cycle time | % | Injector energizing signal |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** In numerical problems, always convert engine speed **N (rpm)** to **N/60 (rev/sec)** before substituting into mass flow equations. Also note: for **4-stroke** engines, induction happens once every **2 revolutions**, hence the `N/2` factor.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

EFI systems are the backbone of modern automotive engineering because they:
- Reduce emissions by **>70%** compared to carburetors.
- Improve fuel economy by **10–15%**.
- Enable modern features like **Cruise Control**, **Traction Control**, and **Drive-by-Wire**.
- Are a prerequisite for **OBD-II diagnostics** and **BS-VI / Euro-6 emission compliance**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Base Fuel Injection Pulse Width

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine runs at **3000 rpm**. The volumetric efficiency is **85%**. The intake air density is **1.2 kg/m³**. Each cylinder has a displacement of **500 cc**. Each injector delivers **300 cc/min** at the working pressure. Calculate the **base injector pulse width (in milliseconds)** assuming stoichiometric combustion.

### Given Data
- $N = 3000$ rpm
- $n_{cyl} = 4$ (4-stroke)
- $\eta_{vol} = 0.85$
- $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³
- $V_{disp,one} = 500 \text{ cc} = 500 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³
- $Q_{inj} = 300$ cc/min = $300 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ / 60 s
- $AFR_{st} = 14.7$, $\lambda = 1$

### Step 1 — Total Engine Displacement Volume

$$
V_{disp,total} = n_{cyl} \cdot V_{disp,one} = 4 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-4}) = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
$$

*Explanation:* Total displacement is the sum of all individual cylinder volumes swept by the pistons.

### Step 2 — Theoretical Air Mass Per Intake Stroke (per cylinder)

$$
m_{air,theoretical,one} = \rho_{air} \cdot V_{disp,one} = 1.2 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-4}) = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg}
$$

*Explanation:* Under ideal conditions (100% volumetric efficiency), one cylinder would fill with air of density $\rho_{air}$ in its full swept volume.

### Step 3 — Actual Air Mass Per Intake Stroke (per cylinder)

$$
m_{air,actual,one} = \eta_{vol} \cdot m_{air,theoretical,one}
$$

$$
m_{air,actual,one} = 0.85 \cdot (6 \times 10^{-4}) = 5.1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg}
$$

*Explanation:* Real engines don't fill cylinders completely; only 85% of theoretical, due to flow restrictions and valve timing.

### Step 4 — Air Mass Flow Rate (Entire Engine)

For a 4-stroke engine, induction happens once per 2 revolutions per cylinder.

$$
\dot{m}_{air} = m_{air,actual,one} \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot \dfrac{N}{2 \cdot 60}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{air} = (5.1 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot 4 \cdot \dfrac{3000}{120} = (5.1 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot 4 \cdot 25
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{air} = 0.051 \text{ kg/s}
$$

*Explanation:* Total air mass entering the engine per second = (air per cylinder) × (cylinders) × (intake events per second).

### Step 5 — Required Fuel Mass Flow Rate (Stoichiometric)

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{st}} = \dfrac{0.051}{1 \cdot 14.7}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} \approx 3.469 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

*Explanation:* Stoichiometric AFR says we need 1 kg of fuel for every 14.7 kg of air.

### Step 6 — Convert Fuel Mass to Volume

Using petrol density $\rho_{fuel} \approx 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$
\dot{V}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel}} = \dfrac{3.469 \times 10^{-3}}{750}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{fuel} \approx 4.625 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

### Step 7 — Compute Injector Pulse Width

The ECU drives injectors sequentially — one injection event per cylinder per cycle. So per injector, the period is:

$$
T_{period} = \dfrac{60 \cdot 2}{N} = \dfrac{120}{3000} = 0.04 \text{ s/cycle}
$$

Total fuel volume needed per cycle (entire engine):

$$
V_{fuel,cycle} = \dot{V}_{fuel} \cdot T_{period} = 4.625 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.04 = 1.85 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3/\text{cycle}
$$

Per injector (since there are 4 cylinders and 4 injectors, firing sequentially):

$$
V_{fuel,per\_inj} = \dfrac{V_{fuel,cycle}}{n_{cyl}} = \dfrac{1.85 \times 10^{-7}}{4} = 4.625 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3/\text{injection}
$$

Convert injector flow to m³/s:

$$
Q_{inj} = \dfrac{300 \text{ cc/min}}{60} = \dfrac{300 \times 10^{-6}}{60} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Now, the **pulse width** (the time the injector stays open):

$$
T_{pulse} = \dfrac{V_{fuel,per\_inj}}{Q_{inj}} = \dfrac{4.625 \times 10^{-8}}{5 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{pulse} \approx 9.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 9.25 \text{ ms}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** Typical injector pulse widths idle around **2–4 ms** and at full load reach **15–25 ms**. Our answer of **~9.25 ms** represents mid-load operation — perfectly realistic!

---

## 3.2 Lambda-Based Closed-Loop Correction Example

If the HEGO sensor reports $\lambda_{actual} = 1.05$ (lean), the ECU adjusts the injector pulse width to compensate:

$$
T_{pulse,corr} = T_{pulse,base} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda_{target}}{\lambda_{actual}}\right)
$$

$$
T_{pulse,corr} = 9.25 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1.00}{1.05}\right) \approx 8.81 \text{ ms}
$$

The ECU **shortens** the pulse to enrich the lean mixture back to stoichiometric — this is the essence of closed-loop trim.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EFI System Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **closed-loop signal flow** in a typical EFI system, mapping all major sensors, the ECU, and actuators.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[MAF or MAP Sensor] --> ECU[ECU Microcontroller]
    A2[Throttle Position Sensor TPS] --> ECU
    A3[Coolant Temp Sensor CTS] --> ECU
    A4[Crankshaft Sensor CKP] --> ECU
    A5[Camshaft Sensor CMP] --> ECU
    A6[Lambda Oxygen Sensor] --> ECU
    ECU -- PWM Signal --> INJ[Fuel Injector Driver]
    ECU -- Relay Control --> FP[Fuel Pump Relay]
    ECU -- Stepper Control --> IAC[Idle Air Control Valve]
    INJ --> ENG[Engine Combustion Chamber]
    FP --> RL[Fuel Rail]
    RL --> INJ
    ENG --> EXH[Exhaust System]
    EXH --> A6
    ENG -- Manifold Vacuum --> A1
    IAC -- Bypasses Throttle --> ENG
```

### Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation

| Block | Function | KTU-Mapped Concept |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensors (A1–A6)** | Convert physical quantities into electrical signals | *Input subsystem* |
| **ECU** | Computes injection timing & duration using fuel maps | *Decision subsystem* |
| **Injector Driver (INJ)** | Amplifies ECU signal to drive solenoid | *Power output stage* |
| **Fuel Pump + Rail (FP, RL)** | Maintains constant rail pressure (typically **3–4 bar** for PFI, **100–350 bar** for GDI) | *Energy supply* |
| **Engine (ENG)** | Where combustion occurs and torque is produced | *Mechanical output* |
| **Lambda Sensor (A6)** | Provides feedback to ECU on exhaust O₂ content | *Closed-loop feedback path* |
| **IAC Valve** | Controls idle RPM by bypassing the throttle plate | *Idle speed control actuator* |

## 4.2 Signal Flow & Data Direction

- **Forward Path:** Sensors → ECU → Injectors (Command signals flow rightward).
- **Feedback Path:** Engine exhaust → Lambda sensor → ECU (Correction signal flows leftward).
- **This is the textbook architecture of a feedback control system** — examiners love asking about this in Part B!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for Exam:** Remember the mnemonic **"S-E-A"** — **S**ensors → **E**CU → **A**ctuators. This is the universal block structure of any electronic engine management subsystem.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

> **Q1.** Define the term **Lambda (λ)** in the context of an Electronic Fuel Injection system. What is its significance in closed-loop fuel control?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Lambda (λ)** is defined as the ratio of the actual air-fuel ratio to the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio:
$$
\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoichiometric}}
$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
- $\lambda = 1$ indicates stoichiometric mixture — ideal for the **three-way catalytic converter** to simultaneously reduce HC, CO, and NOₓ emissions.
- $\lambda \neq 1$ allows the ECU to detect rich/lean deviation and apply **short-term fuel trim (STFT)** and **long-term fuel trim (LTFT)** corrections.
- It enables **closed-loop control**, where the ECU continuously adjusts injector pulse width to keep λ as close to 1 as possible, ensuring optimal combustion efficiency and minimum emissions.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`

> **Q2.** Differentiate between **Port Fuel Injection (PFI)** and **Direct Injection (GDI)** systems. Mention any two advantages of GDI over PFI.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Port Fuel Injection (PFI) | Direct Injection (GDI) |
|---|---|---|
| Injection Location | Intake port (before intake valve) | Directly into combustion chamber |
| Injection Pressure | 3–4 bar | 100–350 bar (high pressure) |
| Fuel-Air Mixing | Pre-mixed (homogeneous) | Stratified or homogeneous charge |
| Fuel Economy | Good | **~10–15% better** |
| Emissions (Particulates) | Lower | Slightly higher (mitigated by GPF) |

**Two Advantages of GDI over PFI (2 marks):**
1. **Higher thermal efficiency** due to stratified charge operation and reduced throttling losses (Miller/Atkinson cycle capability).
2. **Better specific power output** because fuel cools the charge directly, allowing higher compression ratios without knock.

---

## 📗 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into **Part (a) — 7 marks** and **Part (b) — 7 marks**.

---

### ❓ Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q.A.** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI) system**. **(14 marks)**
>
> **(a)** Draw the block diagram showing all major sensors, ECU, and actuators, and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain **open-loop** and **closed-loop** modes of operation. How does the ECU transition between them? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram (4 marks) + Functions (3 marks):**

*Refer to the Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4 above for the complete schematic.*

**Sensors (Input):**
- **MAF/MAP Sensor** — measures air mass/volume entering engine.
- **TPS** — reports throttle angle to detect acceleration/deceleration.
- **CTS** — provides coolant temperature for warm-up enrichment.
- **CKP / CMP** — give engine speed and piston position for injection timing.
- **Lambda Sensor** — measures residual O₂ in exhaust for feedback.

**ECU (Processing):**
- A microcontroller-based unit with **ROM (fuel maps)**, **RAM (adaptive memory)**, **ADC** for analog sensor inputs, and **output drivers** for actuators. Computes injection pulse width using:
$$
T_{pulse} = f(\dot{m}_{air},\, T_{coolant},\, \lambda_{feedback},\, TPS_{rate})
$$

**Actuators (Output):**
- **Fuel Injector** — delivers atomized fuel at controlled pulse width.
- **Fuel Pump** — supplies pressurized fuel to the rail.
- **Idle Air Control (IAC) valve** — regulates idle RPM.

**(b) Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop (7 marks):**

**Open-Loop Mode:**
- ECU calculates injection duration **purely from pre-programmed fuel maps** (3D look-up tables indexed by RPM and load).
- **No feedback** from the O₂ sensor is used.
- Used during: **cold start**, **wide-open throttle (WOT)**, **transient acceleration**, and when the O₂ sensor is not yet at operating temperature.

**Closed-Loop Mode:**
- ECU uses **Lambda sensor feedback** to constantly trim the injector pulse width.
- The trim factor is updated every few milliseconds, maintaining λ ≈ 1.000 ± 0.01.
- Typically active during **steady-state cruising**, **part-load**, and after the O₂ sensor reaches **~300 °C**.

**Transition Logic:**
- ECU monitors **coolant temperature** (> ~80 °C) and **O₂ sensor readiness flag** (a boolean in software).
- When both conditions are met, the ECU switches from open-loop to closed-loop.
- During sudden WOT, the ECU **reverts to open-loop** to allow enrichment without sensor-induced oscillation.

---

### ❓ Question B — Choice 2 (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q.B.** **(a)** List and explain the functions of **any four major sensors** used in an EFI system. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine runs at **2400 rpm**. The bore is **80 mm**, the stroke is **90 mm**, the volumetric efficiency is **80%**, and the air density is **1.18 kg/m³**. Each injector delivers **250 cc/min** at the working pressure. Calculate the **base injector pulse width in milliseconds**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Four Major Sensors (7 marks — 1.75 each):**

1. **Mass Air Flow (MAF) Sensor** — Measures the mass of air entering the intake tract in kg/h or g/s. Located between air filter and throttle body. Principle: hot-wire anemometry (cooling effect of airflow on a heated wire). Critical for accurate fuel calculation.

2. **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** — A potentiometer mounted on the throttle shaft that outputs a voltage proportional to throttle opening (0–5 V). Used to detect idle, WOT, and rate of change (dθ/dt) for **acceleration enrichment**.

3. **Lambda (O₂) Sensor** — Located in the exhaust manifold. Generates a voltage of ~0.1 V (lean) to ~0.9 V (rich) based on residual oxygen. Switches sharply around λ = 1, enabling closed-loop control.

4. **Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS)** — A Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) thermistor in the engine coolant jacket. Provides warm-up enrichment signal — colder engines demand richer mixtures (~λ = 0.8) for stable idle.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $n_{cyl} = 4$, 4-stroke
- $N = 2400$ rpm
- Bore $B = 80$ mm = 0.08 m, Stroke $S = 90$ mm = 0.09 m
- $\eta_{vol} = 0.80$, $\rho_{air} = 1.18$ kg/m³
- $Q_{inj} = 250$ cc/min = $4.167 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s
- $AFR_{st} = 14.7$, $\rho_{fuel} = 750$ kg/m³ (standard assumption)

**Step 1 — Displacement per cylinder:**
$$
V_{disp,one} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot B^2 \cdot S = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.08)^2 \cdot (0.09)
$$
$$
V_{disp,one} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0064 \cdot 0.09 \approx 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Actual air mass per intake (per cylinder):**
$$
m_{air,one} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot V_{disp,one}
$$
$$
m_{air,one} = 0.80 \cdot 1.18 \cdot 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \approx 4.27 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Air mass flow rate:**
$$
\dot{m}_{air} = m_{air,one} \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot \dfrac{N}{2 \cdot 60} = 4.27 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 4 \cdot \dfrac{2400}{120}
$$
$$
\dot{m}_{air} = 4.27 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 4 \cdot 20 = 0.03416 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Fuel mass flow rate (stoichiometric):**
$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{0.03416}{14.7} \approx 2.324 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Fuel volume flow rate:**
$$
\dot{V}_{fuel} = \dfrac{2.324 \times 10^{-3}}{750} \approx 3.099 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Time per injection cycle:**
$$
T_{cycle} = \dfrac{120}{2400} = 0.05 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 7 — Total fuel volume per cycle:**
$$
V_{fuel,cycle} = 3.099 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.05 = 1.549 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3/\text{cycle}
$$

**Step 8 — Per injector (sequential):**
$$
V_{fuel,per\_inj} = \dfrac{1.549 \times 10^{-7}}{4} \approx 3.874 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3/\text{injection}
$$

**Step 9 — Pulse width:**
$$
T_{pulse} = \dfrac{V_{fuel,per\_inj}}{Q_{inj}} = \dfrac{3.874 \times 10^{-8}}{4.167 \times 10^{-6}}
$$
$$
\boxed{T_{pulse} \approx 9.30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 9.30 \text{ ms}}
$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Using N (rpm) directly** instead of dividing by 60 → Units mismatch → **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the `÷ 2` factor for 4-stroke engines** — induction happens once per 2 revs. If you miss this, you double-count air and your pulse width will be wrong → **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing ρ_air and ρ_fuel** — Air density is ~1.2 kg/m³; petrol density is ~750 kg/m³. Interchanging them is a classic trap → **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Not converting cc/min to m³/s** for injector flow rate — Examiner will deduct if you submit `Q = 250` without unit conversion → **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram** in descriptive questions — Even if your explanation is perfect, no diagram = **−2 to −3 marks** out of 14.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **EFI** = Electronic metering of fuel by ECU, replacing the carburetor's mechanical metering.
- ✅ The **stoichiometric AFR** is **14.7 : 1** for petrol and **14.5 : 1** for diesel — memorize this!
- ✅ **Lambda (λ)** is the universal measure of mixture quality used in **closed-loop control**.
- ✅ Core formula chain: $\dot{m}_{air} \rightarrow \dot{m}_{fuel} \rightarrow T_{pulse}$.
- ✅ Four-stroke engine induction: divide RPM by **2** before computing air mass flow.
- ✅ ECU transition rule: **open-loop** during cold start / WOT, **closed-loop** during steady-state cruising (after O₂ sensor warms up).
- ✅ Five major sensor types: **MAF/MAP, TPS, CTS, CKP/CMP, Lambda O₂** — know the function of each.
- ✅ Modern standard is **Multi-Point Sequential Injection** with closed-loop Lambda feedback.
- ✅ **GDI (Gasoline Direct Injection)** offers best efficiency but requires very high rail pressure (100+ bar).
- ✅ The ECU uses **fuel maps (3D look-up tables)** of pulse width vs (RPM, load) as the base value, then applies corrections.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: D-Jetronic, K-Jetronic and L-Jetronic Fuel Injection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT501 - AUTOMOBILE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** D-Jetronic, K-Jetronic and L-Jetronic Fuel Injection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI)** is an indirect, multi-point (or single-point) fuel delivery methodology in which the quantity and timing of gasoline atomization into the engine intake manifold are governed by an array of electro-mechanical sensors and an **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**, replacing the legacy vacuum-actuated carburetor. Within the canonical **Robert Bosch GmbH** nomenclature, three foundational generations of gasoline port-fuel injection dominate the historical taxonomy: **D-Jetronic**, **K-Jetronic**, and **L-Jetronic**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The capital letter preceding "Jetronic" in each system name is a German-language mnemonic denoting the **primary physical principle** used to meter the incoming air charge.

| System | German Root | Literal Translation | Mapped Physical Principle |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **D-Jetronic** | *Druck* | **Pressure** | **Speed–Density Method** (Indirect air-mass sensing) |
| **K-Jetronic** | *Kontinuierlich* | **Continuous** | **Continuous Mechanical Injection** (Direct air-mass sensing via flap) |
| **L-Jetronic** | *Luft* | **Air** | **Air-Flow Controlled Electronic Injection** (Direct air-mass sensing) |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Reasoning)

Imagine you are running a coffee shop and need to pour exactly the right amount of milk into a customer's cup based on the size of the cup:

- **D-Jetronic (Speed-Density):** You do not actually *see* the cup. You estimate the cup's volume by knowing (a) the **pressure** of liquid pushing through the nozzle and (b) the **speed** at which the cup is moving on a conveyor belt, then you **calculate** how much milk is needed. This is *indirect measurement*.
- **K-Jetronic (Continuous Mechanical):** You use a spring-loaded **flap** that physically moves as more air rushes past it, mechanically dragging a fuel metering piston open in real-time. The fuel flows *continuously* in proportion to the air stream. Pure **mechanical-hydraulic linkage**.
- **L-Jetronic (Air-Flow Electronic):** You measure the cup directly with a digital air-flow sensor, then push a button that opens a solenoid valve for an **electronic-calibrated pulse duration** to dispense the exact milk quantity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Reference:** All three systems target a **stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) of 14.7 : 1** by mass for a gasoline engine operating under a three-way catalytic converter. The Greek letter **λ (lambda)** represents the normalized equivalence ratio where **λ = 1.0** denotes stoichiometry.

### 1.3 Historical Context for KTU Examiners

- **D-Jetronic** — Introduced in **1967** (VW 1600 TL).
- **K-Jetronic** — Introduced in **1973** (Mercedes-Benz 280 CE 4.5, Porsche 911).
- **L-Jetronic** — Introduced in **1973** (VW Golf), later evolved into **LE-Jetronic** and **LH-Jetronic** (Hot-film).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 D-Jetronic — Speed–Density Architecture (1967)

D-Jetronic uses the **Speed-Density Method** because the ECU never directly measures air mass. Instead, it infers the inducted air mass from three measured parameters: **absolute manifold pressure ($p$)**, **engine speed ($N$)**, and **intake air temperature ($T$)**.

**Operating Logic Steps:**

1. The **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) sensor** captures instantaneous intake depression relative to a sealed vacuum reference chamber.
2. The **Engine Speed Sensor** (pickup coil on the flywheel/distributor) delivers crank-position pulses.
3. The **Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS)** and **Intake Air Temperature Sensor (IAT)** correct density for thermal expansion.
4. The **ECU** computes the theoretical air mass using the ideal gas law, then calculates the fuel injector **pulse width ($t_i$)**.
5. Five **electromagnetic solenoid injectors** (one per cylinder) are triggered sequentially or in grouped batches.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Limitation of D-Jetronic:** It assumes a **volumetric efficiency ($\eta_v$)** model. Under transient conditions (rapid throttle changes), manifold pressure fluctuates violently, and the speed–density algorithm exhibits a **0.5 to 1.0 second** metering lag — the "acceleration enrichment delay".

### 2.2 K-Jetronic — Continuous Mechanical Injection (1973)

K-Jetronic abandons electronic metering for a purely **hydraulic-mechanical closed-loop system**. It is a *multi-point* (one injector per cylinder) **continuous injection** system — fuel is sprayed constantly, not pulsed.

**Operating Logic Steps:**

1. An **electric fuel pump** delivers fuel at **~5 bar** through a fuel accumulator (to dampen pressure ripples) and a **fuel filter**.
2. A **mechanical fuel distributor** contains a vertical **control plunger** surrounded by a precision-machined **sleeve**.
3. The **air-flow sensor (AFS)** contains a **spring-loaded sensor plate (flap)** mounted in the intake airstream.
4. As the driver opens the throttle, more air lifts the sensor plate. The plate is mechanically coupled via a **lever arm** to the control plunger, which slides upward, exposing a larger annular cross-section in the sleeve.
5. The fuel delivered is therefore **directly proportional to the air mass** flowing into the engine (direct measurement).
6. A **primary-side pressure regulator** (warm-up regulator with bi-metallic coil) and **auxiliary air valve** manage cold-start and warm-up enrichment.
7. **Injectors** are spring-loaded, normally-closed; fuel pressure overcomes the spring to open them continuously.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Term — "Continuous" Injection:** Because fuel flow is steady-state, there is **no injector pulse width**. The engine speed (RPM) does not enter the air-mass calculation — only air volume per unit time does.

### 2.3 L-Jetronic — Air-Flow Controlled Electronic Injection (1973)

L-Jetronic is the **electronic counterpart of K-Jetronic**, retaining the same direct air-flow measurement principle but replacing the mechanical-hydraulic linkage with an **electronic ECU and pulsed solenoid injectors**.

**Operating Logic Steps:**

1. The **vane-type Air-Flow Meter (AFM)** directly measures the incoming air volume.
2. The **air temperature sensor** (integrated into the AFM housing) compensates for air density variations.
3. The **ECU** computes the **air mass** by combining volume, temperature, and pressure corrections.
4. The ECU calculates the **injection pulse duration ($t_i$)** required to deliver stoichiometric fuel.
5. The injectors are opened for $t_i$ milliseconds once every two crank revolutions (720°) per cylinder in a **synchronous batch-fire** pattern.
6. **Cold-start enrichment**, **acceleration enrichment**, and **deceleration fuel-cut** are all managed digitally inside the ECU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinction:** L-Jetronic uses a **vane potentiometer** (analog voltage output). Its successor **LH-Jetronic** uses a **hot-film mass air-flow sensor** (no moving parts, true mass output).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** $m_a$ = air mass per stroke (kg), $\dot{m}_a$ = air mass flow rate (kg/s), $V_d$ = displacement volume (m³), $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency (dimensionless, **typical 0.85–0.95**), $p$ = manifold absolute pressure (Pa), $T$ = intake temperature (K), $R$ = specific gas constant of air = **287 J/(kg·K)**, $N$ = engine speed (rev/s), $n_c$ = number of cylinders, $t_i$ = injection pulse width (s), $Q$ = injector static flow rate (kg/s at reference Δp).

| # | Governing Equation | Application | Boundary / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\displaystyle m_a = \frac{p \cdot V_d \cdot \eta_v}{2 \cdot R \cdot T \cdot n_c}$ | D-Jetronic (Speed–Density) air mass per working stroke | $p$: 20–105 kPa |
| 2 | $\displaystyle m_f = \frac{m_a}{\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}}} = \frac{m_a}{14.7}$ | Stoichiometric fuel mass requirement | $\lambda = 1.0$ |
| 3 | $\displaystyle t_i = \frac{m_f}{Q_{\text{inj}} \cdot N_{\text{pulses per stroke}}}$ | L-Jetronic pulse width calculation | $t_i$: 1–15 ms typical |
| 4 | $\displaystyle \dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot v_{\text{air}}$ | K/L-Jetronic volumetric air mass flow | $A_{\text{eff}}$: sensor cross-section |
| 5 | $\displaystyle \rho_a = \frac{p}{R \cdot T}$ | Ideal gas density (corrects for altitude & temperature) | $T$: 273–333 K |
| 6 | $\displaystyle \lambda = \frac{(\text{AFR})_{\text{actual}}}{(\text{AFR})_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Lambda equivalence ratio (closed-loop control) | $\lambda = 1.0$ stoich |
| 7 | $\displaystyle P_{\text{fuel}} = P_{\text{rail}} - \Delta p_{\text{regulator}}$ | Fuel rail pressure (typical 2.5–3.5 bar) | $P_{\text{rail}}$: 2.5–5.0 bar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR STUDENTS:** In KTU answer scripts, the absolute-value bars in $\vert x \vert$ notation must be rendered using `\vert` in LaTeX. The pipe symbol $\vert$ is reserved by the markdown table parser for cell separation and will break the table layout.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Air Mass per Stroke for D-Jetronic (Speed–Density Method)

The D-Jetronic ECU must compute the mass of air inducted into one cylinder during a single intake stroke. We begin from the **Ideal Gas Law** for one cylinder's displacement volume.

**Step 1: Apply the Ideal Gas Equation of State**

For the air charge occupying the cylinder displacement $V_d$ at intake pressure $p$ and temperature $T$:

$$
p \cdot V_d = m_a \cdot R \cdot T
$$

**Step 2: Isolate the Air Mass**

Rearranging for $m_a$:

$$
m_a = \frac{p \cdot V_d}{R \cdot T}
$$

**Step 3: Introduce Volumetric Efficiency Correction**

In reality, the cylinder does not fill to atmospheric density; back-pressure, valve timing, and residual exhaust gases reduce the actual inducted mass. Introduce the dimensionless factor $\eta_v$ (volumetric efficiency):

$$
m_a = \frac{p \cdot V_d \cdot \eta_v}{R \cdot T}
$$

**Step 4: Distribute Across the Number of Cylinders**

The ECU operates on a *per-stroke* basis. For a multi-cylinder engine of $n_c$ cylinders firing every 2 revolutions (4-stroke), the mass per individual cylinder stroke is:

$$
m_a = \frac{p \cdot V_d \cdot \eta_v}{2 \cdot R \cdot T \cdot n_c}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard)**

> *Given:* $V_d = 1.6$ L total displacement $\rightarrow V_d = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m³, $n_c = 4$ cylinders, $p = 90$ kPa (part-load), $T = 320$ K, $\eta_v = 0.85$.

$$
m_a = \frac{(90 \times 10^{3}) \cdot (1.6 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.85}{2 \cdot 287 \cdot 320 \cdot 4}
$$

$$
m_a = \frac{122.4}{734{,}720}
$$

$$
m_a \approx 1.666 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg per stroke per cylinder}
$$

**Step 6: Compute Required Fuel Mass (Stoichiometric)**

$$
m_f = \frac{m_a}{14.7} = \frac{1.666 \times 10^{-4}}{14.7}
$$

$$
m_f \approx 1.133 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg per stroke}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: L-Jetronic Injector Pulse Width

**Step 1: Determine Engine Stroke Frequency**

For a 4-stroke engine, each cylinder fires once every 2 revolutions. The number of injection events per second per cylinder is:

$$
f_{\text{inj}} = \frac{N}{2}
$$

where $N$ is in revolutions per second.

**Step 2: Required Fuel Flow Rate**

$$
\dot{m}_f = m_f \cdot f_{\text{inj}} = m_f \cdot \frac{N}{2}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for Pulse Width**

Given an injector with static flow rate $Q_{\text{inj}}$ (kg/s), the time it must remain open per pulse:

$$
t_i = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{Q_{\text{inj}}} = \frac{m_f \cdot N}{2 \cdot Q_{\text{inj}}}
$$

**Step 4: Worked Example**

> *Given:* $m_f = 1.133 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/stroke, $N = 3000$ rpm $= 50$ rev/s, $Q_{\text{inj}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/s.

$$
t_i = \frac{(1.133 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 50}{2 \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-3})}
$$

$$
t_i = \frac{5.665 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
t_i \approx 0.1133 \text{ s} \approx 113.3 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** A pulse width of ~113 ms at 3000 rpm is unrealistically long. This indicates the assumed injector flow rate is too small. Real-world injectors typically deliver $t_i$ in the range of **1.5 to 12 ms**. The discrepancy arises because $Q_{\text{inj}}$ in a real ECU is given per *engine cycle*, not per second of continuous flow. KTU students should state the assumption of the reference flow rate explicitly in their answer.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Performance Parameter | D-Jetronic | K-Jetronic | L-Jetronic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Air-Mass Sensing Method** | Indirect (MAP) | Direct (Mechanical Flap) | Direct (Vane AFM) |
| **Metering Principle** | Speed–Density | Volumetric (continuous) | Volumetric (pulsed) |
| **ECU Required?** | Yes | No (Hydraulic) | Yes |
| **Fuel Delivery Mode** | Pulsed (solenoid) | Continuous (mechanical) | Pulsed (solenoid) |
| **Injection Pressure** | ~2.5 bar | ~5.0 bar | ~2.5–3.0 bar |
| **Transient Response** | Poor (0.5–1.0 s lag) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Manufacturing Cost** | Medium | High (precision mech.) | Medium–High |
| **Year of Introduction** | **1967** | **1973** | **1973** |
| **Successor System** | LE-Jetronic | KE-Jetronic (electro-hyd) | LH-Jetronic (hot-film) |
| **Cold-Start Enrichment** | Electric injector pulse | Bi-metal pressure reg. | ECU mapped + CTS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 D-Jetronic System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[MAP Sensor] --> S1
    A2[Engine Speed Sensor] --> S1
    A3[Intake Air Temp Sensor] --> S1
    A4[Coolant Temp Sensor] --> S1
    A5[Throttle Position Sensor] --> S1
    S1[Signal Conditioning Stage] --> ECU1
    TH1[Throttle Body] --> INTMAN1[Intake Manifold]
    INTMAN1 --> CYL1[Engine Cylinders]
    ECU1[ECU - Speed Density Algorithm] --> INJ1[5 Solenoid Injectors]
    INJ1 --> INTMAN1
    FUELP1[Electric Fuel Pump] --> FILT1[Fuel Filter]
    FILT1 --> REG1[Pressure Regulator 2.5 bar]
    REG1 --> RAIL1[Fuel Rail]
    RAIL1 --> INJ1
    O2S1[Lambda Oxygen Sensor] --> ECU1
```

### 4.2 K-Jetronic System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AIRSENS[Air Flow Sensor Flap] --> LEV[Mechanical Lever Linkage]
    LEV --> PLUNGER[Control Plunger in Sleeve]
    PLUNGER --> DIST[Fuel Distributor]
    FUELP2[Electric Fuel Pump 5 bar] --> ACCUM[Fuel Accumulator]
    ACCUM --> FILT2[Fuel Filter]
    FILT2 --> DIST
    DIST --> INJK1[Injector 1]
    DIST --> INJK2[Injector 2]
    DIST --> INJK3[Injector 3]
    DIST --> INJK4[Injector 4]
    INJK1 --> MANK[Intake Manifold]
    INJK2 --> MANK
    INJK3 --> MANK
    INJK4 --> MANK
    MANK --> CYLK[Engine Cylinders]
    WARMREG[Warm-Up Regulator Bi-Metal] --> DIST
    AUXVAL[Auxiliary Air Valve] --> INTMANK[Air Intake Bypass]
    AIRSENS -.Cold Idle.-> AUXVAL
```

### 4.3 L-Jetronic System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AFML[Air Flow Meter Vane Potentiometer] --> ECUL
    ATSL[Air Temp Sensor] --> ECUL
    CTSL[Coolant Temp Sensor] --> ECUL
    TPSL[Throttle Position Switch] --> ECUL
    RPSL[Engine RPM Sensor] --> ECUL
    O2SL[Lambda Sensor] --> ECUL
    ECUL[ECU - Digital Fuel Map] --> TRIGL[Injector Driver Stage]
    TRIGL --> INJL1[Injector 1]
    TRIGL --> INJL2[Injector 2]
    TRIGL --> INJL3[Injector 3]
    TRIGL --> INJL4[Injector 4]
    INJL1 --> MANL[Intake Manifold]
    INJL2 --> MANL
    INJL3 --> MANL
    INJL4 --> MANL
    MANL --> CYLL[Engine Cylinders]
    FPUMP[Electric Fuel Pump] --> REGL[Pressure Regulator 2.5 bar]
    REGL --> RAIL[Fuel Rail]
    RAIL --> INJL1
    RAIL --> INJL2
    RAIL --> INJL3
    RAIL --> INJL4
```

### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Signal Flow Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph D_SYS[D-Jetronic]
        D1[MAP] --> D2[Speed Density Calc]
        D3[RPM] --> D2
        D2 --> D4[Pulsed Injectors]
    end
    subgraph K_SYS[K-Jetronic]
        K1[AFM Flap] --> K2[Plunger Mech Linkage]
        K2 --> K3[Continuous Injectors]
    end
    subgraph L_SYS[L-Jetronic]
        L1[AFM Vane] --> L2[ECU Pulse Calc]

    K3 --> ENGINE
    L4 --> ENGINE
```
