> The diagram above uses **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `A1`, `ECUL`, `TRIGL`, `D_SYS`). No reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node IDs. Node labels contain **plain uppercase text only**, with no bold/italic/HTML formatting tags inside the quoted label strings.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain the significance of the alphabets 'D', 'K' and 'L' used as prefixes in Bosch fuel injection system nomenclature. State the principle of air-mass measurement used in each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **D-Jetronic:** 'D' stands for the German word *"Druck"* meaning **pressure**. It uses the **Speed–Density method** — air mass is calculated indirectly from manifold absolute pressure, engine speed, and intake air temperature.
- **K-Jetronic:** 'K' stands for *"Kontinuierlich"* meaning **continuous**. It uses a **mechanical air-flow sensor (sensor plate/flap)** to directly measure incoming air volume, with fuel flowing continuously.
- **L-Jetronic:** 'L' stands for *"Luft"* meaning **air**. It uses an **electronic vane-type air-flow meter** to directly sense the air charge, with fuel delivery controlled via pulsed solenoid injectors.

*(1 mark each for the German meaning + principle)*

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Why is K-Jetronic called a "continuous injection" system even though the engine operates on a 4-stroke cycle?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
K-Jetronic is termed "continuous" because the **injectors are open at all times** while the engine is running — fuel is not pulsed on/off like in D or L-Jetronic. The mechanical fuel distributor meters a steady fuel stream proportional to the air mass entering the engine. The intake/exhaust strokes of the 4-stroke cycle are simply handled by the **engine's valve timing**, not by injector switching. The fuel not consumed on the intake stroke remains in the manifold intake port (wall-wetting) or recirculates.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style Question Set — Module 2 Internal Choice]**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose Either (a) or (b)**

**Q.A.(a) [7 marks]**  
*With a neat block diagram, explain the working of the D-Jetronic fuel injection system. Discuss its limitations.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**  
D-Jetronic, introduced by Bosch in 1967, is an **electronic multi-point port fuel injection (MPFI)** system. The "D" stands for *"Druck"* (pressure). It uses the **Speed–Density method** for air-mass calculation.

**2. Major Components (2 marks):**
- **MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) sensor** — measures intake manifold depression.
- **Engine Speed Sensor** — inductive pickup on flywheel teeth.
- **Intake Air Temperature (IAT) Sensor** — thermistor for density correction.
- **Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS)** — for warm-up enrichment.
- **Throttle Position Switch (TPS)** — idle and wide-open-throttle signals.
- **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** — analog/digital computing.
- **Five (or one per cylinder) solenoid injectors** — pulsed operation.
- **Electric fuel pump + pressure regulator (2.5 bar) + fuel rail.**

**3. Working Principle (2 marks):**  
The ECU samples MAP, RPM, IAT, and CTS signals at high frequency. Using the **ideal gas equation** $m_a = p V_d \eta_v / (2 R T n_c)$, the ECU infers the air mass per stroke. The corresponding **fuel mass** is computed by dividing by **14.7** (stoichiometric AFR). The ECU then energizes the injectors for a calculated **pulse width $t_i$** to deliver the precise fuel quantity.

**4. Limitations (2 marks):**
- **Transient lag** of 0.5–1.0 s under rapid throttle changes because manifold pressure fluctuates dynamically.
- Assumes constant $\eta_v$ — inaccurate at low RPM and high load.
- Requires altitude compensation (no direct air mass measurement).
- Higher ECU computational demand compared to K-Jetronic.

---

**Q.A.(b) [7 marks]**  
*Explain the L-Jetronic fuel injection system with a block diagram. Compare it with the D-Jetronic system.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**  
L-Jetronic ("L" for *"Luft"* = Air), introduced 1973, is an **electronic MPFI** system with **direct air-flow measurement** using a vane-type Air-Flow Meter (AFM).

**2. Major Components (1.5 marks):**  
Vane-type AFM, air temperature sensor, ECU, fuel pump, pressure regulator, fuel rail, solenoid injectors, lambda sensor (closed-loop), CTS, TPS.

**3. Working Principle (2 marks):**  
The **vane in the AFM** is deflected by incoming air. A **potentiometer** on the vane shaft outputs an analog voltage proportional to air volume. The ECU combines this with RPM, IAT, and CTS inputs to calculate **air mass** and **injection pulse width $t_i$**. The injectors fire once per 2 crank revolutions (720°) per cylinder.

**4. Comparison Table (2.5 marks):**

| Feature | D-Jetronic | L-Jetronic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Air-Mass Method | Indirect (Speed–Density) | Direct (Vane AFM) |
| Key Sensor | MAP | AFM |
| Transient Response | Poor | Excellent |
| Altitude Compensation | Poor | Automatic |
| ECU Algorithm | Complex | Simpler |
| Year | 1967 | 1973 |

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B.(a) [7 marks]**  
*With the help of a block diagram, describe the working of the K-Jetronic (CIS) fuel injection system. List its merits and demerits.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**  
K-Jetronic — *Kontinuierlich* (continuous) — Bosch's 1973 **Continuous Injection System (CIS)**. **Mechanical-hydraulic** MPFI; no ECU required for fuel metering.

**2. Major Components (1.5 marks):**  
Air-flow sensor (sensor plate), fuel distributor (control plunger + sleeve), electric fuel pump, fuel accumulator, fuel filter, primary pressure regulator, warm-up regulator (bi-metallic), auxiliary air valve, 4 (or more) spring-loaded injectors.

**3. Working Principle (3 marks):**  
Air flowing into the engine lifts the **spring-loaded sensor plate**. A **lever** mechanically couples plate movement to the **control plunger** in the fuel distributor. As the plunger rises, a larger annular gap opens, allowing more fuel to flow to the injectors. The injectors open at a fixed pressure (~5 bar) and **spray continuously** in proportion to the air mass. The **primary pressure regulator** maintains constant rail-to-plunger differential (~0.2 bar). The **warm-up regulator** increases this differential at low coolant temperatures to enrich the mixture. The **auxiliary air valve** bleeds extra air past the throttle for fast-idle during warm-up.

**4. Merits (0.75 mark):**
- Excellent transient response (direct mechanical linkage).
- No ECU needed; highly reliable.

**5. Demerits (0.75 mark):**
- Complex precision machining → high cost.
- No closed-loop lambda control in original version.
- Difficult to integrate with modern OBD-II diagnostics.

---

**Q.B.(b) [7 marks]**  
*A four-cylinder, 4-stroke gasoline engine has a displacement of 1.6 L and runs at 3000 rpm. At a part-load condition, the manifold absolute pressure is 80 kPa, intake temperature is 330 K, and volumetric efficiency is 0.88. Using the D-Jetronic speed-density method, calculate: (i) the air mass inducted per stroke per cylinder, and (ii) the required stoichiometric fuel mass per stroke. Assume $R = 287$ J/(kg·K) and AFR = 14.7.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V_d = 1.6$ L $= 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m³, $n_c = 4$, $p = 80 \times 10^3$ Pa, $T = 330$ K, $\eta_v = 0.88$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K), AFR = 14.7.

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Air mass per stroke per cylinder:

$$
m_a = \frac{p \cdot V_d \cdot \eta_v}{2 \cdot R \cdot T \cdot n_c}
$$

Substituting:

$$
m_a = \frac{(80 \times 10^{3}) \cdot (1.6 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.88}{2 \cdot 287 \cdot 330 \cdot 4}
$$

$$
m_a = \frac{112.64}{757{,}680} \approx 1.487 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/stroke/cylinder}
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Stoichiometric fuel mass per stroke:

$$
m_f = \frac{m_a}{14.7} = \frac{1.487 \times 10^{-4}}{14.7}
$$

$$
m_f \approx 1.011 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/stroke}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Hourly fuel consumption (bonus insight):

At 3000 rpm, each cylinder fires 1500 strokes/min, total 6000 strokes/min for 4 cylinders.

$$
\dot{m}_f = 1.011 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 6000 = 0.0607 \text{ kg/min} \approx 3.64 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Engine load summary:
- Indicated mean effective pressure (IMEP) approximation shows part-load at **~60%** of full-throttle.
- Result is dimensionally and physically consistent (4-stroke SI engine at cruise condition).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones — What KTU Examiners Flag:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "continuous" with "direct" injection:** K-Jetronic is *continuous* (fuel flows always) and *port* (injects into the intake port). It is **NOT** gasoline direct injection (GDI). Writing "K-Jetronic is direct injection" loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong German translation:** Mixing up *Druck* with *Dichte* (density) or *Luft* with *Leistung* will fail. Memorize: **D = Druck, K = Kontinuierlich, L = Luft**.
> 3. **D-Jetronic ECU confusion:** D-Jetronic uses an **analog ECU** with a frequency-voltage converter (F/V), not a digital microcontroller. Students writing "microprocessor" for D-Jetronic lose 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Speed-Density formula errors:** Forgetting the factor of **2** in the denominator (4-stroke cycle) is the most common arithmetic mistake — it halves or doubles the answer.
> 5. **Unit confusion in $R$:** Some texts use $R_{\text{air}} = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K). Confirm units before substituting to avoid $10^3$ errors.
> 6. **Missing stoichiometric ratio (14.7):** If a numerical problem asks for fuel mass but the student forgets to divide by 14.7, full credit is **NOT** awarded.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> - **D-Jetronic (1967):** *Druck* = Pressure. **Speed–Density** (indirect). MAP + RPM + IAT. Pulsed solenoid injectors. 0.5–1 s transient lag.
> - **K-Jetronic (1973):** *Kontinuierlich* = Continuous. **Mechanical-hydraulic CIS**. Sensor-plate + control plunger. ~5 bar rail. No ECU for metering. Bi-metallic warm-up regulator.
> - **L-Jetronic (1973):** *Luft* = Air. **Direct air-flow + electronic ECU**. Vane potentiometer AFM. Pulsed injectors. Successor: LH-Jetronic (hot-film).
> - **Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline** = **14.7 : 1** (mass). Lambda = 1.0 at stoich.
> - **Ideal gas air-mass equation:** $\displaystyle m_a = \frac{p V_d \eta_v}{2 R T n_c}$
> - **Injector pulse width:** $\displaystyle t_i = \frac{m_f N}{2 Q_{\text{inj}}}$
> - **Specific gas constant of air** $R = 287$ J/(kg·K).
> - **D-Jetronic → LE-Jetronic → LH-Jetronic** is the evolution chain (D → L → LH).
> - **K-Jetronic → KE-Jetronic** evolution (KE adds an electronic *electro-hydraulic actuator* on the control plunger for closed-loop lambda correction).
> - **K-Jetronic injectors are spring-loaded, normally-closed, continuously open** at rail pressure (~5 bar) — they do *not* pulse.
> - **L-Jetronic injectors are pulsed** synchronously once per 720° crank per cylinder.
> - **Closed-loop control** uses the **lambda (O₂) sensor** — added to D and L in later years; KE-Jetronic is the first K-system with closed loop.
> - **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v$ typically ranges **0.85–0.95** for naturally aspirated 4-stroke SI engines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Injection Schemes: Single Point, Multi Point, Sequential, Direct Injection, Common Rail Direct Injection, Gasoline Direct Injection, Supercritical Injection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT501 - AUTOMOBILE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Injection Schemes: Single Point, Multi Point, Sequential, Direct Injection, Common Rail Direct Injection, Gasoline Direct Injection, Supercritical Injection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Fuel Injection Schemes

A **fuel injection scheme** is a precisely controlled electromechanical fuel delivery architecture used in internal combustion engines (both Spark Ignition and Compression Ignition) in which the **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** meters and dispenses atomized fuel into the engine's induction or combustion system at electronically calculated timing intervals, replacing the older mechanical/carburetor-based fuel delivery methods.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme context**, fuel injection schemes are classified based on two principal parameters:

1. **Injection Location** — *Where* the fuel is delivered (throttle body / intake port / combustion chamber).
2. **Injection Timing Strategy** — *How* and *when* the injectors are fired (simultaneous / batched / sequential).
3. **Injection Pressure Regime** — *At what pressure* the fuel is atomized (low pressure: 1–5 bar, high pressure: 100–200 bar for GDI, ultra-high pressure: 1500–2500 bar for CRDI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must clearly differentiate between **low-pressure port injection** systems (SPI, MPI, SFI) and **high-pressure direct injection** systems (CRDI, GDI, Supercritical). The classification of GDI as a separate entity from CRDI is mandatory.

---

## 1.2 The Seven Injection Schemes — An Overview Table

| S.No. | Injection Scheme | Common Abbreviation | Typical Fuel | Injection Pressure | Location of Injector |
|:-----:|------------------|---------------------|--------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Single Point Injection | SPI / TBI / CFI | Gasoline | **1.0 – 2.5 bar** | Before throttle body |
| 2 | Multi-Point Injection | MPI / PFI | Gasoline | **2.5 – 4.0 bar** | One per cylinder, before intake valve |
| 3 | Sequential Fuel Injection | SFI / SEFI | Gasoline | **3.0 – 4.5 bar** | One per cylinder, before intake valve |
| 4 | Direct Injection | DI | Diesel (IDI/DI) | **200 – 1100 bar** | Directly into combustion chamber |
| 5 | Common Rail Direct Injection | CRDI | Diesel | **1500 – 2500 bar** | Directly into combustion chamber |
| 6 | Gasoline Direct Injection | GDI / D4 / DISI | Gasoline | **50 – 200 bar** | Directly into combustion chamber |
| 7 | Supercritical Injection | SCI | Diesel/Gasoline | **> 46 bar** (above critical pressure) | Combustion chamber, near-TDC |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Office Water Cooler System"**
>
> Imagine an office building with 4 floors (representing the 4 cylinders of an engine):
>
> 1. **Single Point Injection** = *One central water tank on the ground floor.* Everyone walks to a single tap to fill their bottle. The pressure is low, and during peak hours (WOT), bottlenecks occur.
>
> 2. **Multi-Point Injection** = *Each floor has its own tap, but all 4 taps are opened together (simultaneously).* Each floor gets water, but the timing isn't perfectly matched to "who is drinking right now."
>
> 3. **Sequential Injection** = *Each floor's tap is opened only when someone on that floor is actively drinking.* Perfect timing, no waste, no queue.
>
> 4. **Direct Injection** = *Water is sprayed directly into each person's mouth using a high-pressure jet, exactly when they open their mouth.* Minimal wastage, max precision.
>
> 5. **Common Rail Direct Injection** = *A single high-pressure pipeline (the "Common Rail") feeds all 4 high-pressure jets.* A central pump builds extreme pressure, and electronically actuated valves spray precisely.
>
> 6. **Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)** = *The same as CRDI, but using gasoline instead of diesel, with stratified charge air-fuel mixing.*
>
> 7. **Supercritical Injection** = *Water so hot and so pressurized that it no longer behaves as a liquid or gas — it becomes a "supercritical fluid."* This advanced state enables unique mixing patterns and is used in next-generation clean combustion engines.

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-know constants for the KTU Board Exam:**
> - **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Gasoline):** **A/F = 14.7 : 1**
> - **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Diesel):** **A/F = 14.5 : 1** (by mass)
> - **Density of Diesel:** **ρ ≈ 830 kg/m³** at 15°C
> - **Density of Gasoline:** **ρ ≈ 745 kg/m³** at 15°C
> - **Critical Pressure of Diesel:** **P_c ≈ 46 bar**
> - **Critical Temperature of Diesel:** **T_c ≈ 370°C**
> - **Critical Pressure of Gasoline:** **P_c ≈ 40 bar**
> - **Critical Temperature of Gasoline:** **T_c ≈ 300°C**
> - **Common Rail Pressure (Modern CRDI):** **2000 bar (typical peak)**

---

## 1.5 Module 2 Syllabus Context Map

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this topic fits in Module 2 of PCAUT501 (Automobile Electrical & Electronics):**
> - Module 2 typically covers **Engine Management Systems**, including fuel injection, ignition, and sensors.
> - The progression is: **Conventional Carburetor → Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI) → Various Injection Schemes → Sensors & Actuators → Engine Mapping**.
> - The **injection scheme is the "heart" of an Engine Management System (EMS)**, controlled by the ECU based on inputs from MAP, MAF, TPS, O2, and crank position sensors.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Scheme 1 — Single Point Injection (SPI / TBI)

### 2.1.1 Working Principle
A **single electromagnetic injector** (often called a *throttle body injector* or *central fuel injector*, CFI) is mounted above the throttle body. Fuel at **1.0–2.5 bar** is sprayed into the airstream upstream of the throttle plate. The air-fuel mixture then divides among all cylinders via the intake manifold.

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic Steps
- The ECU receives inputs: **Engine RPM, MAP, MAF, TPS, CTS, O2 sensor**.
- ECU calculates the required **fuel pulse width (τ)** in milliseconds.
- The single injector is energized once per **engine revolution (2-stroke firing) or twice per 720° (4-stroke)**.
- All cylinders receive the same air-fuel mixture through the intake manifold.

### 2.1.3 Advantages & Limitations
- **Advantages:** Simple, cheap retrofit to carburetor engines, fewer components.
- **Limitations:** Uneven cylinder-to-cylinder distribution, fuel puddle/wall wetting in manifold, poor atomization at low RPM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** SPI was Bosch's first EFI system, branded **"Mono-Jetronic"** (1971) and later **"Mono-Motronic"**.

---

## 2.2 Scheme 2 — Multi-Point Injection (MPI / PFI)

### 2.2.1 Working Principle
Each cylinder has its **own dedicated injector** mounted in the **intake port**, just before the intake valve. Fuel pressure is regulated to **2.5–4.0 bar**. The injectors are usually fired in **simultaneous mode** (all injectors fire together once per revolution) or in **batch mode** (two injectors fire together).

### 2.2.2 Firing Strategies in MPI
1. **Simultaneous Firing:** All injectors open together. Simpler ECU logic.
2. **Batched Firing:** Injectors paired (1-4 together, 2-3 together for a 4-cyl). Smooth power delivery.
3. *(Note: True sequential firing is classified as SFI, the next scheme.)*

### 2.2.3 Operational Logic
- The injector is typically a **solenoid-type pintle nozzle** with a calibrated flow rate.
- Pulse width τ is calculated as:
$$\tau = \frac{m_{fuel}}{Q_{inj} \cdot \rho_{fuel} \cdot N_{inj}}$$
where $Q_{inj}$ is injector static flow (cm³/min), $\rho_{fuel}$ is fuel density, $N_{inj}$ is the number of injections per cycle.

---

## 2.3 Scheme 3 — Sequential Fuel Injection (SFI / SEFI)

### 2.3.1 Working Principle
**SFI is a sub-category of MPI** in which each injector fires **independently and precisely timed** to the intake stroke of its corresponding cylinder. ECU uses the **camshaft position sensor (CMP)** in addition to the crank position sensor (CKP) to identify each cylinder's intake stroke.

### 2.3.2 Why SFI is Superior
- Each injector fires **once per 720°** (full 4-stroke cycle), not once per 360°.
- **Saves fuel** (less wall-wetting, no fuel pooling).
- **Lower HC emissions** during cold start and idle.
- **Better idle quality** and **improved catalytic converter efficiency**.

### 2.3.3 Cylinder Identification
The ECU uses a **camshaft position sensor** (often a Hall-effect sensor) to determine which cylinder is on its **intake stroke**. The firing order is typically stored in the ECU's **non-volatile memory (EEPROM/Flash)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** *Sequential injection* is the **default mode in 99% of modern gasoline cars (post-1995)**. The 360° → 720° firing change is a key exam point.

---

## 2.4 Scheme 4 — Direct Injection (DI) — Diesel

### 2.4.1 Working Principle
In a **Direct Injection diesel engine**, the injector is mounted **directly inside the combustion chamber** (or pre-chamber in IDI). The injector must overcome in-cylinder pressures of **30–200 bar** during compression stroke, hence **injection pressures of 200–1100 bar** are required.

### 2.4.2 Historical Development
- **1892 — Rudolf Diesel** invented the DI diesel engine.
- **Early systems** used jerk pumps (inline) and unit injectors.
- Modern DI diesel engines are now **overwhelmingly CRDI-based**.

### 2.4.3 Combustion Process
- Fuel is injected **near TDC (Top Dead Center)**.
- The high in-cylinder temperature (~600°C from compression) ignites the fuel spontaneously.
- **Injection timing** is critical: advance affects power and emissions; retard reduces NOx but increases PM.

---

## 2.5 Scheme 5 — Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI)

### 2.5.1 The Revolutionary Architecture
**Common Rail** refers to a **single high-pressure accumulator (rail)** that supplies fuel to all injectors at a **constant, electronically-controlled pressure** of **1500–2500 bar**, completely decoupling pressure generation from injection timing.

### 2.5.2 System Components
1. **Transfer Pump (Low-Pressure):** Draws fuel from tank, supplies to high-pressure pump.
2. **High-Pressure Pump (Radial Piston Type):** Generates 1500–2500 bar.
3. **Common Rail (Accumulator):** Stores high-pressure fuel; pressure controlled by **Pressure Control Valve (PCV)**.
4. **Solenoid/Piezo Injectors:** ECU-actuated, deliver precise fuel quantity.
5. **ECU:** Controls pressure, timing, and injection quantity.

### 2.5.3 Multi-Pulse Injection Capability
A unique feature of CRDI is **multiple injections per combustion cycle**:
- **Pilot Injection:** Small dose 10–20° BTDC to reduce combustion noise.
- **Main Injection:** Primary power-producing dose.
- **After Injection:** For DPF regeneration (diesel particulate filter burn-off).
- Total: up to **9 injections per cycle** in modern systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Note:** The decoupling of **pressure generation** from **injection timing** is the **single most important innovation** in CRDI compared to traditional jerk-pump systems. This is a frequently asked 7-mark question.

---

## 2.6 Scheme 6 — Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)

### 2.6.1 Working Principle
GDI injects gasoline **directly into the combustion chamber** at **50–200 bar**, eliminating the intake port. This enables two distinct operating modes:

1. **Homogeneous Mode (Low Load):** Fuel injected during intake stroke, fully mixed with air.
2. **Stratified Mode (Low Load, Idle):** Fuel injected late (near TDC), creating a **rich mixture near the spark plug** surrounded by lean air — drastically improving part-load efficiency.

### 2.6.2 Key Players
- **Bosch** (D-Jetronic heritage, then Motronic MED)
- **Mitsubishi** (GDI — first mass production in 1996)
- **Toyota** (D-4)
- **Audi/VW** (FSI, TSI)

### 2.6.3 Challenges
- **Particulate (PM) emissions** — must add GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter).
- **Injector carbon deposits** — require specialized fuel additives.
- **High-pressure pump noise** — needs acoustic dampening.

---

## 2.7 Scheme 7 — Supercritical Injection

### 2.7.1 What is "Supercritical"?
A fluid is **supercritical** when it is heated above its **critical temperature (T_c)** and pressurized above its **critical pressure (P_c)**. In this state, it has **no distinction between liquid and gas phases** — it has the density of a liquid but the viscosity and diffusivity of a gas.

For diesel: **T_c ≈ 370°C, P_c ≈ 46 bar**
For gasoline: **T_c ≈ 300°C, P_c ≈ 40 bar**

### 2.7.2 Why Supercritical Injection?
In advanced low-temperature combustion concepts (**HCCI, RCCI, PPC**), fuel must be injected into a **high-temperature, high-pressure environment** where conventional liquid atomization fails. Supercritical injection ensures:
- **Uniform mixing** without droplet formation.
- **No wall wetting** (no liquid impingement).
- **Controlled auto-ignition** for clean combustion.

### 2.7.3 Real-World Status
- **Research-stage** technology (as of 2024).
- Used in **HCCI (Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition)** prototypes.
- Studied for **marine engines** and **heavy-duty diesels** to meet future IMO/EPA regulations.

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory formulas for KTU Board Exam — memorize and understand the derivation of each.**

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-----:|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = \frac{V_d \cdot N \cdot \eta_v}{2 \cdot 60}$ | Volumetric air flow (4-stroke) | m³/s |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{air} = Q \cdot \rho_{air}$ | Mass air flow rate | kg/s |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{(A/F)_{stoich} \cdot \lambda}$ | Mass fuel flow rate | kg/s |
| 4 | $\tau = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot 60}{n_{cyl} \cdot q_{inj} \cdot \rho_{fuel}}$ | Injection pulse width | ms |
| 5 | $P_{rail} = P_{injector,min} + \Delta P_{line}$ | Common rail pressure | bar |
| 6 | $P_c^{diesel} \approx 46 \text{ bar}$ | Critical pressure of diesel | bar |
| 7 | $T_c^{diesel} \approx 370°C$ | Critical temperature of diesel | °C |
| 8 | $A/F_{stoich} = 14.7$ | Stoichiometric ratio (gasoline) | — |
| 9 | $TDC = \theta_{inj,start} + \theta_{duration}$ | Injection timing at TDC | degrees |
| 10 | $\eta_{vol} \approx 0.85 - 0.95$ | Volumetric efficiency (typical) | — |

Where:
- $V_d$ = displacement volume (m³)
- $N$ = RPM
- $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency
- $\lambda$ = equivalence ratio ($\lambda = 1$ stoichiometric)
- $q_{inj}$ = injector static flow (cm³/min)
- $\theta_{inj}$ = injection timing angle

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - **Emissions compliance:** Euro 6/BS-VII/BS-VI norms are only achievable with GDI or CRDI — not with port injection or carburetors.
> - **Fuel economy:** CRDI improves diesel BSFC by **15–25%** vs IDI diesel.
> - **Performance:** GDI enables **stratified-charge** operation, reducing pumping losses by up to **30%**.
> - **HCCI and PPC** (the next revolution) are *only possible* with supercritical injection.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Volumetric Air Flow Rate (4-Stroke Engine)

We start from fundamental gas dynamics for a 4-stroke, naturally aspirated engine.

**Given:**
- Displacement volume $V_d$ (m³)
- Engine speed $N$ (RPM)
- Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$ (dimensionless)

**Step 1:** In a 4-stroke engine, one complete power cycle requires **2 revolutions (720°)**. So the number of fresh-air charges per minute is:
$$n_{cycles} = \frac{N}{2}$$

**Step 2:** The theoretical volume of air drawn in per minute equals the displacement times the number of cycles:
$$V_{air,theoretical} = V_d \cdot \frac{N}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Accounting for volumetric efficiency, the **actual** air flow per minute is:
$$V_{air,actual} = V_d \cdot \frac{N}{2} \cdot \eta_v$$

**Step 4:** Converting to per-second basis (dividing by 60):
$$Q = \frac{V_d \cdot N \cdot \eta_v}{2 \cdot 60} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s)}$$

**Step 5:** Mass air flow using ideal gas approximation:
$$\dot{m}_{air} = Q \cdot \rho_{air} = \frac{V_d \cdot N \cdot \eta_v \cdot \rho_{air}}{120}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass Fuel Flow Rate

**Step 1:** At stoichiometric conditions, by definition of Air-Fuel ratio:
$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = 14.7 \quad (\text{gasoline})$$

**Step 2:** For an equivalence ratio $\lambda$ (lambda sensor's reference):
$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}/\dot{m}_{fuel}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$$

**Step 3:** Rearranging to solve for $\dot{m}_{fuel}$:
$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{(A/F)_{stoich} \cdot \lambda}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $\dot{m}_{air}$ from Section 3.1:
$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{V_d \cdot N \cdot \eta_v \cdot \rho_{air}}{120 \cdot 14.7 \cdot \lambda}$$

**Step 5:** For a 4-cylinder, 1.5L engine at 3000 RPM, $\eta_v = 0.9$, $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\lambda = 1$:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3000 \cdot 0.9 \cdot 1.2}{120}$$

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{4.86}{120} = 0.0405 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{0.0405}{14.7} = 2.755 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} = 9.91 \text{ kg/hr}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Injection Pulse Width Calculation

**Given:** For a 4-cylinder SFI engine, total fuel required per cycle = $\dot{m}_{fuel} \times t_{cycle}$, where $t_{cycle} = 120/N$ seconds.

**Step 1:** Fuel mass per injection event (per cylinder per cycle):
$$m_{inj} = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot 120}{N \cdot n_{cyl}}$$

**Step 2:** Injector static flow rate $q_{inj}$ is typically given in **cm³/min**. Convert fuel mass to volume:
$$V_{fuel,inj} = \frac{m_{inj}}{\rho_{fuel}}$$

**Step 3:** Pulse width is the time the injector solenoid is energized:
$$\tau = \frac{V_{fuel,inj}}{q_{inj}/60} = \frac{60 \cdot m_{inj}}{q_{inj} \cdot \rho_{fuel}}$$

**Step 4:** Combining:
$$\tau = \frac{60 \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot 120}{N \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot q_{inj} \cdot \rho_{fuel}}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{7200 \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel}}{N \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot q_{inj} \cdot \rho_{fuel}}}$$

**Step 5:** For our previous example: $q_{inj} = 200$ cm³/min, $\rho_{fuel} = 745$ kg/m³, $n_{cyl} = 4$, $N = 3000$ RPM:

$$\tau = \frac{7200 \cdot 2.755 \times 10^{-3}}{3000 \cdot 4 \cdot 200 \cdot 745 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\tau = \frac{19.836}{1788} = 11.09 \text{ ms}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Common Rail Pressure Energy Balance

**Step 1:** The high-pressure pump must deliver energy to compress fuel from transfer pressure ($P_{trans} \approx 5$ bar) to rail pressure ($P_{rail} \approx 2000$ bar).

**Step 2:** Specific work of compression (assuming isentropic):
$$w_{comp} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot \frac{R \cdot T}{\rho} \left[ \left(\frac{P_{rail}}{P_{trans}}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 3:** For diesel: $\gamma \approx 1.4$, $R \approx 287$ J/(kg·K) for the carrier fluid properties, $T = 320$ K, $\rho = 830$ kg/m³:

$$\left(\frac{2000}{5}\right)^{0.4/1.4} = 400^{0.2857} \approx 5.45$$

**Step 4:** Total specific work:
$$w_{comp} = 3.5 \cdot \frac{287 \cdot 320}{830} \cdot (5.45 - 1) = 3.5 \cdot 110.6 \cdot 4.45$$

$$w_{comp} \approx 1723 \text{ J/kg}$$

**Step 5:** For an engine consuming 10 kg/hr of fuel, pump power required:
$$P_{pump} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot w_{comp} = \frac{10}{3600} \cdot 1723 = 4.79 \text{ W (minimum theoretical)}$$

Actual pump power is 5–10× higher due to mechanical and volumetric losses.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Seven Schemes

| Parameter | SPI | MPI | SFI | DI (Diesel) | CRDI | GDI | Supercritical |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|-------------|------|-----|---------------|
| **Fuel Type** | Gasoline | Gasoline | Gasoline | Diesel | Diesel | Gasoline | Both |
| **Pressure (bar)** | 1.0–2.5 | 2.5–4.0 | 3.0–4.5 | 200–1100 | 1500–2500 | 50–200 | > 46 (above P_c) |
| **Injector Count (4-cyl)** | 1 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 4 |
| **Firing Strategy** | All cyls at once | Sim/Batch | One per 720° | Mechanical | Multi-pulse | Hom/Strat | Continuous/Timed |
| **Cold Start HC** | High | Medium | Low | Very Low | Very Low | Low | Very Low |
| **BSFC Penalty** | +10% | +5% | +3% | Baseline | −15% | −20% | Theoretical −25% |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Medium | Medium-High | High | Highest | Very High | R&D only |
| **Particulate Filter** | Not needed | Not needed | Not needed | DPF required | DPF required | GPF required | Conceptual |
| **Technology Status** | Legacy | Legacy | **Current (Std)** | Replaced by CRDI | **Current (Diesel Std)** | **Current (Premium)** | Research |
| **Cylinder Distribution** | Poor | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Wall Wetting** | Severe | Moderate | Minimal | None | None | None | None |
| **Engine Architecture** | TBI | PFI | PFI | IDI/DI | DI | DI | DI |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** *For 14-mark questions, this matrix structure (with 5–6 key parameters) is a guaranteed high-scoring answer format. Memorize the pressure ranges and firing strategies.*

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5: Supercritical State Verification

**Step 1:** For a fluid to be supercritical:
$$T > T_c \quad \text{AND} \quad P > P_c$$

**Step 2:** For diesel: $T_c = 370°C$, $P_c = 46$ bar.

**Step 3:** In an HCCI engine at TDC, in-cylinder conditions are:
- $T_{TDC} \approx 800°C$ (after compression, ratio 18:1)
- $P_{TDC} \approx 60$ bar

**Step 4:** Verification:
$$T_{TDC} = 800°C > 370°C = T_c \quad \checkmark$$
$$P_{TDC} = 60 \text{ bar} > 46 \text{ bar} = P_c \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, diesel fuel injected at TDC in HCCI conditions exists in the **supercritical state**. There is **no phase boundary** — no atomization in the classical sense. Mixing is governed by **diffusion**, not droplet breakup.
