## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. Differentiate between Single Point Injection (SPI) and Multi-Point Injection (MPI). Mention the typical injection pressure of each.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Single Point Injection (SPI) | Multi-Point Injection (MPI) |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Number of Injectors | 1 (central) | 1 per cylinder (4 for 4-cyl) |
| Location | Above throttle body | In intake port, before intake valve |
| Injection Pressure | 1.0 – 2.5 bar | 2.5 – 4.0 bar |
| Cylinder-to-Cylinder Distribution | Poor (manifold-dependent) | Excellent (direct port injection) |
| Cold Start HC Emissions | High | Moderate |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Wall Wetting | Severe | Moderate |
| Brand Example | Bosch Mono-Jetronic | Bosch L-Jetronic / LH-Jetronic |

> [!NOTE]
> *For 3 marks, mention at least 3 differentiating parameters with values.*

---

### **Q2. What is Sequential Fuel Injection (SFI)? Why is it preferred over simultaneous MPI?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Sequential Fuel Injection (SFI)** is a variant of MPI in which each injector is fired **independently and precisely timed** to the intake stroke of its corresponding cylinder, using **camshaft position sensor (CMP)** data in addition to the crankshaft position sensor.

**Why SFI is preferred over simultaneous MPI:**

1. **Fuel Economy:** In simultaneous MPI, fuel is injected every 360°, causing wall wetting in the intake port. SFI injects once per 720°, only on the intake stroke → **3–5% fuel savings**.
2. **Lower HC Emissions:** Reduced wall wetting means less fuel wash-down into the oil and less cold-start HC.
3. **Better Idle Quality:** Each cylinder receives fuel at the right time → smoother idle.
4. **Improved Catalyst Efficiency:** More precise A/F ratio control → better three-way catalyst operation.
5. **Faster Cold Start Response:** Fuel is delivered only when needed, not wasted in the manifold.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI)** system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the high-pressure pump, common rail, pressure control valve, and injectors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **CRDI** with **Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)** in terms of injection pressure, fuel type, atomization, combustion mode, particulate emissions, and cost. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### Part (a): CRDI Detailed Explanation (7 marks)

**Definition:**
The **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI)** system is a diesel fuel injection architecture in which a **single high-pressure accumulator (the "common rail")** supplies fuel at a constant, electronically-controlled pressure (typically **1500–2500 bar**) to all electronically-actuated injectors, completely decoupling pressure generation from injection timing and quantity.

**Major Components:**

1. **Fuel Tank & Transfer (Lift) Pump:**
   - Draws diesel from the tank.
   - Provides low-pressure supply (~5 bar) to the high-pressure pump.
   - Includes a **fuel filter** and **water separator**.

2. **High-Pressure Pump (HPP):**
   - Typically a **3-piston radial pump** driven by the camshaft.
   - Generates 1500–2500 bar.
   - **Pressure is controlled by the ECU** through the Pressure Control Valve (PCV), not by spring setting.

3. **Common Rail (Accumulator):**
   - A high-pressure reservoir (volume ~25 cm³) that **smooths pressure pulsations**.
   - Maintains nearly constant pressure across all injection events.
   - Has a **rail pressure sensor** for closed-loop feedback.

4. **Pressure Control Valve (PCV):**
   - Mounted on the rail or pump.
   - Modulates fuel return to control rail pressure.
   - **Closed-loop** with the rail pressure sensor → ±20 bar accuracy.

5. **Solenoid / Piezo Injectors:**
   - **Solenoid type:** 7-hole nozzle, response time ~0.3 ms.
   - **Piezo type:** Faster (~0.1 ms), allows more precise multi-pulse.
   - **Nozzle opening pressure (NOP):** 200–350 bar (in addition to rail pressure).

6. **ECU (Engine Control Unit):**
   - Controls injection timing, quantity, pressure, and number of pulses.
   - Inputs: CKP, CMP, MAP, MAF, coolant temp, fuel temp, rail pressure.

**Working Principle:**

The ECU calculates required fuel quantity based on accelerator pedal position and engine speed. The high-pressure pump maintains rail pressure at the target value (e.g., 2000 bar at full load, 400 bar at idle). At the precise crank angle (e.g., 6° BTDC for main injection), the ECU energizes the injector solenoid, opening the nozzle. Fuel is sprayed directly into the combustion chamber at ~2000 bar, ensuring ultra-fine atomization.

**Multi-Pulse Injection Strategy (a key advantage):**

- **Pilot Injection:** 1–3 mg of fuel injected 20–30° BTDC to initiate gentle combustion and reduce diesel knock.
- **Main Injection:** Primary 4–15 mg dose at 5–10° BTDC for power generation.
- **After Injection:** 1–2 mg post-main to increase exhaust temperature for **DPF regeneration**.

---

#### Part (b): CRDI vs GDI Comparison (7 marks)

| Parameter | CRDI | GDI |
|-----------|------|-----|
| **Fuel Type** | Diesel | Gasoline |
| **Injection Pressure** | 1500 – 2500 bar | 50 – 200 bar |
| **Atomization** | Ultra-fine (multi-pulse) | Fine (single/dual pulse) |
| **Combustion Mode** | Compression Ignition (CI) | Spark Ignition (SI) — Homogeneous / Stratified |
| **Compression Ratio** | 16:1 – 22:1 | 9:1 – 12:1 (lower, to prevent knock) |
| **Particulate Emissions** | High → DPF mandatory | Moderate → GPF required for Euro 6 |
| **NOx Emissions** | High → SCR / EGR mandatory | Moderate → Three-way catalyst |
| **Cold Start HC** | Very Low | Moderate (rich mixture required) |
| **Fuel Economy Gain vs PFI** | +20% (vs IDI) | +15% (vs port injection) |
| **Cost** | Very High (₹1.5–3 lakh for system) | High (₹0.8–1.5 lakh for system) |
| **Injector Carbon Deposit Issues** | None (diesel is lubricating) | Severe — requires additive or frequent cleaning |
| **Example Vehicle** | Maruti Swift DDIS, Toyota Innova Crysta | Toyota Camry Hybrid, Audi A4 TFSI |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> - **Common mistake:** Students often confuse **IDI (Indirect Injection)** with **CRDI**. IDI uses a pre-chamber and lower pressure (~200 bar); CRDI is direct injection at 2000 bar. Be precise.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Many students write "GDI uses compression ignition" — this is **WRONG**. GDI still uses **spark ignition**, just with direct injection of fuel.
> - **Common mistake #3:** In Part (b), students forget to mention the **particulate filter (DPF for diesel, GPF for GDI)**. This is a 1-mark loss.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]:**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What is **Supercritical Injection**? Explain the conditions for a fluid to reach the supercritical state, and discuss its relevance to **HCCI (Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition)** engines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable diagrams, explain the **Single Point Injection (SPI)** and **Sequential Fuel Injection (SFI)** systems. Compare them in terms of fuel distribution, emissions, and cold-start performance. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### Part (a): Supercritical Injection (7 marks)

**Definition:**
**Supercritical injection** is the process of injecting fuel into a combustion chamber where the fuel's **pressure and temperature exceed its critical point**, causing it to exist as a **single-phase supercritical fluid** — exhibiting the density of a liquid but the diffusivity and viscosity of a gas.

**Critical Point Conditions for Common Fuels:**

| Fuel | Critical Pressure (P_c) | Critical Temperature (T_c) |
|------|-------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Gasoline | ~40 bar | ~300°C |
| Diesel | ~46 bar | ~370°C |
| n-Heptane | 27.3 bar | 267°C |
| Methanol | 81 bar | 240°C |

**Why Supercritical State Occurs in HCCI:**

In an HCCI engine, the in-cylinder conditions at TDC (Top Dead Center) during compression are:
- **Temperature:** 700–900°C
- **Pressure:** 50–100 bar

Since $T_{TDC} > T_c$ and $P_{TDC} > P_c$ for diesel/gasoline, fuel injected near TDC **instantly transitions to the supercritical state** upon entering the chamber.

**Relevance to HCCI Engines:**

1. **No Atomization Required:** Classical droplet breakup is absent. Mixing is **diffusion-controlled**, leading to ultra-homogeneous charge.
2. **No Wall Wetting:** No liquid impingement on chamber walls — eliminates crevice HC emissions.
3. **Controlled Auto-Ignition:** The homogeneous, supercritical mixture enables **predictable, low-temperature combustion**, reducing NOx and soot simultaneously.
4. **Wide Load Range:** HCCI with supercritical injection can operate from idle to medium loads with diesel-like efficiency but gasoline-like emissions.
5. **Future Marine & Heavy-Duty Applications:** Being researched for IMO Tier III and EPA 2027 compliance.

**Challenges:**
- **Difficult injection timing control** due to rapid mixing.
- **No commercial production** as of 2024.
- **Limited operating range** (hard to extend to high loads).

---

#### Part (b): SPI vs SFI Comparison (7 marks)

**Single Point Injection (SPI) — Diagram & Explanation:**

In SPI, a **single injector** is mounted directly above the throttle body. Fuel is sprayed into the air stream upstream of the throttle plate at 1.0–2.5 bar. The mixture is then distributed to all cylinders via the intake manifold.

```
[Fuel Pump] → [Pressure Regulator 1.5 bar] → [SINGLE INJECTOR] → [Throttle Body] → [Manifold] → [Cyl 1, 2, 3, 4]
```

**Sequential Fuel Injection (SFI) — Diagram & Explanation:**

In SFI, **four separate injectors** are mounted in the intake ports, one per cylinder, each at 3.0–4.5 bar. The ECU fires each injector **individually**, precisely on the intake stroke of its cylinder, using CKP + CMP sensor data.

```
[Fuel Pump] → [Rail 4 bar] → [INJ 1] → [Cyl 1 Intake Port]
                              [INJ 2] → [Cyl 2 Intake Port]
                              [INJ 3] → [Cyl 3 Intake Port]
                              [INJ 4] → [Cyl 4 Intake Port]
                  (Each INJ fired separately on its intake stroke)
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | SPI | SFI |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Cylinder-to-Cylinder Fuel Distribution** | Poor (5–10% variation) | Excellent (<1% variation) |
| **Wall Wetting** | Severe (manifold pooling) | Minimal (port injection, timed) |
| **Cold Start HC Emissions** | High (3–5× higher) | Low (close to stoichiometric from start) |
| **Idle Quality** | Rough (uneven distribution) | Very Smooth |
| **Cost** | 1/3 of SFI | 3× of SPI |
| **Maintenance** | Easy | Requires ECU + sensors |
| **Modern Use** | Discontinued (post-2000) | Standard in 99% of gasoline cars (post-1995) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> - **Common mistake:** Students write "SPI has 1 injector, SFI has 4 injectors" without explaining the **timing difference**. The examiner wants to see the explanation of *firing strategy* (all together vs. individually timed).
> - **Common mistake #2:** For the supercritical part, students forget to state the **specific numerical values** of $P_c$ and $T_c$. Always include these.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Many students confuse **HCCI** with **GDI** — they are **different** technologies. HCCI is compression-ignition; GDI is spark-ignition. Both can use direct injection, but combustion modes are entirely different.

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Examination Strategy Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **How to score 14/14 in this topic:**
> 1. **Always draw a labeled block diagram** in any 7-mark question (minimum 5 components labeled).
> 2. **Use bullet points, not paragraphs** — the examiner appreciates clarity.
> 3. **Include the year/era of technology** (e.g., "CRDI was introduced by Bosch in 1997 for passenger cars") — this shows depth.
> 4. **End with a comparison table** — guaranteed 1–2 bonus marks.
> 5. **Mention environmental/emissions aspect** — KTU 2024 scheme is outcome-based and emphasizes **BS-VI/Euro 6 compliance**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this on your wall!):**

- ✅ **SPI** = 1 injector, 1.5 bar, throttle body, **OBSOLETE** in modern cars.
- ✅ **MPI** = 1 injector per cylinder, 3.5 bar, simultaneous or batch firing.
- ✅ **SFI** = Sub-type of MPI, each injector timed to its cylinder's intake stroke using **CMP sensor**.
- ✅ **DI Diesel** = Injector in combustion chamber, 200–1100 bar, jerk-pump or unit-injector.
- ✅ **CRDI** = High-pressure common rail at **2000 bar**, decouples pressure from timing, **multi-pulse injection** (pilot + main + after).
- ✅ **GDI** = Gasoline direct injection at 100 bar, enables **stratified charge**, requires **GPF**, prone to **injector carbon deposits**.
- ✅ **Supercritical** = Fuel above $P_c$ and $T_c$, used in **HCCI research**, **no atomization**, diffusion-controlled mixing.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric A/F** = **14.7 : 1** for gasoline; **14.5 : 1** for diesel.
- ✅ **Critical Pressure of Diesel** = **46 bar**; **Critical Temperature** = **370°C**.
- ✅ **Multi-pulse injection** = Pilot + Main + After; reduces noise, NOx, and enables DPF regen.
- ✅ **SFI saves ~3–5% fuel** vs simultaneous MPI.
- ✅ **CRDI is the standard for all modern BS-VI diesel cars** (post-2020 in India).
- ✅ **GDI + GPF** = the current trend in premium petrol cars (Euro 6 / BS-VI compliant).
- ✅ **HCCI + Supercritical Injection** = the **future of combustion** (post-2030 projection).
- ✅ **Remember the firing order "1-3-4-2"** for a typical 4-cylinder SFI engine.
- ✅ **Always state numerical pressure values** in the exam — generic statements lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Headlights, Headlight Reflectors, Headlight Lenses, Indicator Lamp Details, Lighting Circuit, Projector Headlights
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT501 - AUTOMOBILE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Headlights, Headlight Reflectors, Headlight Lenses, Indicator Lamp Details, Lighting Circuit, Projector Headlights

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Automotive Headlight Systems

An automotive **headlight** is a luminaire fitted to the front of a road vehicle designed to illuminate the road ahead during low-visibility conditions (night, fog, heavy rain) and to make the vehicle conspicuously visible to oncoming traffic. In modern vehicles, headlamp units are integrated assemblies containing the **light source (bulb)**, **reflector**, **lens**, **housing**, **aiming mechanism**, and **connector/wiring harness**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** This topic covers the construction, working, and classification of headlight units, the optical role played by reflectors and lenses, signaling (indicator) lamp specifications, the complete lighting circuit with switching and protection, and the modern projector-type headlamp assembly.

### 1.2 Classification of Headlights (Types of Headlights)

Automotive headlights have evolved through several generations, distinguished primarily by the **light-producing technology**, the **photometric output**, and the **beam control mechanism**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Headlamp:** A headlamp is a sealed or serviceable optical assembly that produces a legally specified dipped-beam (passing-beam) and main-beam (driving-beam) pattern, conforming to ECE Regulation No. 48 / AIS-008 (India) photometry limits.

#### (a) Sealed Beam Headlights (Conventional/Vacuum)
- Introduced in 1939 (USA) and adopted in India through the 1980s–1990s.
- The tungsten filament, reflector, and front lens are **fused into a single sealed gas-filled unit** (typically argon at ~1 atm).
- Bulb cannot be replaced – the entire unit must be discarded when the filament burns out.
- Common types: **Type 1 (2 filaments)**, **Type 2 (single filament)**, **Round (7-inch)** and **Rectangular (5.75 inch)**.

#### (b) Halogen Headlights (HSV – Halogen Sealed Vision)
- A small replaceable quartz-glass bulb contains a tungsten filament and a **halogen gas** (iodine or bromine at 5–10 bar pressure).
- The **Halogen Regenerative Cycle** redeposits evaporated tungsten back on the filament, allowing filament temperatures of **~2,900 K** (vs. 2,500 K for vacuum lamps) and producing **~1,500 lumens** from a single H4 bulb.
- Common bulb designations: **H1, H3, H4, H7, H11, H13, H15, HIR2 (9011)**.
- Operating voltage: **12 V DC** (some commercial vehicles use **24 V DC**).

#### (c) HID / Xenon Headlights (High Intensity Discharge)
- The bulb (D – Discharge) contains **xenon gas** and **mercury and metal halide salts**. No filament.
- Light is generated by an **electric arc** between two electrodes at ~25 kV ignition, stabilized at ~85 V / 0.4 A.
- Requires a **ballast** (DC-DC inverter) and igniter.
- Color temperature: **4,000 K – 6,000 K** (whiter, daylight-like).
- Luminous efficacy: **90–120 lm/W** (almost double halogen).
- Bulb types: **D1S, D1R, D2S, D2R, D3S, D4S** (S = for projector lens, R = for reflector).

#### (d) LED Headlights (Light Emitting Diode)
- Uses **high-power white LEDs** (luminous flux per LED ~ 1,000–3,000 lm).
- Color temperature: **5,000 K – 6,500 K**.
- Service life: **> 10,000 hours** (vs. 500 hrs for halogen, 2,500 hrs for HID).
- Requires **LED driver / heat sink / thermal management**.
- Enables complex shapes and **Matrix LED** pixel-level beam control.

#### (e) Laser Headlights
- Laser diodes (blue, 450 nm) excite a yellow phosphor to generate white light.
- Used in high-end vehicles (BMW i8, Audi R8). Range: **~600 m** (almost 2× LED high beam).
- Highly compact light source (~ 30 µm²).

#### (f) Adaptive / Matrix Headlights
- A camera detects oncoming traffic; an ECU controls individual LEDs to **shadow** approaching vehicles while keeping high beam on elsewhere.
- Uses **DLP (Digital Light Processing)** or **μAFS** modules.

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy – Flashlight vs. Spotlight:** Think of headlights like the difference between a basic flashlight (sealed beam, light spreads everywhere) and a precision spotlight (projector HID/LED, light is sculpted into a sharp, controlled beam). The reflector is the *mirror dish* behind the bulb; the lens is the *glass window* that focuses or shapes the beam. Modern cars increasingly use the *projector* approach – like a slide projector – to get a sharp cutoff line.

### 1.3 Headlight Reflectors – Definition

A **headlight reflector** is a highly polished, vacuum-metallized (typically aluminum-coated) parabolic, ellipsoidal, or free-form surface placed behind the light source. Its function is to **collect light radiated in the backward hemisphere** (about 65 % of total luminous flux) and **redirect it forward**, parallel to the optical axis, so that the lens can shape it into the legally required beam pattern.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Reflector:** An optical component whose interior surface is shaped to redirect rays emitted by the light source into a controlled forward distribution, maximising the on-road illuminance within ECE/AIS limits.

### 1.4 Headlight Lenses – Definition

The **headlight lens** is the transparent front cover of the headlamp. It is manufactured from either **tempered glass** or (more commonly today) **polycarbonate (PC)** with a hard anti-UV coating. The outer surface is smooth (aerodynamic), while the inner surface carries a pattern of **flutes / prisms / Fresnel rings** that refract and re-distribute light rays to form the beam pattern (cut-off line, spread, hot-spot).

### 1.5 Indicator Lamps (Turn Signals) – Definition

**Indicator lamps** (also called turn signals, flashers, or direction indicators) are amber-coloured signalling devices fitted to the front, rear, and sides of a vehicle. They communicate the driver's intent to change direction or, when operated together, to indicate a hazard (Hazard Warning).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ECE Regulation No. 6 / AIS-008 mandates that **front, rear, and side indicators** must produce **amber light** (UN "yellow-amber" chromaticity boundary: x ≈ 0.585, y ≈ 0.415). White turn indicators (used on US-market vehicles) are illegal under ECE regulations.

### 1.6 Lighting Circuit – Definition

The **lighting circuit** of an automobile comprises the headlamps (dipped beam, main beam), parking/sidelights, tail lamps, brake lamps, reverse lamps, indicators, and instrument-panel illumination, all interconnected through a **wiring harness**, **switches**, **relays**, **fuses**, and the **body control module (BCM)** in modern vehicles.

### 1.7 Projector Headlights – Definition

A **projector headlight** (also called a DE – Discharge Ellipsoidal headlight, since it is required for HID bulbs) is a headlamp that uses an **ellipsoidal reflector**, a **shield (cut-off plate/shutter)**, and a **condenser lens** to project a sharp, well-defined beam with a precise horizontal cut-off line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Projector Headlamp:** A headlamp employing an ellipsoidal reflector that focuses light onto a focal point where a mechanical shutter trims the beam to produce a sharp cut-off, after which a plano-convex condenser lens collimates the beam onto the road.

---

## 1.8 Summary Table – Comparison of Headlight Types

| Parameter | Sealed Beam | Halogen | HID / Xenon | LED | Laser |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Luminous Flux (lm) | 1,000 | 1,500 | 3,200 | 2,000–4,000 | 5,500+ |
| Color Temp (K) | 2,800 | 3,200 | 4,300–6,000 | 5,000–6,500 | 5,500–6,000 |
| Efficacy (lm/W) | 18 | 25 | 90 | 80–100 | 170 |
| Service Life (hrs) | 200 | 500–1,000 | 2,500 | 10,000+ | 30,000+ |
| Ballast / Driver | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Cost (relative) | Lowest | Low | High | Medium-High | Highest |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory – How a Headlight Produces a Beam

The process of producing a road-legal headlamp beam involves three sequential optical transformations:

1. **Generation** – Electrical energy → light energy inside the bulb (filament or arc).
2. **Collection & Direction** – The reflector captures diverging rays and converts them into a near-parallel (collimated) beam.
3. **Shaping** – The lens (fluted/prismatic) or projector shield distributes the beam on the road with a defined cut-off and spread.

### 2.2 Theory of Headlight Reflectors

#### (a) Parabolic Reflector
The classical headlamp reflector is a **paraboloid of revolution** whose axis coincides with the optical axis. The defining property is:

$$ y^2 = 4 p x $$

where $p$ is the focal length (distance from vertex to focus). The **filament is placed at the focus** $F(p, 0)$. Any ray leaving $F$ reflects off the paraboloid parallel to the **+X axis**.

- **Focal length (p):** typically 25–35 mm in sealed-beam units; 15–25 mm in modern free-form reflectors.
- **Aperture diameter:** 170–220 mm in round headlamps; 100 × 220 mm in rectangular units.
- **Reflector material:** Borosilicate glass moulded to shape, coated internally with **vacuum-deposited aluminium** (reflectivity > 90 % in visible spectrum).
- **Collection efficiency:** A parabolic reflector can collect roughly **65 %** of the bulb's total luminous flux and redirect it into the forward hemisphere.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why parabolic?** A spherical reflector suffers from spherical aberration (rays from a point source do not converge to a single image point). A paraboloid has the unique property that *all* paraxial rays from its focus become parallel after reflection. This produces the collimated beam required for a headlamp.

#### (b) Ellipsoidal Reflector (Used in Projector Lamps)
An ellipsoid has **two foci**, $F_1$ and $F_2$. The defining property is that the **sum of distances** from any point $P$ on the surface to the two foci is constant:

$$ \vert PF_1 \vert + \vert PF_2 \vert = 2a $$

where $2a$ is the **major axis length**. When the bulb sits at the **primary focus** $F_1$ (close to the bulb), light rays converge at the **secondary focus** $F_2$ (just in front of the lens), forming a small intense image of the arc/filament. This image is then collimated by the condenser lens.

#### (c) Multi-Surface / Free-Form Reflectors
Modern headlamps use **computer-designed free-form surfaces** with multiple non-symmetric zones (sometimes 50–100 facets). Each zone directs light to a specific region of the road. The trade-off: more uniform illumination at the cost of complex mould design.

### 2.3 Theory of Headlight Lenses

The front lens of a headlamp is *not* a simple window – it is an **optical element** that shapes the beam. The lens outer face is smooth (aerodynamic, easy to clean, scratch-resistant after hard-coating). The inner face contains:

#### (a) Flutes / Prismatic Patterns
Small prism-shaped ridges (each typically 0.5–2 mm wide, 30–60° apex angle) refract light by total internal reflection or refraction. Different zones of the lens correspond to different regions of the beam:
- **Central zone** → controls the bright "hot-spot" (illumination directly ahead).
- **Upper zone** → flattens the beam, produces the **horizontal cut-off line**.
- **Lower zone** → spreads light toward road edges and road signs.
- **Side zones** → throw light onto curbs and road shoulders.

#### (b) Fresnel Lens (Used in Sealed Beams)
A flat-equivalent lens divided into concentric annular rings. Each ring refracts light to a focal point as a full-thickness lens would, but the lens is flat and lightweight.

#### (c) Plano-Convex Condenser Lens (Used in Projectors)
A spherical convex lens, typically 50–70 mm diameter, made of optical-grade glass or polycarbonate, with a focal length of about 25–40 mm.

### 2.4 Theory of Indicator Lamps

#### (a) Photometric Requirements (ECE R6 / AIS-008)
The minimum and maximum luminous intensity of an indicator at the reference axis (H = 0°, V = 0°) is given by:

- **Category 1, 1a, 1b (front):** min 175 cd, max 1,000 cd
- **Category 2a (rear):** min 50 cd, max 500 cd
- **Category 5 (side repeater):** min 17.5 cd, max 500 cd

#### (b) Flashing Frequency
The indicator must flash at a rate of **1 Hz ± 0.5 Hz** (60–120 flashes per minute), with the "ON" duration equal to or greater than the "OFF" duration.

#### (c) Bulb Types
- **P21W** – 21 W, BA15s (single contact), white (rear).
- **PY21W** – 21 W, BAU15s (offset pins), amber glass (front).
- **H21W** – 21 W, BAY9s, halogen.
- **LED** – increasingly common, often with pulse-width-modulated (PWM) flasher.

### 2.5 Theory of the Lighting Circuit

A typical lighting circuit contains the following blocks:

- **Battery** (12 V / 24 V) – supplies electrical energy.
- **Master light switch** – multi-position rotary or push-button (OFF / Parking / Auto / Headlamp).
- **Dip switch / Stalk** – toggles between dipped beam (low) and main beam (high).
- **Headlamp relay(s)** – high-current coil (~ 100 mA) controls high-current load (~ 5 A per lamp), preventing switch contact welding.
- **Fuses** – protect each branch (typically 10 A for headlights, 5 A for parking lamps).
- **Lamps** – H4 / H7 / D2S / LED modules.
- **BCM (Body Control Module)** – in modern cars, the BCM controls the lamps via MOSFET drivers and monitors bulb health.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use a relay?** A halogen H4 bulb draws ~ 4.5 A per filament. Without a relay, this current flows through the dashboard switch, leading to voltage drop, contact pitting, and heat. A relay allows a low-current switch to control a high-current load via a magnetic coil.

### 2.6 Theory of Projector Headlights

#### Components
1. **Light source** – Halogen (H7, H9, H11), HID (D2S/D4S), or LED.
2. **Ellipsoidal reflector bowl** – concentrates light at the secondary focus.
3. **Cut-off shield (shutter)** – a small metal blade positioned at the secondary focus. The shield's straight top edge creates the **sharp horizontal cut-off line** that prevents glare for oncoming drivers.
4. **Condenser lens** – plano-convex glass lens that collimates the beam from the shield.

#### Bi-Xenon / Bi-LED Projector
In a **bi-xenon** projector, an electromagnet (solenoid) tilts the shield down (or removes it entirely) when high beam is selected. This *single projector* produces both low and high beams – hence "bi".

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Law | Expression | Used For | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parabolic reflector | $y^2 = 4 p x$ | Predicting reflected ray parallelism | mm |
| Ellipsoid equation | $\dfrac{x^2}{a^2}+\dfrac{y^2}{b^2}+\dfrac{z^2}{b^2}=1$ | Predicting dual focus geometry | mm |
| Luminous flux (Φ) | $\Phi = 4\pi I_{avg}$ | Total light output of bulb | lm |
| Illuminance (E) | $E=\dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$ | Light reaching the road (Inverse-square law) | lux (lm/m²) |
| Luminous efficacy | $\eta=\dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | Efficiency of light source | lm/W |
| Voltage drop in wire | $V_{drop}=\dfrac{I \rho L}{A}$ | Wiring harness design | V |
| Power dissipated | $P = V I$ | Bulb / relay rating | W |
| Relay coil current | $I_{coil}=\dfrac{V_{bat}}{R_{coil}}$ | Coil design verification | A |
| Flasher frequency | $f=\dfrac{1}{T_{on}+T_{off}}$ | Indicator pulse rate | Hz |
| Cut-off angle (headlamp) | $\theta_{cutoff}=0.57^\circ \pm 0.05^\circ$ | ECE R112 photometric rule | degrees |

> [!TIP]
> **Important Constants for KTU:**
> - Standard vehicle voltage: **12 V DC** (passenger cars), **24 V DC** (CV/HCV).
> - ECE cut-off line inclination: **0.57° below horizontal** (this is the famous "elbow" line).
> - Indicator flash rate: **1 ± 0.5 Hz** (60–90 fpm typical).
> - Color of front & rear indicators: **Amber/Yellow** (ECE), **Red/White** (FMVSS-108 US).

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **Reflector design** dictates beam reach (long-distance straight highway driving needs narrow, parallel rays).
- **Lens fluting** controls side-spread (urban driving needs wide spread for pedestrian visibility).
- **Indicator visibility** is a safety-critical legal requirement (failure to indicate causes accidents at junctions).
- **Projector headlights** deliver ~ 30 % more on-road illumination than equivalent reflectors, with much less glare.
- **Lighting circuit relays** extend switch life ten-fold and improve headlamp illumination by reducing voltage drop (a poorly wired halogen lamp can lose 0.5–1.0 V, equivalent to ~ 15 % loss in luminous flux).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Luminous Flux from Luminous Intensity

The **luminous intensity** $I$ of a source is defined as the luminous flux $\Phi$ emitted per unit solid angle $\Omega$ in a given direction.

$$ I = \frac{d\Phi}{d\Omega} $$

For an isotropic source (uniform intensity in all directions), the total solid angle is $4\pi$ steradians. Therefore:

$$ \Phi_{total} = \int_0^{4\pi} I \, d\Omega = I \cdot 4\pi $$

For a non-isotropic headlamp source, integration must be performed over the **upper hemisphere** ($2\pi$ sr) only, since the bulb radiates mainly forward.

$$ \Phi_{forward} = \int_{hemisphere} I(\theta, \phi) \, d\Omega \approx 2\pi \bar{I} $$

> **Numerical Example (KTU-type):** A halogen H4 bulb emits 1,500 lm. If 65 % is collected by the parabolic reflector and 90 % transmitted through the lens, the road-going flux is:
>
> $$\Phi_{road} = 0.65 \times 0.90 \times 1500 = 877.5 \text{ lm}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Inverse-Square Law of Illuminance

Consider a point source of luminous intensity $I$ at the origin. The flux $\Phi$ crossing a small surface area $dA$ at distance $d$ is distributed over the surface of a sphere of radius $d$. The **illuminance** $E$ is defined as flux per unit area.

**Step 1:** Total flux through the sphere of radius $d$:

$$ \Phi = I \cdot \Omega = I \cdot \frac{A_{sphere}}{d^2} \cdot d^2 \cdot \frac{1}{d^2} $$

Wait – let us apply this rigorously. The solid angle subtended by an area $dA$ perpendicular to the source direction is:

$$ d\Omega = \frac{dA}{d^2} $$

**Step 2:** The illuminance is therefore:

$$ E = \frac{d\Phi}{dA} = \frac{I \, d\Omega}{dA} = \frac{I \cdot dA/d^2}{dA} = \frac{I}{d^2} $$

If the surface is tilted by angle $\theta$ from the source direction, the projected area becomes $dA \cos\theta$, giving the **cosine law of illuminance**:

$$ E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^2} $$

> **Numerical Example:** A halogen bulb has luminous intensity $I = 30,000$ cd at the beam axis. The road point is 50 m ahead and 0.75 m below the lamp axis.
>
> $$\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{0.75}{50}\right) = 0.859^\circ$$
>
> $$E = \frac{30{,}000 \times \cos(0.859^\circ)}{50^2} = \frac{30{,}000 \times 0.99988}{2500} = 11.999 \text{ lux}$$
>
> This matches the ECE R112 minimum of 12 lux at point 75R (50 m, 0.75 m below H-H).

## 3.3 Derivation: Parabola Proof (Why a Paraboloid Collimates Light)

A parabola has focus $F(p, 0)$ and directrix $x = -p$. The defining property is:

$$ \vert PF \vert = \text{perpendicular distance from } P \text{ to directrix} $$

For any point $P(x, y)$ on the parabola:

$$ \sqrt{(x-p)^2 + y^2} = x + p $$

Squaring both sides:

$$ (x-p)^2 + y^2 = (x+p)^2 $$

$$ x^2 - 2px + p^2 + y^2 = x^2 + 2px + p^2 $$

$$ y^2 = 4px $$

This is the standard form of a parabola. The **reflective property** states that a ray from $F$ reflects off the parabola parallel to the X-axis. Proof: the tangent at any point on a parabola makes equal angles with the line to the focus and the line parallel to the axis. Hence the **angle of incidence = angle of reflection** guarantees the ray exits parallel to the axis.

> **Numerical Example:** A parabolic reflector has focal length $p = 25$ mm. At a horizontal distance of $x = 200$ mm from the vertex, the parabola's depth is:
>
> $$y = \sqrt{4 \times 25 \times 200} = \sqrt{20{,}000} = 141.4 \text{ mm}$$
>
> This represents the half-diameter of the reflector's aperture – i.e. ~ 142 mm radius, or ~ 285 mm diameter aperture. This is typical for a large halogen headlamp.

## 3.4 Derivation: Voltage Drop in Headlamp Wiring

Headlamp wiring must be sized to keep the voltage drop below **0.3 V** at the bulb (10 % of 12 V is acceptable; many manufacturers target < 0.2 V for accurate photometric output).

**Step 1:** Resistance of a copper wire:

$$ R = \frac{\rho L}{A} $$

where $\rho_{Cu} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$.

**Step 2:** Voltage drop:

$$ V_{drop} = I R = \frac{I \rho L}{A} $$

**Step 3:** Required cross-sectional area for a given drop:

$$ A_{min} = \frac{I \rho L}{V_{drop, max}} $$

> **Numerical Example:** A halogen bulb draws 4.5 A. The cable run from battery to bulb is 4 m total (positive + negative). Allowable voltage drop = 0.3 V.
>
> $$A_{min} = \frac{4.5 \times 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \times 4}{0.3} = 1.032 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2 = 1.032 \text{ mm}^2$$
>
> Hence **1.5 mm² wire** is the next standard size, safely handling up to 14 A.

## 3.5 Derivation: Indicator Flasher Frequency

A thermal (bimetallic) flasher has a heating coil ($R_h$) in series with the indicator bulbs. When cold, the bimetallic strip closes the contact → current flows → bulbs light. As the heating coil warms, the bimetallic strip bends, breaking the contact → bulbs go off and current ceases → the strip cools and closes again.

**Step 1:** Heating time (lamps ON):

$$ T_{on} \propto \frac{m \, c \, \Delta T}{P_{heat} - P_{loss}} $$

**Step 2:** Cooling time (lamps OFF):

$$ T_{off} \propto \frac{m \, c \, \Delta T}{P_{loss}} $$

**Step 3:** Frequency:

$$ f = \frac{1}{T_{on} + T_{off}} $$

For ECE compliance, $f$ must lie between **0.5 Hz and 2 Hz**; in practice $f \approx 1.5$ Hz (90 flashes/min).

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix – Reflector vs. Projector

| Parameter | Parabolic Reflector Headlamp | Projector (Ellipsoidal) Headlamp |
|---|---|---|
| Optical geometry | Paraboloid (1 focus) | Ellipsoid (2 foci) + condenser lens |
| Beam cut-off | Soft, diffuse (controlled by lens flutes) | Sharp, knife-edge (controlled by shield) |
| Cut-off line angle | ~ 0.57° | ~ 0.57° (sharper, less glare) |
| Glare to oncoming driver | Higher (lens-based control only) | Lower (mechanical shield + lens) |
| Axial luminance | Lower | ~ 30 % higher |
| Bulb alignment tolerance | ±1 mm | ±0.3 mm (more critical) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Application | Economy cars, sealed beams | Mid/High-end cars, HID, LED |

## 3.7 Beam Pattern Specification (ECE R112 / AIS-008)

| Test Point | Distance | Position | Min Illuminance |
|---|---|---|---|
| HV (Hot spot / point 0) | 25 m | 0 m H, 0 m V | 0.8 lux (low beam) |
| 75R | 25 m | 75 m right, 0.5 m below H-H | 6 lux |
| 50R | 25 m | 50 m right, 0.5 m below H-H | 6 lux |
| 50V | 25 m | 0 m H, 0.5 m below H-H | 6 lux |
| 25L | 25 m | 25 m left, 1.5 m above H-H | 1.5 lux (for road signs) |
| B50L | 25 m | 50 m left, 0.25 m above H-H | ≤ 0.4 lux (anti-glare) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram – Halogen Headlamp Subsystem

This diagram maps the functional architecture of a complete halogen headlamp unit (left) and its relationship to the vehicle electrical system (right).

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Battery 12V] --> B[Master Light Switch]
    B --> C[Relay Coil 100mA]
    C --> D[Relay Contacts 30A]
    A --> D
    D --> E[Fuse 10A]
    E --> F[Headlamp Left H7]
    E --> G[Headlamp Right H7]
    F --> H[Reflector Parabolic]
    G --> I[Reflector Parabolic]
    H --> J[Bulb Filament at Focus]
    I --> K[Bulb Filament at Focus]
    J --> L[Lens Fluted Front]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Beam Pattern on Road]
    N[Dip Switch] --> B
    O[Body Control Module BCM] -.Monitors.-> F
    O -.Monitors.-> G
```

> **Interpretation:** The BCM monitors bulb current via shunt resistors. If a bulb fails (current drops), BCM alerts the driver via dashboard indicator.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture – Projector (DE) Headlamp Block Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Power Supply 12V DC] --> B[Ballast DC-DC Inverter]
    B --> C[Igniter 25kV Pulse]
    C --> D[HID Bulb D2S Arc Tube]
    D --> E[Ellipsoidal Reflector Bowl]
    E --> F[Secondary Focus Point]
    F --> G[Cut-off Shield Shutter]
    G --> H[Condenser Lens Plano-Convex]
    H --> I[Forward Beam on Road]
    J[Beam Select Solenoid] --> G
    K[BCM Control Unit] --> J
    K --> B
```

> **Interpretation:** The bulb arc emits light → ellipsoidal reflector converges it at the secondary focus → a metal shield clips the upper half of the light → the condenser lens collimates the remaining lower half into a sharp horizontal beam. When the driver selects high beam, the solenoid tilts the shield down, projecting the entire beam onto the road.

## 4.3 Automotive Lighting Circuit – Complete Wiring Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    BAT[Battery 12V] --> MAIN[Main Light Switch 4-Position]
    MAIN --> OFF[OFF]
    MAIN --> PARK[Position PARK]
    MAIN --> HEAD[Position HEAD]
    MAIN --> AUTO[Position AUTO]
    PARK --> PSR[Parking Lamps 5W x4]
    HEAD --> RL[Relay Headlamp Coil]
    RL --> RC[Relay Contacts NO]
    BAT --> RC
    RC --> F1[Fuse 15A Dipped]
    RC --> F2[Fuse 15A Main Beam]
    F1 --> LBL[Left Dipped Lamp H7]
    F1 --> LBR[Right Dipped Lamp H7]
    F2 --> LML[Left Main Beam H1]
    F2 --> LMR[Right Main Beam H1]
    DIP[Dip Switch Stalk] --> RL
    MAIN --> TAIL[Tail Lamp 5W x2]
    MAIN --> ILL[Instrument Illumination PWM]
    MAIN --> F3[Fuse 10A Indicators]
    F3 --> IND[Indicator Flasher Unit]
    IND --> FLF[Front Left Indicator PY21W]
    IND --> FRF[Front Right Indicator PY21W]
    IND --> RLR[Rear Left Indicator P21W]
    IND --> RRR[Rear Right Indicator P21W]
    IND --> SLF[Side Repeater Left]
    IND --> SLR[Side Repeater Right]
    HAZ[Hazard Switch] --> IND
    BCM[BCM ECU] -.PWM Control.-> ILL
    BCM -.Bulb Monitoring.-> LBL
```

> **Interpretation:** The master switch selects OFF, PARK, HEAD, or AUTO mode. In HEAD mode, the relay coil is energised (low current through the switch), closing the relay contacts (high current to lamps). Dipped beam uses H7 lamps; main beam uses H1 lamps. The dip switch toggles the relay or selects between dipped/main via the BCM.

## 4.4 Lens Fluting – Functional Effect on Beam Distribution

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Lens Central Zone] --> A1[Beam Hot-Spot Straight Ahead]
    B[Lens Upper Zone] --> B1[Beam Cut-Off Line Horizontal]
    C[Lens Lower Zone] --> C1[Beam Spread Near Field 0-25m]
    D[Lens Right Zone] --> D1[Beam Right Spread up to 90m]
    E[Lens Left Zone] --> E1[Beam Left Spread Kerb]
    F[Lens Side Zone] --> F1[Wide Spread 100 degree]
```

> **Interpretation:** Each flute/prism zone in the lens performs a specific optical function – the lens is essentially a "pixel-level" beam shaper.

## 4.5 Block Flow – Headlight Beam Generation Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Electrical Energy] --> S2[Step 2 Filament or Arc Generates Light]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Reflector Collects Backward Flux]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Reflector Collimates Rays]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Lens Refracts to Form Pattern]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Cut-off Line and Spread on Road]
    S7[Projector Variant] --> S8[Step 3b Ellipsoid Focuses at Point]
    S8 --> S9[Step 4b Shield Defines Cut-off]
    S9 --> S10[Step 5b Condenser Lens Collimates]
    S10 --> S6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between a sealed-beam headlamp and a halogen headlamp.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**
- **Sealed beam headlamp:** The filament, reflector, and lens are fused into one hermetically sealed glass unit. The bulb cannot be replaced; the entire unit must be discarded upon filament failure. The cavity contains argon gas at near-atmospheric pressure. Filament temperature ≈ 2,500 K, luminous flux ≈ 1,000 lm, life ≈ 200 hrs.
- **Halogen headlamp:** A small replaceable quartz-halogen bulb (H1, H4, H7, etc.) is inserted into a separate reflector+lens housing. The bulb contains a halogen gas (iodine/bromine) at 5–10 bar, enabling the halogen regenerative cycle. Filament temperature ≈ 2,900 K, luminous flux ≈ 1,500 lm, life ≈ 500–1,000 hrs.

| **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + Working (1) + Comparison (1) |

### Q2. What is the function of a parabolic reflector in a headlamp? State its equation.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**
- **Function:** A parabolic reflector, with the bulb filament placed at its focus, collects diverging light rays emitted in the backward hemisphere (about 65 % of total luminous flux) and reflects them into a parallel (collimated) beam along the optical axis. This collimated beam is then shaped by the front lens into the road beam pattern.
- **Equation:** $y^2 = 4 p x$, where $p$ is the focal length (typically 25–35 mm).
- **Why parabolic?** A ray from the focus reflects parallel to the axis because the tangent at any point of the parabola makes equal angles with the focal line and the axis (Fermat's principle of equal optical path length).

| **Marks Distribution:** Function (2) + Equation (1) |

---

## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't skip the "reason" or "physics"** behind reflector geometry – explaining *why* a paraboloid collimates light (not just *that* it does) earns full marks.
> 2. **Draw neat labelled diagrams** for every question (headlamp section, lens fluting, circuit). A diagram alone, even without text, typically scores 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Cite ECE/AIS regulation numbers** (R112, R48, R6, AIS-008) wherever photometric or color specs are asked. Examiners reward regulation-aware answers.
> 4. **In numerical problems,** always state the formula, substitute with units, and box the final answer. Skipping units = −1 mark.
> 5. **In circuit diagrams,** mark every component (fuse, relay coil, relay contact, switch positions). A circuit without component labels loses easy marks.

---

### Q-A. (14 Marks) – Question Choice A

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of a **projector (DE) headlamp**. Compare it with a conventional parabolic headlamp.

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Definition:**
A projector headlamp (DE – Discharge Ellipsoidal) is an optical assembly that uses an ellipsoidal reflector, a mechanical cut-off shield (shutter), and a condenser lens to project a sharp, well-defined beam with a precise horizontal cut-off line.

**Step 2 – Components (sketch required):**

1. **Light source** – Halogen (H7, H9, H11), HID (D2S, D4S), or LED module, positioned at the **primary focus** $F_1$ of the ellipsoid.
2. **Ellipsoidal reflector bowl** – Polished aluminium-coated glass or PC. Gathers diverging rays from $F_1$ and converges them to the **secondary focus** $F_2$.
3. **Cut-off shield (shutter)** – A small metal blade positioned at $F_2$. Its straight horizontal upper edge defines the **cut-off line**. Any ray that would project above the horizon is blocked.
4. **Condenser lens** – Plano-convex glass lens (focal length ~ 25–40 mm) that collimates the light emerging from the shield into a parallel forward beam.
5. **Solenoid actuator** (in bi-xenon units) – Tilts the shield down when high beam is selected, unblocking the upper beam zone.

**Step 3 – Working:**
1. Bulb emits light → ellipsoid reflects it toward $F_2$ → shield clips upper half → condenser lens collimates → beam exits.
2. The shield's edge, being in the focal plane of the condenser lens, projects as a **sharp horizontal cut-off line** on the road.
3. For high beam, the solenoid tilts the shield ~ 0.4° downward, removing the obstruction → full beam is projected.

**Step 4 – Comparison with Parabolic Headlamp:**

| Parameter | Parabolic | Projector |
|---|---|---|
| Reflector shape | Paraboloid (1 focus) | Ellipsoid (2 foci) + condenser lens |
| Cut-off sharpness | Diffuse (lens-only control) | Sharp (mechanical shield) |
| Anti-glare | Lower | Higher |
| Beam precision | Lower | Higher |
| Bulb tolerance | Loose | Tight |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

**Step 5 – Applications:**
Standard on all HID/LED-equipped modern vehicles; mandatory in some markets.

| **Marks Distribution:** Definition (2) + Components (2) + Working (2) + Comparison (1) |

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of an **automotive headlamp lighting circuit** using a relay. List the components and their functions.

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Circuit Diagram (sketch required):**

```
Battery(+) ──► Master Switch ──► Relay Coil ──► Ground
Battery(+) ──► Relay Contact (NO) ──► Fuse 15A ──► H7 Lamp ──► Ground
Dip Switch ──► Relay Coil Driver
```

**Step 2 – List of Components & Functions:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Battery (12 V / 24 V) | Source of DC electrical energy |
| Master Light Switch | Selects OFF / PARK / HEAD / AUTO modes |
| Dip Switch (Stalk) | Toggles between dipped beam (low) and main beam (high) |
| Relay (SPDT or SPST-NO) | Allows low-current control of high-current lamp load |
| Fuse (15 A typical) | Protects wiring from over-current / short circuit |
| Headlamp Bulb (H7 / H1) | Light source |
| Wiring harness | Provides current path |
| BCM (Body Control Module) | Monitors bulb health, controls PWM illumination |
| Earth/Ground return | Completes circuit |

**Step 3 – Working:**
1. When the driver turns the master switch to HEAD, current flows through the relay coil (~ 100 mA via a low-current path).
2. The electromagnetic field closes the relay's NO contact, completing the high-current path from battery → relay contact → fuse → bulb → ground.
3. The bulb draws ~ 4.5 A (H7) and lights up.
4. The dip switch energises either the dipped-beam relay or main-beam relay, selecting which filament lights.
5. The fuse blows if current exceeds rating, protecting the wiring.

**Step 4 – Why a Relay?**
- Reduces voltage drop at the bulb (relay contacts have < 5 mΩ resistance).
- Extends switch life (low current through dashboard switch).
- Allows multiple control signals (BCM, autolight sensor).

**Step 5 – Numerical verification (optional):**
A halogen H7 bulb draws 4.5 A at 13.5 V. If a 4 m, 1.5 mm² wire is used (R = 0.077 Ω), the drop is $V_{drop} = 4.5 \times 0.077 = 0.35$ V. This is within the 0.3–0.5 V acceptable range.

| **Marks Distribution:** Diagram (3) + Components (1) + Working (2) + Relay justification (1) |

---

### Q-B. (14 Marks) – Question Choice B

#### (a) [7 Marks] Describe the **types of headlights** used in automobiles. Compare halogen, HID, and LED headlamps in terms of luminous efficacy, color temperature, and life.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Types of Headlights:**

1. **Sealed Beam Headlights** – Filament, reflector, lens fused in one unit (older cars).
2. **Halogen Headlights** – Replaceable halogen bulb + separate reflector/lens housing (modern economy cars).
3. **HID/Xenon Headlights** – Arc-discharge bulb with ballast (mid/high-end cars).
4. **LED Headlights** – Light-emitting diodes with driver electronics (modern premium cars).
5. **Laser Headlights** – Laser-excited phosphor (high-end, e.g., BMW, Audi).
6. **Adaptive/Matrix Headlights** – Camera-controlled pixel-level beam shaping.

**Step 2 – Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Halogen | HID | LED |
|---|---|---|---|
| Luminous flux (lm) | 1,500 | 3,200 | 2,000–4,000 |
| Color temperature (K) | 3,200 | 4,300–6,000 | 5,000–6,500 |
| Efficacy (lm/W) | 25 | 90 | 80–100 |
| Service life (hrs) | 500–1,000 | 2,500 | 10,000+ |
| Ballast/driver needed | No | Yes (igniter + ballast) | Yes (LED driver) |
| Cost | Lowest | High | Medium-High |
| Warm-up time | Instant | 3–5 sec | Instant |

**Step 3 – Detailed Working:**

- **Halogen:** Tungsten filament heated to 2,900 K in a halogen gas (I₂/Br₂). Halogen regenerative cycle prevents blackening of the bulb wall. Bulb life ≈ 500–1,000 hrs.
- **HID:** Xenon gas + mercury/metal halide salts. Two electrodes separated by 4–5 mm. Ignition pulse of 25 kV strikes an arc. Ballast limits current to 0.4 A. Lumen output nearly double halogen. Bulb life ≈ 2,500 hrs.
- **LED:** Electrons recombining with electron holes in a semiconductor (typically GaN/InGaN) emit photons. Multiple LEDs arranged on a PCB with thermal management (heat sink, fan). Instant on/off, very long life.

**Step 4 – Selection Criteria:**
- Economy segment: Halogen (low cost).
- Mid-segment: HID (high brightness).
- Premium segment: LED/Matrix LED (efficiency + adaptive).

| **Marks Distribution:** Types (2) + Working (2) + Comparison table (2) + Selection (1) |

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the **function of the headlamp lens** and describe different fluting patterns used to shape the beam. Sketch the ECE R112 low-beam pattern.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Function of the Lens:**
The front lens is not a plain cover – it is an **active optical element** that performs:
1. **Protection** – seals the bulb and reflector from dust, water, stones.
2. **Beam shaping** – flutes/prisms refract light to control the cut-off line, beam spread, and side illumination.
3. **Aesthetic integration** – allows sleek aerodynamic shapes.

**Step 2 – Fluting Patterns (with zones):**

The inner surface of the lens is divided into distinct zones, each responsible for shaping a specific region of the beam:

| Lens Zone | Optical Pattern | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Central | Vertical/horizontal flutes | Bright "hot-spot" (illumination ahead) |
| Upper | Tight horizontal prisms | Sharp horizontal cut-off line |
| Lower | Diagonal flutes | Spread near the vehicle (0–25 m) |
| Right | Diagonal flutes | Side spread for road signs, pedestrians |
| Left | Curved flutes | Spread toward the kerb |
| Side edges | Wide-angle prisms | Wide lateral spread (~ 100°) |

**Step 3 – ECE R112 Low Beam Pattern:**

The low beam pattern is **asymmetric**:
- **Left side** – Higher cut-off (anti-glare for oncoming traffic).
- **Right side** – Cut-off tilted upward by **0.57°** (the famous "elbow") to illuminate road signs and pedestrians on the right.
- **Hot-spot** – Located ~ 50 m ahead and slightly right of the vehicle centreline.

(Sketch required: horizontal cut-off line on the left, elbow rising upward on the right, hot-spot in the centre-right.)

**Step 4 – Construction Material:**
- **Glass** – Excellent optical clarity, scratch-resistant, heavier, can shatter.
- **Polycarbonate (PC)** – Lightweight, impact-resistant, requires UV-hard-coating to prevent yellowing.

| **Marks Distribution:** Function (2) + Fluting (2) + ECE pattern (2) + Material (1) |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Headlights – Types
- **Sealed beam** – single fused unit; obsolete in modern cars.
- **Halogen** – H1, H4, H7, H11; uses halogen regenerative cycle.
- **HID/Xenon** – D1S, D2S, D3S, D4S; needs ballast and igniter (25 kV).
- **LED** – 5,000–6,500 K, 80–100 lm/W, > 10,000 hrs life.
- **Laser** – blue laser + yellow phosphor; range ~ 600 m.

### Headlight Reflectors
- **Parabolic** – $y^2 = 4px$, collimates rays from focus.
- **Ellipsoidal** – 2 foci; used in projector lamps.
- **Free-form / multi-surface** – computer-designed for uniform beam.
- Reflector collects ~ 65 % of bulb flux.

### Headlight Lenses
- Outer face: smooth (aerodynamic).
- Inner face: **fluted / prismatic** for beam shaping.
- Lens zones: central (hot-spot), upper (cut-off), lower (near-field), sides (spread).
- Material: tempered glass or UV-coated polycarbonate.

### Indicator Lamps
- Color: **Amber** (ECE) or **White/Red** (FMVSS US).
- Flash rate: **1 ± 0.5 Hz**.
- Bulbs: P21W, PY21W, H21W, or LED.
- Flasher types: thermal, electronic, BCM-controlled.

### Lighting Circuit
- Battery → Master switch → Relay coil → Relay contacts → Fuse → Bulb → Ground.
- Use of **relay** reduces voltage drop and extends switch life.
- Maximum allowable voltage drop at bulb: **0.3 V**.
- Fuse rating: typically **10–15 A**.
- BCM monitors bulb health.

### Projector Headlights
- Components: bulb + ellipsoid + cut-off shield + condenser lens.
- Sharp **horizontal cut-off** prevents glare.
- **Bi-xenon / bi-LED** projectors use a solenoid to tilt shield for high beam.
- Beam control superior to parabolic reflectors.

### Key Photometric Formulas (Must Remember)
- $\Phi = 4\pi I$ (isotropic source)
- $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$ (inverse-square + cosine law)
- $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ (efficacy)
- $y^2 = 4px$ (parabola)
- $V_{drop} = \dfrac{I \rho L}{A}$ (wire sizing)

### ECE / AIS Regulation Quick Reference
- ECE R112 – Headlamps (asymmetric low beam).
- ECE R48 – Installation of lighting & signalling devices.
- ECE R6 – Direction indicators.
- AIS-008 (India) – Equivalent of R48/R112/R6.
- ECE cut-off angle: **0.57° below horizontal** on the right side.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Horn and Wiper Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT501 - AUTOMOBILE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Horn and Wiper Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Horn Mechanisms

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
An **automotive horn** is an electromechanical or electropneumatic audible warning device mounted on a vehicle, designed to alert pedestrians and other road users of the vehicle's approach or presence. The **horn mechanism** is the integrated assembly comprising an electromagnetic coil, vibrating diaphragm, contact breaker (interruptor), acoustic resonator, and a horn relay that together convert electrical energy from the vehicle's battery into a sustained acoustic signal of calibrated loudness and pitch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The horn mechanism must be studied under three pillars: (i) **Constructional anatomy** of the electromagnetic/vibrating reed type horn, (ii) **Working principle** of the contact-breaker and diaphragm resonance, and (iii) **Horn relay** operation and its necessity in modern vehicles with high-current horn circuits.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of the horn as a **rapidly tapping drumhead driven by a magnet**:
- The diaphragm is the drumhead.
- The electromagnet repeatedly pulls and releases it.
- Each pull-release is one tap; thousands of taps per second become a steady musical note.
- The resonator (horn trumpet) is like the body of a trumpet — without it, the sound would be thin and weak. The trumpet shape amplifies specific frequencies, just like a guitar body amplifies string vibrations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Standard:** Horn loudness is measured in **decibels (dB)** at a distance of **2 meters** from the vehicle. KTU-relevant standard values lie in the range **100 dB to 118 dB**, with a fundamental frequency typically between **400 Hz and 500 Hz** for the high-note horn and **300 Hz to 400 Hz** for the low-note horn (dual-tone setup).

---

## 1.2 Wiper Mechanisms

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
The **wiper mechanism** is a windshield cleaning subsystem comprising a wiper motor (PMDC or wound-field), a mechanical linkage (single-arm, three-bar, or four-bar pantograph), wiper arms, wiper blades, a multi-position control switch, and a **park-position switch** that automatically returns the blades to a rest position along the lower windshield edge after switch-off.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture the wiper as a **robotic windshield painter**:
- The motor is the **muscle** that supplies rotational energy.
- The linkage is the **skeleton** of bones and joints that converts circular motion into a back-and-forth arc.
- The arm and blade are the **paintbrushes** that physically sweep water away.
- The park switch is the **obedient butler** who, when the driver turns off the switch, gently guides the brush back to its cradle so it doesn't stop in the middle of the windshield (which would obstruct the driver's view).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Standard:** Wiper sweep angle is typically **110° to 130°** on the driver side and synchronized on the passenger side. Standard blade lengths vary from **350 mm to 600 mm** depending on vehicle class. The wiping frequency in **low** speed is about **45 cycles/min** and in **high** speed is about **60 cycles/min**.

---

## 1.3 Section 1 Quick-Reference Anchor

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these two systems in one module?**
> Both horns and wipers are **auxiliary electrical loads** that share three design challenges:
> 1. **High inrush current** — handled by **relays** (horn) and **gear reduction** (wiper).
> 2. **Mechanical resonance** — diaphragm in horn, linkage oscillation in wiper.
> 3. **Driver ergonomics** — single-dashboard switch for multiple functions.
> Understanding this commonality is essential for KTU 2024 Scheme integrated questions.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electromagnetic Vibrating-Reed Horn — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Constructional Components
| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Coil (Solenoid)** | Wound copper wire; produces strong magnetic flux when energized (typically **12 V DC** in cars). |
| **Diaphragm (Steel disc)** | Vibrates at the natural frequency to produce sound. |
| **Contact breaker (Platinum-tipped points)** | Interrupts the coil current at the correct instant — this is the heart of the oscillation. |
| **Resonator (Trumpet / Horn shell)** | Amplifies the diaphragm vibration to produce a directional audible note. |
| **Yoke (Iron core frame)** | Concentrates and directs the magnetic flux from coil to diaphragm center. |
| **Capacitor (Condenser, 0.1–0.5 µF)** | Suppresses the back-EMF arc at contact-breaker opening, extending contact life. |
| **Horn relay (SPST 12 V, ~30–40 A)** | Allows a low-current dashboard switch to control a high-current horn circuit. |

### 2.1.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. **Driver presses horn push** — completes the **control circuit** (low current ~ 0.5 A) which energizes the horn relay coil.
2. Relay contact closes — battery **+12 V** flows through the **power circuit** (high current ~ 20–30 A) into the horn coil.
3. The coil becomes a strong **electromagnet**, pulling the steel diaphragm **toward itself** against spring tension.
4. As the diaphragm moves, it flexes and simultaneously **opens the contact-breaker** (mounted on a lever attached to the diaphragm stem).
5. With contacts open, coil current collapses, magnetic force vanishes, and the diaphragm **springs back** to its rest position.
6. The returning diaphragm **re-closes** the contact points, re-energizing the coil — and the cycle repeats.
7. This produces a mechanical oscillation at the **natural frequency $f_n$** of the diaphragm, given by the formula in the cheat sheet below.
8. The **resonator** is tuned (Helmholtz-like cavity) so its acoustic natural frequency **matches the diaphragm frequency** — yielding a loud, pure musical note.
9. The **condenser** across the points absorbs the inductive kick (back-EMF), preventing contact pitting and RFI in the radio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind the capacitor:** When an inductive coil's current is interrupted, the collapsing magnetic flux induces a high voltage $V = -L \frac{di}{dt}$. Without a capacitor, this **arc** would burn the platinum contacts within weeks.

### 2.1.3 Why a Horn Relay is Mandatory
A direct horn switch would carry **20–30 A** through the dashboard — this would require heavy wiring, produce voltage drop, and cause hot switches. The relay reduces dashboard current to **~0.3 A**, and the heavy current flows only through short battery-to-horn cables.

---

## 2.2 Wiper Mechanism — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Wiper Motor Types
| Type | Construction | Speed Control | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Permanent Magnet (PMDC) wiper motor** | Two permanent-magnet poles (Ceramic or Ferrite), three-brush commutator | Speed varied by **tap brush position** (third brush gives LOW speed, second brush gives HIGH speed) | **Most modern cars** (cost-effective, compact) |
| **Wound-field (series-parallel) wiper motor** | Field winding in series with armature | Speed varied by changing **field connection** (series → parallel gives higher speed) | Older heavy commercial vehicles |
| **Gear reduction** | Worm gear on motor shaft drives a sector gear | Reduces motor RPM (~ 3000–8000) to output shaft RPM (~ 45–70) | Integral with motor housing |

### 2.2.2 Working Principle of PMDC 3-Brush Wiper Motor
1. The PMDC motor has **three brushes**: two main brushes supply current to the armature; the **third brush** is positioned at an offset angle.
2. When current enters through the **low-speed brush**, fewer armature coils are energized → **lower speed, higher torque**.
3. When current enters through the **high-speed brush** (closer to the main brush), more coils participate → **higher speed, lower torque**.
4. The **worm and wheel gear** reduces speed from thousands of RPM to useful wiper-shaft RPM.
5. The **cam (eccentric)** on the gear hub operates the **park switch** — an SPDT switch that, in PARK mode, completes the circuit through a **separate set of brushes** to drive the motor to the rest position.
6. The **linkage** (described next) converts the output shaft rotation into the oscillating arc of the wiper arms.

### 2.2.3 Wiper Linkage Types
| Linkage Type | Mechanism | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Single lever (Tie-rod)** | Motor crank → connecting rod → single tie-rod → both arms | Compact cars (e.g., Maruti Alto) |
| **Three-bar (Pantograph)** | Two long arms + one short arm forming a four-bar linkage | Most sedans and SUVs |
| **Four-bar (Parallelogram)** | Four-bar mechanism preserves blade orientation | Heavy commercial vehicles, buses |
| **Rack-and-pinion** | Motor drives a rack; pinion on each arm shaft | Some European designs (BMW, Mercedes) |

### 2.2.4 Park Position Mechanism — The "Butler" Function
1. Driver switches off wiper.
2. The wiper switch's **park contact** is still connected to the motor through a **dedicated pair of brushes** (park brushes) that are live only when wiper is OFF.
3. The **cam-operated park switch** is closed in this state, allowing current to flow.
4. The motor continues rotating until the cam rotates to the position that **opens the park switch**.
5. The motor stops **precisely** at the rest position (lower edge of windshield) every single time.

### 2.2.5 Intermittent Wiper Operation
- A **time-delay relay** (bimetallic strip or electronic RC timer) keeps the park circuit energized for a few seconds every 5–20 seconds.
- The wiper makes one sweep, returns to park, waits, then sweeps again — ideal for light drizzle or mist.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Box** — Memorize these for KTU numericals.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of diaphragm: $k$ = stiffness (N/m), $m$ = mass (kg) | **400 – 500 Hz** for horn |
| 2 | $V_{L} = -L \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | Inductive kick (back-EMF) across horn coil at contact opening | Up to **300–500 V** spike |
| 3 | $P_{horn} = V \cdot I$ | Horn electrical power | $12 \text{ V} \times 25 \text{ A} = 300 \text{ W}$ |
| 4 | $L_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | Sound pressure level, $p_0 = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa}$ reference | **100 – 118 dB** typical horn SPL |
| 5 | $f_{wiper} = \dfrac{N_{motor}}{i \cdot G}$ | Wiper sweep frequency: $N_{motor}$ = motor RPM, $G$ = gear ratio, $i$ = linkage ratio | **0.75 – 1.0 Hz** (45–60 cycles/min) |
| 6 | $T_{output} = T_{motor} \cdot \eta \cdot G$ | Output torque at wiper shaft, $\eta$ = gear efficiency (~0.7) | $5 - 15 \text{ N·m}$ typical |
| 7 | $I_{stall} = \dfrac{V}{R_a}$ | Wiper motor stall current, $R_a$ = armature resistance | **40 – 60 A** momentary |
| 8 | $t_{park} = \dfrac{\theta_{park}}{2\pi N_{motor} \cdot 60}$ | Time for cam to rotate to park position, $\theta_{park}$ in radians | **0.3 – 0.6 s** |
| 9 | $\omega_{output} = \dfrac{2\pi N_{motor}}{60 \cdot G}$ | Wiper shaft angular speed | **5 – 8 rad/s** |
| 10 | $\eta_{total} = \eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{linkage}$ | Combined mechanical efficiency of wiper system | **0.5 – 0.7** typical |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe symbol rule (for KTU notes readability):** Wherever absolute value is used, write as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ — **never** use the raw `|` character inside a markdown table cell, as it breaks the table syntax in the rendered PDF.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Horn loudness tuning** uses Helmholtz resonator equations in modern dual-tone horns (Hertz-Hertz pair at 500 Hz and 410 Hz produces the familiar musical "la-la").
- **Wiper linkage geometry** is designed using **four-bar linkage synthesis** (Freudenstein's equation) to ensure near-linear blade tip velocity across the sweep, which gives uniform cleaning.
- Both systems are studied in the broader domain of **vehicle NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness)** engineering and **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** ergonomics.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Natural Frequency of Horn Diaphragm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-requisite:** Treat the diaphragm as a clamped circular plate, but for KTU 2024 Scheme we use the equivalent **spring-mass** idealization — this is what appears in the question bank.

### Given
- Diaphragm mass $m$ in **kg**.
- Effective spring constant $k$ in **N/m** (composite of diaphragm bending stiffness + return spring).
- Damping ratio $\zeta$ (assumed small, $\zeta \ll 1$).

### To Derive
The natural angular frequency $\omega_n$ and the audible frequency $f_n$ in Hz.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's second law to the diaphragm moving with displacement $x(t)$:**

$$m \dfrac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + c \dfrac{dx}{dt} + k x = F(t)$$

where $c$ is the damping coefficient and $F(t)$ is the electromagnetic pull force.

**Step 2 — For free undamped vibration, set $c = 0$ and $F(t) = 0$:**

$$m \dfrac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + k x = 0$$

**Step 3 — Assume harmonic solution $x(t) = X_0 \cos(\omega_n t)$:**

$$m \cdot (-\omega_n^2 X_0 \cos\omega_n t) + k \cdot X_0 \cos\omega_n t = 0$$

$$X_0 \cos\omega_n t \,(\,k - m \omega_n^{2}\,) = 0$$

For a non-trivial solution, the bracket must vanish:

$$k - m \omega_n^{2} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the natural angular frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \quad \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to audible frequency in Hz:**

$$f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \quad \text{Hz}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example (KTU-style):**
Let $m = 0.020 \text{ kg}$ (typical steel diaphragm) and $k = 1.6 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/m}$ (clamped plate equivalent).

$$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{5}}{0.020}}$$

$$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{8.0 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2828.4$$

$$f_n \approx 450.2 \text{ Hz} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{within the 400–500 Hz high-note horn range}$$

This confirms the design intuition: a stiffer diaphragm (higher $k$) raises the pitch; a heavier diaphragm (higher $m$) lowers it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Drop Across Horn Wiring (Practical)

### Given
- Battery voltage $V_b = 12 \text{ V}$
- Horn coil resistance $R_c = 0.45 \,\Omega$
- Wire resistance (positive + return) $R_w = 0.05 \,\Omega$
- Contact resistance $R_{ct} = 0.02 \,\Omega$ (at horn relay contact + horn breaker points)

### To Find
Voltage across horn coil $V_c$ and current $I$ in the power circuit.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Total series resistance:**

$$R_{total} = R_c + R_w + R_{ct}$$

$$R_{total} = 0.45 + 0.05 + 0.02 = 0.52 \,\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's law for the closed circuit:**

$$I = \dfrac{V_b}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{12}{0.52}$$

$$I \approx 23.08 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage across the horn coil (the useful voltage):**

$$V_c = I \cdot R_c = 23.08 \times 0.45$$

$$V_c \approx 10.39 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Verify energy balance:**

$$V_b \cdot I = 12 \times 23.08 = 276.9 \text{ W (input)}$$

$$V_c \cdot I = 10.39 \times 23.08 = 239.8 \text{ W (delivered to coil)}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage loss in wiring and contacts:**

$$\% \text{ loss} = \dfrac{V_b - V_c}{V_b} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.61}{12} \times 100 \approx 13.4\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** This is precisely why horn relays are placed **as close as possible to the battery** and the horn — long thin wires waste precious voltage. A 13% loss means a 2 dB drop in acoustic output, which is auditorily noticeable.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Wiper Sweep Frequency

### Given
- Motor no-load speed $N_{motor} = 6000 \text{ RPM}$
- Worm-to-wheel gear ratio $G_1 = 50 : 1$
- Linkage ratio (crank to wiper arm) $G_2 = 1 : 1$ (typical)
- Wiper cycle = 2 strokes (one back + one forth = one revolution of crank)

### To Find
Wiper sweep frequency $f_{wiper}$ in Hz and in cycles per minute.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Output shaft speed after gear reduction:**

$$N_{output} = \dfrac{N_{motor}}{G_1} = \dfrac{6000}{50} = 120 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2 — Linkage output speed (same as crank in a single-lever system):**

$$N_{linkage} = N_{output} \times G_2 = 120 \times 1 = 120 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3 — A wiper crank rotates once per complete wiper cycle (one out + one back):**

$$f_{wiper} = \dfrac{N_{linkage}}{60} = \dfrac{120}{60} = 2.0 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to cycles per minute:**

$$f_{wiper} = 2.0 \times 60 = 120 \text{ cycles/min}$$

**Step 5 — Check against KTU typical:** This is high — real wipers use larger gear ratio. Re-evaluate with $G_1 = 80$:

$$N_{output} = \dfrac{6000}{80} = 75 \text{ RPM}$$

$$f_{wiper} = \dfrac{75}{60} = 1.25 \text{ Hz} = 75 \text{ cycles/min}$$

This is close to the **standard 60 cycles/min high-speed** typical, validating the design approach.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Summary

| Parameter | Horn Mechanism | Wiper Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary energy conversion** | Electrical → Mechanical vibration → Acoustic | Electrical → Mechanical rotation → Linear sweep |
| **Power consumption** | **250 – 400 W** (intermittent) | **30 – 80 W** (continuous possible) |
| **Operating voltage** | 12 V DC | 12 V DC (24 V in trucks) |
| **Current type** | Pulsed DC at contact-breaker rate | Smooth DC, with park-switch gating |
| **Control element** | Relay (SPST) | Multi-position switch + park switch |
| **Mechanical output** | Diaphragm oscillation at $\sim 450 \text{ Hz}$ | Crank rotation at $\sim 1.25 \text{ Hz}$ |
| **Failure mode** | Stuck contact, weak sound, buzzing | Motor burnout, park failure, linkage wear |
| **Acoustic / kinematic output** | **100 – 118 dB SPL** | **110° – 130° sweep** angle |
| **EMI consideration** | Capacitor across contacts mandatory | RC snubber across motor brushes |
| **Maintenance cycle** | Every 50,000 km (contact service) | Every 20,000 km (blade replacement) |

flowchart LR

> **Reading the diagram:** The relay coil (control circuit, low current ~0.15 A) is on the left branch, while the horn coil (power circuit, high current ~25 A) is on the right branch. The **capacitor** is shown as a dotted-line snubber across the points — this is the critical anti-arc element.

## 4.4 Wiper Linkage Geometry — Three-Bar Pantograph Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Motor Crank Arm radius r1] -->|Connecting Rod L1| B[Joint P1]
    B -->|Link L2| C[Joint P2]
    B -->|Link L3| D[Joint P3 Driver Arm]
    C -->|Link L4| D
    D -->|Output link| E[Driver Wiper Arm shaft]
    B -->|Output link| F[Passenger Wiper Arm shaft]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This is the classical four-bar pantograph. The motor crank $A$ is the input driver; the connecting rod $L_1$ transmits motion to the floating link $B$–$D$. The two output links (one to each wiper arm) are mechanically phased so that both arms sweep in **opposite directions** (a standard requirement for tandem wipers), with optional **opposed phase** (arms move toward each other) for some European cars.

The **park switch** loops back to the switch — a unique feedback path not present in the horn branch. This kind of "system-of-systems" thinking is what KTU 2024 Scheme Module-3 integrated questions test.
