# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
**"With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of an electromagnetic horn used in automobiles. State the function of the capacitor connected across the contact points."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Working (2 marks):** The electromagnetic horn consists of a steel diaphragm, a field coil, a contact breaker, and a resonator. When the horn push is pressed, the relay closes and current flows through the coil, creating a magnetic field that attracts the diaphragm. The diaphragm's movement opens the contact breaker, cutting off the current. The diaphragm then springs back, re-closing the contacts, and the cycle repeats at the diaphragm's natural frequency. The resonator amplifies this oscillation into a loud audible tone.
2. **Capacitor function (1 mark):** The capacitor (typically 0.1 – 0.5 µF) connected across the contact breaker absorbs the **back-EMF** generated when the inductive coil's current is suddenly interrupted. This prevents arcing at the platinum points, extends contact life, and suppresses radio frequency interference (RFI).

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**"What is the function of a park switch in a wiper mechanism? Explain its operation."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Function (1.5 marks):** The park switch ensures that when the driver switches off the wiper, the blades **continue moving** until they reach the rest position at the bottom edge of the windshield — they never stop in the middle of the driver's field of view.
2. **Operation (1.5 marks):** The park switch is an **SPDT (Single Pole Double Throw)** switch mechanically operated by a **cam on the gear output shaft**. When the wiper switch is OFF, the park switch routes battery current through a **dedicated set of low-speed brushes** to keep the motor running. The motor turns the cam; once the cam reaches the park position, the switch **opens**, disconnecting the motor at the exact moment the blades reach their rest position. The motor then halts precisely where intended.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question B1 (Choice A) — **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
**"With the aid of a circuit diagram, explain the construction and working of an automobile horn with a relay. Also explain why a relay is essential in the horn circuit. Compare the electromagnetic horn with an air horn."**

**Sub-parts:**
- **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the construction, working, and necessity of a horn relay with a neat circuit diagram.
- **(b) [7 marks)** Compare electromagnetic (vibrating-reed) horn with air horn in a tabular form. State typical horn loudness standards in dB.

#### Model Answer

**Part (a) — Construction, Working, and Horn Relay Necessity [7 marks]**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The electromagnetic horn consists of the following:
- A **steel diaphragm** (0.6 – 0.8 mm thick) clamped at its periphery.
- A **field coil** wound on a bobbin, typically of resistance 0.4 – 0.5 Ω for a 12 V, 25 A horn.
- A **contact breaker** with platinum-tipped points, mounted on a lever attached to the diaphragm stem.
- A **return spring** that biases the diaphragm to its rest position.
- An **adjusting screw** to set the contact-point gap (0.3 – 0.5 mm).
- A **resonator (trumpet shell)** that amplifies the diaphragm's vibration.
- A **suppression capacitor** (0.1 – 0.5 µF) across the points.
- A **horn relay** — a 12 V SPST relay with ~ 80 Ω coil resistance, capable of switching **30 – 40 A** in the power contacts.

**Working (2 marks):**
1. The driver presses the horn push → a low current (~0.15 A) flows through the relay coil.
2. The relay's high-current contact closes, applying **+12 V** to the horn coil through the power cable.
3. The coil's strong magnetic field pulls the steel diaphragm toward the yoke.
4. This movement **opens the contact-breaker points**, breaking the horn-coil circuit.
5. The diaphragm springs back, **re-closing** the points — and the cycle repeats at the diaphragm's natural frequency.
6. The **capacitor** suppresses the inductive arc; the **resonator** amplifies the resulting sound to 100 – 118 dB SPL at 2 m.

**Necessity of the Horn Relay (2 marks):**
- A 25 A current would otherwise flow through the dashboard horn switch and its thin wiring, causing **excessive voltage drop** and **fire hazard** from hot switches.
- A relay allows the dashboard switch to handle only **~0.15 A** (the relay coil current), making the switch **light, safe, and reliable**.
- The relay can be mounted close to the battery, minimizing voltage drop in the high-current horn circuit.

**Part (b) — Comparison: Electromagnetic vs Air Horn [7 marks]**

| Parameter | Electromagnetic (Vibrating-Reed) Horn | Air Horn |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy source** | Electrical (12 V battery) | Compressed air from engine or compressor |
| **Sound generator** | Vibrating steel diaphragm | Air column vibrating in a tuned pipe |
| **Frequency** | 300 – 500 Hz (musical note) | 200 – 400 Hz (deeper, louder) |
| **Sound pressure level** | 100 – 118 dB at 2 m | 110 – 130 dB at 2 m |
| **Power consumption** | 250 – 400 W electrical | 0.5 – 1.5 HP mechanical (air-driven) |
| **Weight** | Light (1 – 2 kg) | Heavy (3 – 6 kg) due to compressor |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Reliability** | High; long life with proper contact service | Moderate; compressor wear, valve maintenance |
| **Application** | Standard road cars, two-wheelers | Trucks, buses, locomotives, ships |
| **Response time** | < 30 ms (electrical) | 200 – 500 ms (compressor build-up) |
| **Tone** | Steady musical note | "Musical chord" or siren-like |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B1):**
> Students frequently lose 2–3 marks on the following:
> 1. Forgetting to **draw the capacitor** in the circuit diagram — a 1-mark loss.
> 2. Writing only "to reduce sparking" for the capacitor — the KTU key requires mentioning **back-EMF suppression, contact life extension, AND RFI suppression** — write all three.
> 3. Confusing **horn relay coil resistance** (80 Ω, low current) with **horn coil resistance** (0.4 – 0.5 Ω, high current). These are **two different coils** in **two different circuits**.
> 4. Forgetting to mention **Helmholtz resonance** for the trumpet shell — write "the resonator is tuned to match the diaphragm natural frequency for maximum acoustic amplification."

---

### Question B2 (Choice B) — **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
**"Explain the construction and working of a permanent-magnet DC wiper motor with a third brush. With sketches, describe the wiper linkage mechanisms and the park-position switch operation."**

**Sub-parts:**
- **(a) [7 marks]** Construction and working of a PMDC wiper motor with three brushes. Explain how two-speed operation is achieved.
- **(b) [7 marks]** With sketches, explain the **three-bar pantograph** wiper linkage and the **cam-operated park switch** mechanism.

#### Model Answer

**Part (a) — PMDC Wiper Motor with Three Brushes [7 marks]**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A PMDC wiper motor consists of:
- **Two permanent magnets** (ceramic ferrite) providing the field flux — N and S poles.
- A **3-brush commutator and armature** with the brushes positioned at:
  - **B1 and B2**: Main brushes supplying current to the armature through the high-speed and low-speed paths.
  - **B3 (third brush)**: Placed at an offset angle from B2. This is the **low-speed brush**.
- A **worm gear on the motor shaft** that drives a worm wheel (reduction ratio 50:1 to 80:1).
- An **output shaft with crank** that drives the linkage.
- A **cam on the worm wheel** for the park switch.

**Working — Two-Speed Operation (4 marks):**
1. When the wiper switch is in **LOW** position, current enters through the **third brush (B3)** which is offset from the main brush. Only a **portion of the armature windings** between B3 and the nearest main brush carry current. The reduced effective number of conductors lowers the back-EMF, allowing **higher current** to flow — but the **torque per turn is higher** and the **speed is lower**. Result: low-speed wiping with strong torque (suitable for heavy rain, snow).
2. When the wiper switch is in **HIGH** position, current enters through **B1** which is closer to the main brush B2. The **effective winding span is larger**, giving higher back-EMF, **lower current**, **lower torque per turn**, and **higher speed**. Result: high-speed wiping for light rain.
3. In both cases, the **armature reaction flux** shifts the magnetic neutral axis, which actually **assists speed regulation** without any external governor.

**Mathematical Insight (1 mark):**
$$N \propto \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi}$$
where $\Phi$ is the flux per pole. Since $\Phi$ is constant (permanent magnet), the speed depends on the **effective voltage drop** across the active armature coils. The third brush changes the effective number of active coils, thereby changing the speed.

**Part (b) — Linkage and Park Switch [7 marks]**

**Three-Bar Pantograph Linkage (3.5 marks):**
The three-bar pantograph linkage consists of:
- **Crank arm** (radius $r$, attached to motor output shaft).
- **Connecting rod** (length $L_1$).
- **Two long arms** (length $L_2$ and $L_3$) — these form the four-bar mechanism with the **chassis ground** as the fixed fourth link.
- **Output arms** (length $L_4$) attached to the wiper spindle shafts.

**Operation:** As the crank rotates, the connecting rod pulls/pushes the floating link, which in turn rotates both wiper spindles in **opposite directions** (or same direction, depending on design). The four-bar geometry is designed so that the **blade tip velocity remains nearly uniform** across the sweep — this is verified using **Freudenstein's equation**:

$$\dfrac{L_2}{L_1} \cdot \cos\beta - \cos\alpha - \dfrac{L_3}{L_1} = -\dfrac{L_2}{L_1} \cdot \cos(\alpha - \beta)$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the angles of the two long arms. Solving this equation for the geometry gives the optimal link lengths.

**Park Switch Mechanism (3.5 marks):**
1. The **cam** is a lobe-shaped projection on the worm wheel.
2. A **spring-loaded lever** follows the cam profile.
3. At the start of each wiper cycle (blades at rest), the cam is in a position that **closes the park switch**.
4. When the driver switches off, the wiper switch's **park contact** is still active, providing 12 V through the cam-operated switch to the **low-speed brush path**.
5. The motor continues to drive the cam; as soon as the cam rotates to the **rest-angle position**, the lever **opens the park switch**, disconnecting power to the motor.
6. The wiper blades are now precisely at the lower edge of the windshield.
7. If the wiper is switched **ON** mid-cycle, the multi-position switch supplies power directly through the operating brushes, bypassing the park circuit entirely.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B2):**
> 1. Students often **swap the function of the third brush** — remember: third brush = **LOW speed**, main brush = **HIGH speed**. Writing this wrong is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 2. Don't confuse the **park switch** (cam-operated, on the motor) with the **wiper switch** (driver-operated, on the dashboard). They are **two different switches** in series.
> 3. For the pantograph, you must **identify all four bars** of the four-bar linkage — many students only list three and lose 1 mark.
> 4. Always mention **Freudenstein's equation** (or any four-bar synthesis equation) in linkage design — it is a **favourite KTU question** element.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Horn and Wiper Mechanisms**

- [ ] **Horn types**: Vibrating-reed (electromagnetic) and air horn — know both.
- [ ] **Electromagnetic horn** core loop: **Coil energizes → Diaphragm pulls → Points open → Coil de-energizes → Diaphragm releases → Points re-close** — this is a **self-excited oscillation**.
- [ ] **Diaphragm frequency formula**: $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ — memorize with units.
- [ ] **Capacitor across points** is for **back-EMF, contact life, and RFI suppression** — all three reasons.
- [ ] **Horn relay** reduces dashboard switch current from ~25 A to ~0.15 A — safety + reliability.
- [ ] **Horn SPL standard**: **100 – 118 dB at 2 m** for road cars.
- [ ] **Dual-tone horns**: High note (~500 Hz) and low note (~410 Hz) produce a musical pair.
- [ ] **Wiper motor types**: **PMDC** (modern) and **wound-field** (older); PMDC uses **third brush** for two-speed.
- [ ] **Third brush = LOW speed, main brush = HIGH speed** — commonest trap in KTU papers.
- [ ] **Wiper linkage types**: Single-lever, **three-bar pantograph (most common)**, four-bar parallelogram, rack-and-pinion.
- [ ] **Park switch** is an **SPDT cam-operated switch** that completes the circuit only when blades need to return to rest.
- [ ] **Wiper gear ratio**: 50:1 to 80:1 worm-and-wheel; output shaft at 75 – 120 RPM.
- [ ] **Wiper sweep angle**: 110° – 130° (tandem wipers move in opposite directions).
- [ ] **Intermittent wiper** uses a **bimetallic timer or RC circuit** to give a 5 – 20 s delay.
- [ ] **Washer system** uses a separate electric pump, often linked to the wiper switch for combined operation.
- [ ] **Failure modes** to remember: Horn stuck contact, weak sound, buzzing (capacitor failure); Wiper motor burnout, park failure, linkage wear.
- [ ] **Freudenstein's equation** for four-bar linkage synthesis is a **high-yield** KTU concept.
- [ ] **Helmholtz resonance** concept for horn trumpet amplification.
- [ ] **Maintenance intervals**: Horn contact service at **50,000 km**; Wiper blade replacement at **20,000 km** or 6 months.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Scheme Tip:** When answering integrated questions that combine horn + wiper, **always draw the fuse box** at the upstream of both branches. This shows the examiner you understand the **system-level electrical architecture** — a 2024 Scheme OBE (Outcome-Based Education) focus area.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrumentation: Speedometer, Fuel Level Indicator, Oil Pressure and Coolant Temperature Indicators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT501 - AUTOMOBILE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Instrumentation: Speedometer, Fuel Level Indicator, Oil Pressure and Coolant Temperature Indicators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Automotive Instrumentation — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Instrumentation** refers to the integrated cluster of sensors, transducers, signal conditioning circuits, and display units (gauges/warning lamps) that continuously monitor, measure, and communicate critical vehicle operating parameters to the driver. In the KTU 2024 PCAUT501 syllabus, the four primary analog/digital indicators covered in Module 3 are the **Speedometer**, **Fuel Level Indicator**, **Oil Pressure Indicator**, and **Coolant Temperature Indicator**.

Each instrument consists of three functional blocks:
1. **Sensor / Transducer** — converts a physical quantity (rotational speed, fluid level, pressure, temperature) into an electrical signal.
2. **Signal Processor / Driver** — conditions and scales the signal (resistive network, comparator, or microcontroller).
3. **Display Unit** — presents the value to the driver via an analog needle gauge, stepper-motor driven dial, or LED/LCD warning lamp.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cockpit Dashboard"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine an aircraft cockpit. The pilot cannot look at the engine internals, so a panel of gauges translates hidden physical states (oil pressure, fuel quantity, cylinder head temperature, airspeed) into simple visual indicators the pilot can read in a glance. A car dashboard performs exactly the same function — it is the *human-machine interface (HMI)* between the driver and the otherwise inaccessible physical state of the powertrain and chassis. The **sensor** is the "nerve ending," the **gauge** is the "brain's interpretation," and the **needle/lamp** is the "spoken sentence."

## 1.3 Why These Four Instruments?

| Parameter | Why It Is Critical | Driver's Action |
|---|---|---|
| Vehicle Speed | Legal compliance, safety, gear selection | Adjust throttle / brake |
| Fuel Level | Trip planning, refuelling | Visit fuel station |
| Oil Pressure | Engine lubrication integrity | Stop engine immediately if low |
| Coolant Temp | Engine overheating prevention | Reduce load / check cooling system |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Of all the four systems, the **speedometer** and **fuel gauge** are *continuous reading* instruments (operate whenever ignition is ON), whereas the **oil pressure** and **coolant temperature** units are typically *dual-mode* — providing both a continuous gauge reading and a hard-wired **warning lamp** that triggers at a critical threshold.

## 1.4 Classification of Automotive Gauges

Based on the operating principle, automotive indicating instruments are classified as:

- **Magnetic-type (Eddy Current) gauges** — most common; use a permanent magnet and an aluminum armature (e.g., speedometer, fuel gauge, temp gauge).
- **Bimetallic-type gauges** — use thermal expansion of a bimetallic strip heated by current (older vehicles, oil pressure gauges).
- **Bourdon-tube mechanical gauges** — purely mechanical, no electrical input (some oil pressure units).
- **Stepper-motor driven gauges** — modern electronic clusters; microcontroller drives a small stepper motor that positions the needle.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Speedometer

### 2.1.1 Mechanical (Eddy-Current) Speedometer

The mechanical speedometer derives its input from the **transmission output shaft** via a flexible rotating cable (speedometer cable) turning at a speed proportional to vehicle road speed.

**Operating Principle — Step by Step:**

1. The rotating cable spins a permanent **magnet (M)** mounted on the input shaft of the speedometer head.
2. The magnet's rotating magnetic field cuts across an **aluminum cup (drag cup)** held on a pointer spindle, inducing **eddy currents** in the cup by Faraday's Law.
3. These eddy currents produce a secondary magnetic field that **opposes** the rotation (Lenz's Law), creating a torque.
4. The torque rotates the cup (and the attached needle) against the restoring force of a **hairspring**.
5. At equilibrium, the magnetic drag torque = spring restoring torque → needle deflection is **proportional to magnet speed** (and hence to vehicle speed).

**Speed-to-Cable-RPM Relationship:**

$$N_{cable} = \frac{v \cdot G_r \cdot 1000}{60 \cdot \pi \cdot r_{wheel}}$$

Where:
- $N_{cable}$ = rotational speed of speedometer cable / drive gear (RPM)
- $v$ = vehicle speed in **km/h**
- $G_r$ = final drive (axle) gear ratio
- $r_{wheel}$ = effective rolling radius of the wheel in **metres**

### 2.1.2 Electronic Speedometer

In modern vehicles, the mechanical cable is replaced by a **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)** — either:
- **Variable Reluctance (VR) type** — produces an AC sinusoidal voltage whose **frequency** is proportional to wheel speed.
- **Hall-effect type** — produces a **square-wave digital pulse train**.

The pulses are counted by the **Instrument Cluster ECU / Body Control Module (BCM)** over a fixed time window. A stepper motor then drives the needle on the dial.

$$v = \frac{f \cdot 2 \pi \cdot r_{wheel}}{N_{teeth} \cdot 3.6}$$

Where:
- $f$ = pulse frequency in **Hz**
- $N_{teeth}$ = number of teeth on the tone ring / reluctor wheel
- $v$ = vehicle speed in **km/h**

### 2.1.3 The Odometer

The odometer is mechanically or electronically coupled to the speedometer mechanism. It integrates the instantaneous speed over time to display cumulative distance:

$$D = \int_{0}^{t} v(\tau) \, d\tau$$

In electronic clusters, a counter register accumulates the pulses from the VSS.

---

## 2.2 The Fuel Level Indicator

### 2.2.1 Components

1. **Float** — a hollow plastic float that rides on the fuel surface inside the tank.
2. **Variable Resistor (Rheostat / Potentiometer)** — mechanically linked to the float arm; resistance changes as the float moves.
3. **Fuel Gauge (Moving-Iron / Magnetic type)** — pointer deflection proportional to current.

### 2.2.2 Circuit Operation (Wheatstone Bridge Configuration)

The sender unit (variable resistor $R_s$) and the gauge coils form a **Wheatstone bridge** with two fixed resistors and two gauge windings (L1 and L2) wound in **opposition** on a common armature.

When the float is **full** → $R_s$ is **minimum** → most current flows through coil L1 (the "FULL" coil) → pointer deflects to the right (Full mark).

When the float is **empty** → $R_s$ is **maximum** → most current flows through coil L2 (the "EMPTY" coil) → pointer deflects to the left (Empty mark).

### 2.2.3 Gauge Resistance Formulation

For a balanced magnetic gauge, the deflection angle $\theta$ is approximately:

$$\theta \approx K \cdot \frac{R_g}{R_s + R_g}$$

Where:
- $R_g$ = gauge internal resistance (constant)
- $R_s$ = sender unit resistance (variable with float position)
- $K$ = gauge design constant

---

## 2.3 The Oil Pressure Indicator

Two variants exist:

### 2.3.1 Warning Lamp Type (Most Common)

- An **oil pressure switch** (spring-loaded diaphragm + contacts) is screwed into the engine block.
- Below a critical pressure (typically **< 0.3 bar / 30 kPa** at idle), the contacts are **closed** → the warning lamp circuit is completed via ground → lamp illuminates.
- Above the threshold, oil pressure pushes the diaphragm open, breaking the contact → lamp extinguishes.

### 2.3.2 Gauge Type (Bourdon Tube / Electrical)

- Uses a **Bourdon tube** or a **piezoresistive / variable reluctance pressure sensor**.
- For an electrical gauge, the sensor's resistance varies linearly with pressure, and the gauge deflection is:

$$\theta_{gauge} = K_p \cdot P_{oil}$$

Where $P_{oil}$ is the engine oil pressure in **bar** or **psi**, and $K_p$ is the gauge sensitivity.

---

## 2.4 The Coolant Temperature Indicator

### 2.4.1 Sensor — Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) Thermistor

The temperature sensor is an **NTC thermistor** immersed in the coolant. Its resistance **decreases** as temperature rises:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

Where:
- $R_T$ = resistance at temperature $T$ (in **Kelvin**)
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$
- $B$ = Beta constant of the thermistor material (typically **3000–5000 K**)

### 2.4.2 Gauge Response

- At **cold** start, $R_T$ is high → low current → pointer stays at "C" (Cold).
- As engine warms, $R_T$ drops → current through the "HOT" coil increases → pointer moves toward "H" (Hot).
- If temperature exceeds a critical value (typically **105–115 °C**), a separate high-temperature switch closes the warning lamp circuit.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet — All Key Formulas for This Module**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vehicle speed from cable RPM | $N_{cable} = \dfrac{v \cdot G_r \cdot 1000}{60 \cdot \pi \cdot r_{wheel}}$ | $v$ in km/h, $r$ in m | Mechanical speedometer |
| 2 | Vehicle speed from VSS frequency | $v = \dfrac{f \cdot 2 \pi \cdot r_{wheel}}{N_{teeth} \cdot 3.6}$ | $f$ in Hz, $v$ in km/h | Electronic speedometer |
| 3 | Odometer distance (discrete) | $D = \sum v_i \cdot \Delta t$ | D in km | Numerical integration |
| 4 | Eddy current torque | $T_e \propto \omega_m \cdot B^2$ | $T_e$ in N·m, $\omega_m$ in rad/s | Speedometer cup drag |
| 5 | NTC thermistor resistance | $R_T = R_0 \, e^{B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$ | $T$ in K, $B$ in K | Coolant temperature sensor |
| 6 | Fuel gauge deflection | $\theta \approx K \cdot \dfrac{R_g}{R_s + R_g}$ | $R_s$ variable | Wheatstone bridge gauge |
| 7 | Oil pressure warning threshold | $P_{th} \approx 0.3$ **bar** (idle) | Pressure | Diaphragm switch |
| 8 | Magnet speed vs. vehicle speed | $\omega_m = \dfrac{v}{r_{wheel}} \cdot G_r$ | rad/s | Cable drive ratio |

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of These Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Applications Beyond Passenger Cars:**
> - **Commercial Trucks / Buses:** Electronic speedometers with **VSS + ECU** for **fleet management, tachograph logging (mandatory in EU under ECE R39)**.
> - **Motorcycles:** Mechanical cable-driven speedometers are still common due to simplicity and cost.
> - **Off-road / Defence Vehicles:** Bourdon-tube pressure gauges used as backup to electronic units (electromagnetic pulse immunity).
> - **EVs (Electric Vehicles):** Since there is no transmission, speed is derived directly from the **motor encoder** and wheel slip estimation is used for safety.
> - **ADAS Integration:** Modern speedometer signals feed **ABS, ESC, Adaptive Cruise Control, and ISOFIX** subsystems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vehicle Speed from VSS Pulse Frequency

**Given:**
- VSS tone ring with $N_{teeth} = 60$ teeth
- Wheel effective rolling radius $r_{wheel} = 0.32$ m
- Measured pulse frequency $f = 480$ Hz

**Find:** Vehicle speed $v$ in **km/h**.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Each complete revolution of the tone ring produces $N_{teeth}$ electrical pulses. So the **rotational frequency** of the wheel is:

$$f_{wheel} = \frac{f}{N_{teeth}} = \frac{480}{60} = 8 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2:** Convert rotational frequency to angular velocity:

$$\omega = 2 \pi \cdot f_{wheel} = 2 \pi \cdot 8 = 16 \pi \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** Linear velocity at the tire contact patch:

$$v_{m/s} = \omega \cdot r_{wheel} = 16 \pi \cdot 0.32 = 5.12 \pi \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_{m/s} \approx 5.12 \cdot 3.14159 \approx 16.085 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to **km/h** using the factor $3.6$ (since 1 m/s = 3.6 km/h):

$$v_{km/h} = v_{m/s} \cdot 3.6 = 16.085 \cdot 3.6 \approx 57.9 \text{ km/h}$$

**Result:** $v \approx 57.9$ **km/h**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Speedometer Cable Speed for a Given Vehicle Speed

**Given:**
- Vehicle speed $v = 100$ km/h
- Final drive gear ratio $G_r = 4.1$
- Wheel rolling radius $r_{wheel} = 0.30$ m

**Find:** Speedometer cable rotational speed $N_{cable}$ in **RPM**.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Write the governing equation:

$$N_{cable} = \frac{v \cdot G_r \cdot 1000}{60 \cdot \pi \cdot r_{wheel}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values:

$$N_{cable} = \frac{100 \cdot 4.1 \cdot 1000}{60 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.30}$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator:

$$100 \cdot 4.1 \cdot 1000 = 410{,}000$$

**Step 4:** Compute denominator:

$$60 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.30 = 18 \pi \approx 56.549$$

**Step 5:** Divide:

$$N_{cable} = \frac{410{,}000}{56.549} \approx 7250.7 \text{ RPM}$$

**Result:** $N_{cable} \approx 7251$ **RPM**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coolant Temperature Sensor Resistance

**Given:**
- Reference resistance $R_0 = 1000 \, \Omega$ at $T_0 = 298$ K (25 °C)
- Beta constant $B = 3950$ K
- Operating temperature $T = 363$ K (90 °C)

**Find:** Sensor resistance $R_T$ at 90 °C.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the thermistor equation:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the bracket term:

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{363} - \frac{1}{298}$$

$$\frac{1}{363} \approx 2.755 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{298} \approx 3.356 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \approx (2.755 - 3.356) \times 10^{-3} = -0.601 \times 10^{-3} = -6.01 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by Beta:

$$B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) = 3950 \cdot (-6.01 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.374$$

**Step 4:** Exponentiate:

$$e^{-2.374} \approx 0.0931$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by $R_0$:

$$R_T = 1000 \cdot 0.0931 \approx 93.1 \, \Omega$$

**Result:** $R_T \approx 93 \, \Omega$ at 90 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The resistance dropped by a factor of ~10x as temperature rose by 65 °C — typical NTC behavior. This dramatic change is exploited to drive the gauge from "C" to "H" reliably.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Four Instrumentation Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Comparison Table for KTU Exam:**

| Feature | Speedometer | Fuel Level Indicator | Oil Pressure Indicator | Coolant Temp. Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Sensor** | VSS (Hall / VR) or cable | Float + rheostat | Pressure switch / sensor | NTC thermistor |
| **Physical Quantity** | Rotational speed (rad/s) | Fluid level (mm) | Pressure (bar) | Temperature (°C) |
| **Sensor Output** | Frequency / pulse train | Variable resistance | Switch contact / voltage | Variable resistance |
| **Display Type** | Dial + odometer | Dial (E to F) | Warning lamp + optional dial | Dial (C to H) + warning lamp |
| **Driver Action** | Read continuously | Read continuously | Immediate if lamp ON | Immediate if lamp ON |
| **Critical Threshold** | Legal limits (varies) | 1/4 tank (warning) | **< 0.3 bar at idle** | **> 105–115 °C** |
| **Failure Mode** | Frozen needle / blank display | Stuck at E or F | False warning | False warning / no response |
| **Operating Principle** | Eddy current / stepper | Wheatstone bridge | Diaphragm switch | Magnetic gauge + NTC |
| **Modern Variant** | ECU + stepper motor | ECU + stepper motor | ECU + warning lamp | ECU + stepper motor |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the Instrument Cluster

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sensors["SENSOR LAYER - Vehicle Subsystems"]
        VSS1["VSS1\nVehicle Speed Sensor"]
        FLS["FLS1\nFloat + Rheostat"]
        OPS["OPS1\nOil Pressure Switch"]
        CTS["CTS1\nNTC Thermistor"]
    end

    subgraph ECU["SIGNAL PROCESSING - BCM or Cluster ECU"]
        MCU1["MCU1\nMicrocontroller"]
        ADC1["ADC1\nAnalog to Digital Converter"]
        CTR1["CTR1\nPulse Counter"]
    end

    subgraph Display["DISPLAY LAYER - Driver Interface"]
        SPD["SPD1\nSpeedometer Stepper"]
        FGD["FGD1\nFuel Gauge Dial"]
        OPL["OPL1\nOil Pressure Lamp"]
        CTL["CTL1\nTemp Gauge + Lamp"]
    end

    VSS1 -->|Pulse Train| CTR1
    FLS -->|Variable Resistance| ADC1
    OPS -->|Switch Ground| MCU1
    CTS -->|Variable Resistance| ADC1

    CTR1 --> MCU1
    ADC1 --> MCU1

    MCU1 -->|Stepper Pulses| SPD
    MCU1 -->|Stepper Pulses| FGD
    MCU1 -->|Lamp Driver| OPL
    MCU1 -->|Stepper + Lamp| CTL

    style Sensors fill:#e1f0ff
    style ECU fill:#fff4cc
    style Display fill:#d8f5d0
```

---

## 4.2 Mechanical Speedometer — Internal Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT - From Transmission"]
        SHAFT["SHAFT1\nDrive Cable Shaft"]
        MAG["MAG1\nPermanent Magnet"]
    end

    subgraph ASSEMBLY["SPEEDOMETER HEAD"]
        CUP["CUP1\nAluminum Drag Cup"]
        NEEDLE["NDL1\nPointer Needle"]
        SPRING["SPR1\nHairspring"]
        ODOM["ODO1\nOdometer Gears"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT - To Driver"]
        DIAL["DIAL1\nCalibrated Scale"]
        DIST["DIST1\nDistance Counter"]
    end

    SHAFT --> MAG
    MAG -->|Rotating B Field| CUP
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Explain the operating principle of a magnetic-type (eddy current) fuel level indicator with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

The magnetic-type fuel level indicator works on the **Wheatstone bridge principle** and uses a **permanent-magnet moving-coil** mechanism. It consists of:

- **Sender Unit:** A float rides on the fuel surface, connected via a lever arm to a **variable resistor (rheostat)**. As the float moves up with rising fuel level, the wiper on the rheostat decreases its effective resistance. Conversely, as fuel drops, resistance increases.

- **Gauge Unit:** A soft iron armature is mounted on a spindle carrying the pointer. The armature sits inside two coils — **L1 (FULL coil)** and **L2 (EMPTY coil)** — wound in **magnetic opposition** on a common yoke with a permanent magnet.

**Operation:** When fuel is full, $R_s$ (sender resistance) is minimum. Maximum current flows through L1, creating a strong magnetic field that pulls the armature (and pointer) toward the "FULL" position. As fuel drops, $R_s$ increases, diverting more current into L2, and the pointer deflects toward "EMPTY." The opposing magnetic torques of the two coils set the pointer position based on the bridge balance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Students often say "L1 and L2 attract the armature" — they actually produce **opposing torques**; the *net* deflecting torque positions the pointer.
> 2. Many forget to mention the **hairspring** that provides the restoring force.
> 3. Skipping the **Wheatstone bridge** connection is a 1-mark deduction.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Differentiate between a mechanical speedometer and an electronic speedometer.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Mechanical Speedometer | Electronic Speedometer |
|---|---|---|
| **Input source** | Rotating flexible cable from transmission | Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS) — Hall or VR type |
| **Signal type** | Rotational mechanical input | Electrical pulse train (frequency proportional to speed) |
| **Working principle** | Eddy current drag on aluminum cup | ECU counts pulses, drives stepper motor |
| **Indication mechanism** | Magnetic drag torque vs. hairspring torque | Stepper motor positions needle |
| **Odometer** | Geared mechanical counter | Electronic counter / EEPROM-stored value |
| **Accuracy** | Limited by cable wear and friction | High, software-calibrated |
| **Drivetrain dependency** | Direct mechanical link to gearbox output | Software-configurable for different tire sizes |
| **Failure mode** | Cable breakage, frozen needle | Sensor failure, ECU failure |
| **Used in** | Older vehicles, motorcycles, economy cars | All modern vehicles, EVs, hybrids |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

#### (a) **With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an electronic speedometer used in modern automobiles. Show how vehicle speed is computed from the VSS pulse frequency. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** A modern electronic speedometer consists of the following major subassemblies:

1. **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS):** Mounted on the transmission housing or wheel hub. It detects the passage of teeth on a **tone ring (reluctor wheel)**.
   - **Variable Reluctance type:** A permanent magnet with a pickup coil. As teeth pass the magnet, the magnetic flux changes, inducing an **AC sinusoidal voltage** in the coil. Frequency ∝ wheel rotational speed.
   - **Hall-effect type:** A semiconductor Hall element detects magnetic field changes from a magnetized tone ring, producing a **clean digital square wave** with **50% duty cycle**.

2. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU) / Body Control Module (BCM):** Receives the VSS pulses via a dedicated input pin. An on-chip **timer/counter** measures pulse frequency over a sampling window. The ECU firmware applies the calibration formula.

3. **Stepper Motor Indicator:** A small permanent-magnet stepper motor (typically 200 steps/rev) drives the needle. The ECU sends a sequence of coil-energizing pulses to step the motor to the required angular position.

4. **LCD / TFT Display (in fully digital clusters):** Replaces the stepper motor; the speed value is rendered as a digital number or animated bar.

**Working — Step by Step:**

1. As the vehicle moves, the tone ring rotates with the wheel/transmission output.
2. Teeth passing the VSS generate pulses — typically **60 pulses per revolution** (varies by manufacturer).
3. The ECU counts these pulses within a fixed time window (e.g., 100 ms) to compute frequency $f$.
4. Wheel rotational speed: $f_{wheel} = f / N_{teeth}$
5. Vehicle speed: $v = 2 \pi \cdot f_{wheel} \cdot r_{wheel} \cdot 3.6$ km/h
6. The ECU generates appropriate stepper pulse sequences to position the needle accurately.

**Advantages over mechanical speedometers:**
- No cable → no friction, no wear, no calibration drift.
- Tolerant of different tire sizes (reprogrammable).
- Easily interfaces with **ABS, ESC, GPS, ADAS** subsystems.
- Higher accuracy and reliability.

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 — the VSS pulses flow to CTR1 (counter) → MCU1 → SPD1 (stepper).

---

#### (b) **Explain with diagrams the construction and operation of an oil pressure warning system. Discuss the threshold pressure setting and the consequences of system failure. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** The oil pressure warning system consists of:

1. **Oil Pressure Switch:** A compact unit screwed into the engine's main oil gallery. It contains:
   - A **diaphragm** exposed to engine oil pressure on one side.
   - A **movable electrical contact** on the diaphragm.
   - A **fixed contact** at the unit body (connected to ground via threads).
   - A **calibrated return spring** that holds the contacts closed when pressure is low.
   - A **terminal** for the warning lamp wire.

2. **Warning Lamp:** A 12 V / 1.2 W to 2 W filament bulb in the instrument cluster, color-coded **red**.

3. **Wiring:** Lamp connected in series between the **ignition switch (12 V)** and the pressure switch terminal. With switch contacts closed, the lamp finds its ground path through the closed contacts.

**Operation:**

- **Engine OFF, Ignition ON:** Oil pressure = 0, contacts closed (spring holds them together) → lamp glows → serves as a **bulb-check** feature.
- **Engine cranking / starting:** Pressure rising, but until it crosses the threshold (typically **0.3–0.5 bar**), lamp continues to glow.
- **Normal running:** Oil pressure > 0.5 bar pushes the diaphragm against the spring, opening the contacts → lamp extinguishes.
- **Loss of oil pressure (e.g., pump failure, leak):** Pressure drops below threshold, spring pushes diaphragm back, contacts re-close → **lamp glows → driver alerted → engine must be stopped immediately** to prevent seizure.

**Threshold Setting Rationale:** The threshold of 0.3 bar at idle ensures the lamp activates if pressure falls to a level insufficient to maintain hydrodynamic lubrication in bearings. At higher RPM, normal pressure is 2.5–4.5 bar, well above threshold.

**Failure Consequences:**
- **Lamp failed (bulb burnt out):** Driver gets no warning. **Best practice:** Cluster ECU performs a **bulb self-test at ignition ON** — if no current draw is detected, the lamp is flagged faulty.
- **Switch stuck open:** Lamp never glows even at 0 pressure → catastrophic engine damage risk.
- **Switch stuck closed:** Lamp glows continuously even at normal pressure → nuisance, but driver may ignore genuine warnings ("cry wolf" effect).

**Gauge-type variant:** Some vehicles (especially trucks) use a **Bourdon tube** or **piezoresistive sensor** with a continuous gauge showing actual pressure. Bourdon tubes translate pressure into angular displacement of a curved tube; the linkage drives the needle.

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.4 — oil gallery → diaphragm → switch contacts → warning lamp circuit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Students often skip explaining the **bulb-check function** at ignition ON (1 mark lost).
> 2. Failing to mention the **threshold pressure value** (0.3 bar) loses a mark.
> 3. Not linking failure to **engine seizure** — a critical safety consequence worth mentioning.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

#### (a) **Describe the construction and working of a coolant temperature indicator using an NTC thermistor. Derive the expression for thermistor resistance. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**

1. **NTC Thermistor Sensor:** A small bead of sintered metal-oxide semiconductor (typically manganese, nickel, or cobalt oxides) with two lead wires, encapsulated in a brass housing that screws into the engine coolant jacket (cylinder head or thermostat housing). Two terminals: one to the gauge/ECU, one to ground (through the housing threads).

2. **Gauge Unit:** A magnetic-type indicator with two coils — **HOT coil** and **COLD coil** — wound in magnetic opposition. The NTC thermistor is part of the gauge circuit, in series with one of the coils.

3. **High-Temperature Warning Switch (separate):** A bimetallic or snap-action switch that closes at approximately **105–115 °C** to illuminate the overheat warning lamp.

**Working:**

- **Cold Engine (e.g., 25 °C):** NTC resistance is high (typically **1000–3000 Ω**). Very little current flows through the HOT coil; most current flows through the COLD coil, pulling the pointer to the **"C" (Cold)** end of the dial.
- **Warming Up (e.g., 60–90 °C):** NTC resistance drops (to ~200–300 Ω). Current through the HOT coil rises, gradually overcoming the COLD coil torque. Pointer moves from "C" toward the centre of the dial.
- **Normal Operating Temperature (90–100 °C):** NTC resistance is low (~100 Ω). HOT coil dominates → pointer sits in the middle of the dial, in the "NORMAL" range.
- **Overheating (>105 °C):** NTC resistance very low (~50 Ω or less). Pointer drives hard to **"H" (Hot)**. If temperature exceeds threshold, the overheat switch closes → **warning lamp ON**.

**Derivation of Thermistor Resistance:**

The conductivity of a semiconductor depends on the number of charge carriers activated across the band gap $E_g$. The carrier density follows a Boltzmann distribution, leading to:

$$\sigma(T) = \sigma_0 \cdot e^{-E_g / (2 k_B T)}$$

Since resistance is inversely proportional to conductivity:

$$R_T \propto \frac{1}{\sigma(T)} = \frac{1}{\sigma_0} \cdot e^{+E_g / (2 k_B T)}$$

Defining the **Beta constant** $B = E_g / (2 k_B)$ and the reference resistance $R_0$ at $T_0$:

$$\boxed{R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}}$$

Where:
- $R_T$ = thermistor resistance at absolute temperature $T$ (in **K**)
- $R_0$ = thermistor resistance at reference temperature $T_0$
- $B$ = material Beta constant (typically **3000–5000 K**)
- $T, T_0$ in **Kelvin** (always convert °C → K by adding 273.15)

The negative sign in the exponent (since $T > T_0$ implies $1/T < 1/T_0$) means $R_T < R_0$ — confirming **NTC behavior**.

---

#### (b) **With a neat schematic, explain the working of a Wheatstone-bridge magnetic fuel level indicator. Show how the pointer deflection depends on sender resistance. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Schematic Description:** (See Section 4.3 diagram for the full bridge.)

The circuit is a **Wheatstone bridge** with:
- Two fixed resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$
- Two gauge coils $L_1$ and $L_2$ wound in **magnetic opposition** on a common yoke
- The **variable resistor $R_s$** in the sender unit forms the fourth arm
- Battery (12 V, switched via ignition) powers the bridge diagonally

The variable resistor is mechanically connected to the **float** in the fuel tank via a lever arm and wiper.

**Step-by-Step Working:**

1. **Float Full (Fuel = Maximum):** The float is at its highest position, the wiper on the rheostat is at the **minimum resistance** end (low $R_s$, e.g., 10 Ω).
   - More current is diverted through the $L_1$ (FULL) coil.
   - Strong magnetic flux in the $L_1$ yoke pulls the iron armature → pointer deflects to **"F"**.

2. **Float Half (Fuel = Half):** $R_s$ is at the midpoint of its range (e.g., 50 Ω).
   - The bridge is approximately balanced.
   - Currents in $L_1$ and $L_2$ are roughly equal, opposing torques cancel → pointer sits at the **mid-scale (½)**.

3. **Float Empty (Fuel = 0):** Float at lowest position, wiper at maximum resistance end (high $R_s$, e.g., 200 Ω).
   - Current through $L_1$ is severely reduced.
   - The $L_2$ (EMPTY) coil dominates, pulling the armature the other way → pointer at **"E"**.

**Mathematical Expression for Pointer Deflection:**

The pointer deflection angle $\theta$ is proportional to the **net torque** on the armature:

$$T_{net} = T_1 - T_2 = K_1 \cdot I_1^2 - K_2 \cdot I_2^2$$

Where $I_1$ and $I_2$ are currents through coils $L_1$ and $L_2$. Using mesh analysis on the bridge:

$$I_1 \approx \frac{V_{bat}}{R_1 + R_s}, \quad I_2 \approx \frac{V_{bat}}{R_2 + R_g}$$

Where $R_g$ is the gauge coil resistance. At equilibrium, the spring restoring torque $T_{spring} = K_s \cdot \theta$ balances $T_{net}$:

$$\theta = \frac{K_1 \cdot I_1^2 - K_2 \cdot I_2^2}{K_s}$$

For a symmetric gauge ($K_1 = K_2 = K$, $R_1 = R_2 = R$):

$$\theta \propto \frac{1}{(R + R_s)^2} - \frac{1}{(R + R_g)^2}$$

In the **simplified linear approximation** (commonly used in KTU exam answers):

$$\boxed{\theta \approx K \cdot \frac{R_g}{R_s + R_g}}$$

**Design Considerations:**
- Sender unit resistance is chosen to be in the **10 Ω to 200 Ω** range.
- The gauge is calibrated so that **non-linearity of the tank shape** (cylindrical vs. irregular) is compensated by a **tapered resistance card** in the sender rheostat.
- A **damping resistor** is often added across the gauge coils to prevent pointer oscillation due to fuel sloshing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Confusing **sender resistance range** (10–200 Ω) with the **gauge coil resistance** (much higher, ~50–100 Ω each).
> 2. Forgetting to mention the **damping** feature for fuel slosh.
> 3. Stating that the coils are wound in the **same** direction — they are wound in **magnetic opposition**.
> 4. Skipping the **non-linearity compensation** of irregular tank shape.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Universal Pitfalls Across All Four Instrument Topics — Read Before Exam:**
> 1. **Magnitude & units:** Always quote resistance in **Ω**, pressure in **bar** or **kPa**, temperature in **°C** (sensor) or **K** (in formulas), and speed in **km/h**.
> 2. **Threshold values to memorize:**
>    - Oil pressure warning threshold: **0.3 bar at idle**
>    - Coolant overheat threshold: **105–115 °C**
>    - NTC thermistor cold resistance: **~1–3 kΩ at 25 °C**
>    - VSS tone ring teeth (typical): **40–60 teeth**
> 3. **Always mention "bulb-check" function** when discussing warning lamps.
> 4. **Always show formula derivation**, not just the final result.
> 5. **Draw labelled diagrams** — even a rough block diagram earns 1–2 extra marks.
> 6. **Don't mix up mechanisms** — eddy current drag ≠ electromagnetic attraction; Wheatstone bridge ≠ voltage divider.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read 5 Minutes Before Exam):**

- **Speedometer — Mechanical:** Driven by transmission cable → spins a permanent magnet → eddy currents in aluminum drag cup → torque vs. hairspring → pointer. Speed formula: $N_{cable} = v G_r \cdot 1000 / (60 \pi r_{wheel})$.
- **Speedometer — Electronic:** VSS (Hall/VR) → pulse train → ECU counts → stepper motor positions needle. Speed formula: $v = 2 \pi f r_{wheel} / (N_{teeth} \cdot 3.6)$ in km/h.
- **Odometer:** Integrates speed over time; mechanical via geared counter, electronic via pulse accumulator.
- **Fuel Gauge:** Float + rheostat + Wheatstone bridge magnetic gauge. Two coils in **opposition**. Sender resistance range: **10 Ω (full) to 200 Ω (empty)**.
- **Oil Pressure Indicator:** Diaphragm switch + warning lamp. Threshold **0.3 bar** at idle. Bulb-check at ignition ON. Optional gauge with Bourdon tube.
- **Coolant Temp Indicator:** NTC thermistor in coolant. Resistance drops as T rises. $R_T = R_0 \exp[B(1/T - 1/T_0)]$. Overheat warning at **~110 °C**.
- **All four indicators** in modern vehicles are now driven by the **BCM/Instrument Cluster ECU** with **stepper motors** for analog-style needles.
- **Sensor failures** can cause false readings → must be diagnosed via **OBD-II DTCs** (Diagnostic Trouble Codes).
- **EV/HEV-specific note:** In EVs, the speedometer is driven from the **motor encoder** signal, not the transmission output, since there is no gearbox output shaft.
- **Safety-critical instruments** (oil pressure, coolant temp) have **redundant warning lamps** in addition to the gauge, as per **ECE R121** (controls and tell-tales) regulations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Display Devices: LED, LCD, VFD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT501 - AUTOMOBILE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Display Devices: LED, LCD, VFD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Automotive Display Devices

In modern automobiles, the human-machine interface (HMI) relies heavily on **display devices** that convert electrical signals into visible information for the driver. The three dominant display technologies studied in the KTU 2024 syllabus are the **LED (Light Emitting Diode)**, **LCD (Liquid Crystal Display)**, and **VFD (Vacuum Fluorescent Display)**. Each operates on a fundamentally different physical principle but serves the common purpose of communicating vehicle parameters such as speed, fuel level, engine temperature, gear position, and warning indicators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *display device* in an automobile is an electronic output transducer that converts electrical energy into a controlled, visually perceptible form (light, segment activation, or pixel illumination) to convey vehicle operating parameters to the driver in real time.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **municipality control room** for traffic management:

- **LEDs** are like individual **torch lights** held by individual operators — each one is a small, self-contained point of light that you switch on or off independently. They are bright, rugged, and best when you need sharp, single-point signals (like a brake light).
- **LCDs** are like **window blinds** in a skyscraper — the sunlight (backlight) is always there behind the glass, but tiny electric "curtains" (liquid crystals) twist to either let the light pass or block it, forming shapes or images. They are excellent for complex dashboards and screens.
- **VFDs** are like **old movie projector tubes** — a heated filament shoots electrons in a vacuum onto phosphor-coated screens, which glow when struck. They give a distinctive neon-blue glow, common in older car stereos and climate panels.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and standards are critical to remember for KTU exams:

- **Planck's Constant ($h$):** $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s**
- **Speed of Light ($c$):** $3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
- **Automotive Supply Voltage (Nominal):** **12 V DC** (with operating range of **9 V to 16 V**)
- **LED Forward Voltage Drop ($V_F$):** Typically **1.8 V to 3.5 V** depending on color
- **LCD Operating Voltage:** Typically **3 V to 5 V** (logic-level) for segment drivers
- **VFD Anode/Grid Voltage:** Typically **12 V to 25 V DC** with AC filament drive

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Display Devices — "L-V-L":**
> **L**ED = Light Emitting (active emitter)
> **V**FD = Vacuum Fluorescent (active vacuum tube)
> **L**CD = Liquid Crystal (light modulator, not emitter)

## 1.4 Automotive Application Context

| Display Type | Primary Automotive Use | Typical Location |
|---|---|---|
| **LED** | Brake lamps, turn indicators, DRL, instrument cluster backlighting, headlights | Exterior lighting, dashboard |
| **LCD** | Speedometer, tachometer, navigation, infotainment, rear-seat entertainment | Instrument cluster, center console |
| **VFD** | Older car audio displays, climate control readouts, digital clocks | Center stack (legacy vehicles) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LED — Light Emitting Diode

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

An LED is a **P-N junction diode** fabricated from direct-bandgap semiconductor materials. When forward-biased, electrons from the N-region recombine with holes in the P-region across the depletion region. The energy released during recombination is emitted as a **photon**, with wavelength determined by the bandgap energy $E_g$.

### 2.1.2 Key Theoretical Steps

1. **Forward bias applied** → electrons injected into P-side, holes into N-side.
2. **Carrier recombination** occurs near the depletion region.
3. **Energy conservation:** Recombination energy = photon energy, i.e., $E_g = h\nu$.
4. **Photon emission:** Wavelength $\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g}$.
5. **Light extraction** via transparent epoxy lens.

### 2.1.3 Why LED is Ideal for Automobiles

- **High luminous efficacy:** $\eta_{lum}$ of **80–150 lumens/Watt** (vs. 15 lm/W for incandescent).
- **Fast switching:** Turn-on time ≈ **100 ns** (critical for turn signals at 90 flashes/min).
- **Vibration resistance:** Solid-state, no filament to break.
- **Long life:** **> 50,000 hours** vs. ~1,000 hours for incandescent bulbs.
- **Low current draw:** Typical operating current **10–30 mA** per segment.

### 2.1.4 Material-Wavelength Relationship

| LED Color | Semiconductor Material | Forward Voltage $V_F$ | Wavelength (nm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Red** | GaAsP / AlGaAs | **1.8 V – 2.2 V** | 620 – 700 |
| **Orange/Yellow** | GaAsP | **2.0 V – 2.2 V** | 590 – 620 |
| **Green** | GaP / InGaN | **2.2 V – 3.0 V** | 520 – 570 |
| **Blue** | InGaN / SiC | **3.0 V – 3.5 V** | 450 – 490 |
| **White** | InGaN + Phosphor | **3.0 V – 3.5 V** | Broad spectrum |

## 2.2 LCD — Liquid Crystal Display

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

LCD is a **passive, light-modulating** device. It does **not** emit light; instead, it controls the transmission or reflection of ambient/backlight through **liquid crystal molecules** whose orientation is altered by an applied electric field.

The most common automotive variant is the **Twisted Nematic (TN) LCD** with a **transmissive backlit** configuration.

### 2.2.2 Key Theoretical Steps

1. **No voltage applied:** Liquid crystal molecules form a **90° twist** between two polarizers → light passes through → pixel appears **bright**.
2. **Voltage applied:** Electric field re-aligns molecules parallel to the field → no rotation of light polarization → light is **blocked** by the second polarizer → pixel appears **dark**.
3. **Segment addressing:** Each segment is driven by a **multiplexed AC waveform** (DC component avoided to prevent ion migration and display degradation).
4. **Backlight source:** Modern automotive LCDs use **LED backlights** (replacing CCFLs) for better efficiency and color gamut.

### 2.2.3 Types Used in Automobiles

- **Segment LCD** — used for simple digital speedometers and fuel gauges (low cost).
- **Character LCD** — alphanumeric displays (climate control temperature).
- **TFT-LCD (Active Matrix)** — used in infotainment, navigation, digital instrument clusters (high resolution, fast refresh).

### 2.2.4 Why LCD is Preferred for Instrument Clusters

- **Very low power consumption** (except backlight).
- **Excellent sunlight readability** with proper backlight design.
- **Scalable to large panels** — full dashboard displays are LCD/TFT based.
- **Operating temperature:** Typically **-30°C to +85°C** (automotive Grade 2).

## 2.3 VFD — Vacuum Fluorescent Display

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

A VFD is a **passive-matrix, vacuum-tube-based display**. It consists of three electrodes enclosed in a vacuum glass envelope:
- **Cathode (Filament):** Tungsten wire, heated to emit electrons via **thermionic emission**.
- **Grid:** A mesh electrode that controls and accelerates electrons toward the anodes.
- **Anodes:** Phosphor-coated segments (typically **ZnO:Zn** — zinc-oxide doped with zinc) that glow when struck by electrons.

### 2.3.2 Key Theoretical Steps

1. **Filament heated** to ~600–650°C → thermionic electron emission (Richardson's law).
2. **Grid energized** (positive potential) → electrons accelerated toward anodes.
3. **Anode segments** (addressed positively) are struck by electrons.
4. **Phosphor excitation:** Electrons excite phosphor atoms; relaxation emits **blue-green light** (typical peak wavelength **505–530 nm**).
5. **Non-addressed anodes** remain dark (electrons repelled by grid pulsing or anode at 0V).

### 2.3.3 Why VFD was Popular in Automobiles

- **High brightness** with excellent daylight visibility.
- **Wide viewing angle** (nearly 180°).
- **Wide operating temperature:** **-40°C to +85°C** (superior to LCD in cold weather).
- **Distinctive blue-green aesthetic** for premium feel in 1990s–2000s vehicles.
- **Disadvantage:** Higher voltage requirement (~25 V), vacuum fragility, higher power than LCD.

### 2.3.4 The Thermionic Emission Equation (Richardson's Law)

The current density of emitted electrons from the heated cathode is given by:

$$
J = A_{RT} T^2 \exp\left(-\frac{W}{k_B T}\right)
$$

Where:
- $J$ = emission current density (**A/m²**)
- $A_{RT}$ = Richardson's constant (**A·m⁻²·K⁻²**)
- $T$ = absolute temperature of the filament (**K**)
- $W$ = work function of the cathode material (**eV**)
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann's constant = **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**

> [!NOTE]
> KTU students should remember that VFDs are **gradually being phased out** of new vehicles in favor of **TFT-LCD and OLED** displays, but they remain in the syllabus for legacy automotive systems.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LED Photon Energy | $E_g = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | eV, J, m | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| 2 | LED Wavelength | $\lambda = \frac{1240}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ | nm | Direct bandgap semiconductors only |
| 3 | LED Series Resistor | $R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_F}{I_F}$ | $\Omega$ | $V_F$ = forward voltage |
| 4 | LED Luminous Efficacy | $\eta_{lum} = \frac{\Phi_v}{P_{elec}}$ | lm/W | $\Phi_v$ = luminous flux |
| 5 | LCD Threshold Voltage | $V_{th} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{K}{\varepsilon_0 \Delta \varepsilon}}$ | V | $K$ = elastic constant, $\Delta\varepsilon$ = dielectric anisotropy |
| 6 | VFD Thermionic Emission | $J = A_{RT} T^2 \exp\left(-\frac{W}{k_B T}\right)$ | A/m² | Richardson-Dushman equation |
| 7 | VFD Phosphor Wavelength | $\lambda_{peak} \approx 505$–$530$ nm | nm | Blue-green ZnO:Zn |
| 8 | Display Contrast Ratio | $CR = \frac{L_{bright}}{L_{dark}}$ | dimensionless | Higher = better readability |
| 9 | Display Response Time | $\tau_{r}, \tau_{f}$ | ms | Critical for video-rate displays |
| 10 | Automotive Voltage Range | $V_{bat} = 9$ V to 16 V | V | 12 V nominal system |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Voltage for LCD ($V_{th}$):** This is the Freedericksz transition voltage. Below it, the liquid crystal remains in its natural twisted state. Above it, the molecules begin to reorient. This voltage is **typically 1.5–3 V** for TN cells used in automobiles.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: LED Wavelength from Bandgap Energy

### Problem Context
A red automotive brake-light LED is fabricated from **AlGaAs** with a bandgap energy $E_g = 1.9$ eV. Calculate the dominant wavelength of light emitted.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the governing equation linking bandgap energy to photon wavelength.

The photon emitted during band-to-band recombination has energy equal to the bandgap:

$$
E_g = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the wavelength $\lambda$.

$$
\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values. Convert $E_g$ from eV to Joules.

$$
E_g = 1.9 \text{ eV} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV} = 3.044 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the wavelength equation.

$$
\lambda = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}) \times (3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s})}{3.044 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the numerator first.

$$
hc = (6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3 \times 10^{8}) = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J·m}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the final wavelength.

$$
\lambda = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{3.044 \times 10^{-19}} = 6.529 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 7:** Convert to nanometers.

$$
\lambda = 652.9 \text{ nm}
$$

**Step 8:** Interpretation.

$$
\lambda \approx 653 \text{ nm} \implies \text{this lies in the deep red region of the visible spectrum (620–700 nm), ideal for brake lights.}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Red brake lights with $\lambda \approx 650$ nm are preferred because **human eyes are highly sensitive to red in peripheral vision** (mesopic conditions at night), giving faster driver reaction time during emergency braking.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: LED Series Resistor Design for an Automotive Turn Indicator

### Problem Context
An amber turn-indicator LED has a forward voltage $V_F = 2.1$ V and is rated for a maximum continuous forward current $I_F = 20$ mA. The supply is the vehicle's 12 V battery. Calculate:
1. The required series current-limiting resistor.
2. The power dissipated by the resistor.
3. The total power consumed by the LED circuit.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the LED-resistor loop.

$$
V_{bat} = V_R + V_F
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the voltage across the resistor.

$$
V_R = V_{bat} - V_F = 12 - 2.1 = 9.9 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply Ohm's Law to find the resistance.

$$
R = \frac{V_R}{I_F} = \frac{9.9 \text{ V}}{20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{9.9}{0.020} = 495 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 4:** Round up to the nearest standard E12 resistor value.

$$
R_{standard} = 560 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the power dissipated by the resistor.

$$
P_R = I_F^2 \times R = (0.020)^2 \times 560 = 0.0004 \times 560 = 0.224 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 6:** Calculate the actual current with the standard resistor (sanity check).

$$
I_{actual} = \frac{9.9}{560} = 0.01768 \text{ A} \approx 17.7 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 7:** Calculate the power consumed by the LED itself.

$$
P_{LED} = V_F \times I_{actual} = 2.1 \times 0.01768 = 0.0371 \text{ W} \approx 37.1 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 8:** Calculate the total circuit power.

$$
P_{total} = P_R + P_{LED} = 0.224 + 0.037 = 0.261 \text{ W}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Compare this to an incandescent turn-indicator bulb consuming ~21 W. The LED circuit consumes only **0.26 W — a 98.8% reduction**. For four indicators flashing, this saves ~83 W, directly reducing alternator load and improving fuel economy by ~0.1 km/L.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: VFD Cathode Emission Current Density

### Problem Context
A VFD filament is heated to a temperature $T = 2500$ K. The cathode is made of tungsten (oxide-coated) with a work function $W = 1.5$ eV. The Richardson constant is $A_{RT} = 1.2 \times 10^{5}$ A·m⁻²·K⁻². Calculate the thermionic emission current density $J$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Write Richardson's equation.

$$
J = A_{RT} T^2 \exp\left(-\frac{W}{k_B T}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Convert the work function to Joules.

$$
W = 1.5 \text{ eV} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV} = 2.403 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the thermal voltage $k_B T$.

$$
k_B T = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 2500 = 3.45 \times 10^{-20} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the exponent argument.

$$
\frac{W}{k_B T} = \frac{2.403 \times 10^{-19}}{3.45 \times 10^{-20}} = 6.965
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the exponential term.

$$
\exp(-6.965) = 9.45 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute $T^2$.

$$
T^2 = (2500)^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ K}^2
$$

**Step 7:** Substitute all values.

$$
J = (1.2 \times 10^{5}) \times (6.25 \times 10^{6}) \times (9.45 \times 10^{-4})
$$

**Step 8:** Multiply stepwise.

$$
J = (1.2 \times 10^{5}) \times (6.25 \times 10^{6}) = 7.5 \times 10^{11}
$$

$$
J = 7.5 \times 10^{11} \times 9.45 \times 10^{-4} = 7.0875 \times 10^{8} \text{ A/m}^2
$$

**Step 9:** Express the result in a more readable form.

$$
J \approx 7.09 \times 10^{8} \text{ A/m}^2 = 708.75 \text{ A/cm}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This enormous current density is why VFD filaments are designed as very thin, long wires — to maximize surface area for emission and distribute heat evenly. Even so, VFDs consume more power than LCDs, which is why LCD has largely replaced VFD in modern vehicles.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance Table

| Parameter | **LED** | **LCD (TFT)** | **VFD** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Light Generation** | Active emission (electroluminescence) | Modulates backlight | Active emission (cathodoluminescence) |
| **Operating Voltage** | 1.8 – 3.5 V (logic 5V / 12V with resistor) | 3 – 5 V (logic) | 12 – 25 V (anode/grid) |
| **Luminous Efficacy** | 80 – 150 lm/W (very high) | N/A (modulator) + backlight ~30 lm/W | 5 – 10 lm/W (low) |
| **Lifespan** | 50,000 – 100,000 hrs | 30,000 – 60,000 hrs (backlight limited) | 25,000 – 50,000 hrs |
| **Operating Temp Range** | $-40°C$ to $+125°C$ | $-30°C$ to $+85°C$ | $-40°C$ to $+85°C$ |
| **Response Time** | ~100 ns | 5 – 25 ms | ~10 μs |
| **Viewing Angle** | Narrow to medium (120°) | Narrow (TN) to wide (IPS) | Very wide (~160°) |
| **Sunlight Readability** | Excellent | Good (with high-nit backlight) | Excellent |
| **Vibration Tolerance** | Excellent (solid-state) | Good (glass panel) | Poor (vacuum tube) |
| **Cost per Segment/Area** | Moderate, falling | Falling rapidly | Increasing (obsolete) |
| **Color Capability** | Full RGB, narrow spectrum | Full color via filters | Limited (blue-green typical) |
| **Power Consumption** | Very low (10–30 mA per LED) | Low (excluding backlight) | Moderate to high (filament + HV) |
| **Automotive Examples** | Brake lights, DRL, headlamps, dashboard indicators | Infotainment, digital clusters, navigation | Legacy climate controls, older audio systems |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Comparison of Display Device Operation

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional signal-flow and energy-conversion architecture** for each of the three display devices, showing how electrical input becomes a visible output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Automotive ECU or Microcontroller Signal] --> B{Display Technology Selector}

    B -->|LED Path| C1[Logic Level Driver]
    C1 --> D1[Current Limiting Resistor R]
    D1 --> E1[Forward Biased PN Junction]
    E1 --> F1[Carrier Recombination at Junction]
    F1 --> G1[Photon Emission Lambda = hc by Eg]
    G1 --> H1[Epoxy Lens Collimation]
    H1 --> I1[Direct Visible Light Output]

    B -->|LCD Path| C2[Segment or TFT Driver IC]
    C2 --> D2[AC Multiplexed Waveform]
    D2 --> E2[ITO Transparent Electrodes]
    E2 --> F2[Liquid Crystal Molecule Reorientation]
    F2 --> G2[Polarization Rotation or Blocking]
    G2 --> H2[Backlight LED Source]
    H2 --> I2[Modulated Visible Output on Screen]

    B -->|VFD Path| C3[Filament Drive Transformer]
    C3 --> D3[Heated Tungsten Cathode]
    D3 --> E3[Thermionic Electron Emission]
    E3 --> F3[Grid Acceleration Voltage 12 to 25V]
    F3 --> G3[Phosphor Coated Anode Segments]
    G3 --> H3[Cathodoluminescent Excitation]
    H3 --> I3[Blue Green Light Output 505 to 530 nm]
```

This diagram shows how a **single 7-segment digit** (commonly used in digital speedometers) is driven by an automotive microcontroller via current-limiting resistors.

```mermaid
    R1 --> SA[Segment A Top Horizontal]

    R4 --> SD[Segment D Bottom Horizontal]
    R5 --> SE[Segment E Lower Left]
    R6 --> SF[Segment F Upper Left]
    R7 --> SG[Segment G Middle Horizontal]
    RDP --> SP[Decimal Point LED]

    SA --> GND1[Common Cathode or Anode Return]

    SG --> GND1
```

## 4.3 VFD Internal Electrode Architecture

This diagram illustrates the **internal structure of a VFD cell**, showing the spatial relationship of cathode, grid, and anodes inside the vacuum envelope.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VACC[Vacuum Envelope Glass] --> FIL[Filament Cathode Tungsten Wire]
    FIL --> HEAT[Heated to 600 to 650 degrees Celsius]
    HEAT --> EMIT[Thermionic Electron Cloud]

    GRID --> ACCEL[Accelerated by Positive Grid Voltage]
```

This Mermaid block diagram maps the **layered structure of a Twisted Nematic LCD cell**, showing how light is controlled.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    POL1 --> ITO1[Bottom ITO Electrode with Pixel Pattern]
    ITO1 --> LC1[Liquid Crystal Layer Twisted Nematic 90 degree]

```
