[ ] Jam density: ~120–150 veh/km/lane - [ ] Critical density: $\rho_j / 2$ - [ ] Capacity: ~1800 veh/hr/lane (urban), ~2200 veh/hr/lane (highway) - [ ] Latency budget: 100 ms = 30 (sense) + 20 (compute) + 20 (comm) + 30 (actuate) **Common Student Errors to Avoid** - [ ] Confusing Connected Car with Smart Car (they are *not* synonymous) - [ ] Forgetting to convert km/h → m/s in stopping distance calculations - [ ] Writing "Tesla = Autonomous" — Tesla Model 3 is Level 2, not Level 5 - [ ] Omitting V2G when listing V2X domains - [ ] Stating V2X "eliminates accidents" — it *reduces* them; physics and human factors remain. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, always (1) draw the block diagram first (2 marks), (2) state assumptions explicitly (1 mark), (3) derive step-by-step with units (5 marks), (4) provide a real-world example/commentary (2 marks), (5) conclude with limitations (1 mark). This 2-1-5-2-1 distribution mirrors KTU's valuation key pattern.

topics.title: On-Board Diagnostics: OBD, OBD II
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT501 - AUTOMOBILE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** On-Board Diagnostics: OBD, OBD II

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is On-Board Diagnostics (OBD)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OBD (On-Board Diagnostics)** is a vehicle's built-in self-diagnostic and reporting capability integrated into the Engine Control Unit (ECU) and other electronic control modules. It continuously monitors the performance of major engine, transmission, and emissions-related components to detect malfunctions, store Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs), and illuminate the **Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL)** — commonly known as the *Check Engine Light*.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of OBD as a **doctor embedded inside your car**. Just as a doctor uses sensors (stethoscope, blood test) to check your vitals, the car's ECU uses sensors (oxygen sensor, MAF sensor, crankshaft position sensor) to constantly monitor engine health. When something goes wrong, the doctor writes a prescription (a fault code like `P0301`) and alerts the patient with a flashing warning (Check Engine Light). However, you still need an external mechanic (a scan tool) to *read* that prescription in **first-generation OBD**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — OBD-I vs OBD-II:**
> - **OBD-I**: Introduced in the **1980s**, proprietary to each manufacturer. No standard connector, no standard codes.
> - **OBD-II**: Mandated in the **USA from 1996** (all cars sold in California from 1994), **EU/EOBD from 2001 (petrol) and 2004 (diesel)**, **India from 2013 (BS-IV) and 2017 (BS-VI)**. Uses a standardized **16-pin SAE J1962 connector** and uniform DTCs.

## 1.2 Why OBD-II Was Introduced — The Need for Standardization

In the OBD-I era, diagnosing a Volkswagen vs. a Toyota required two entirely different scan tools, cables, and code dictionaries. This was expensive and inefficient. The **California Air Resources Board (CARB)** and the **Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE)** collaborated to create a universal diagnostic language. The key standardization bodies and standards are:

| Standard | Governing Body | Purpose |
|----------|----------------|---------|
| **SAE J1962** | SAE | Defines the 16-pin DLC (Data Link Connector) |
| **SAE J1979** | SAE | Defines Diagnostic Test Modes (Modes $01–$0A) |
| **SAE J2012** | SAE | Defines DTC format and message structure |
| **ISO 9141-2** | ISO | 5-baud initialization serial protocol |
| **ISO 14230-4 (KWP2000)** | ISO | Keyword Protocol 2000 |
| **ISO 15765-4 (CAN)** | ISO | Controller Area Network diagnostics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized battery voltage on Pin 16: 12 V (petrol cars) / 24 V (heavy-duty diesel trucks)**.
> **OBD-II mandates continuous monitoring of emission-related components**, not just engine control.

## 1.3 OBD-II Connector (SAE J1962) — The Universal Gateway

The Data Link Connector (DLC) is the **standardized 16-pin trapezoidal port** typically located under the dashboard near the steering column. Pin layout:

| Pin | Assignment | Pin | Assignment |
|-----|------------|-----|------------|
| 2 | J1850 Bus+ (VPW/PWM) | 10 | J1850 Bus− |
| 4 | Chassis Ground | 14 | CAN Low (ISO 15765-4) |
| 5 | Signal Ground | 15 | L-Line (ISO 9141-2 / KWP) |
| 6 | CAN High (ISO 15765-4) | 16 | Battery Power (**+12 V / +24 V**) |
| 7 | K-Line (ISO 9141-2 / KWP) | — | Pins 1, 3, 8, 9, 11, 12, 13 reserved |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Since 2008, all new vehicles sold in the US must use ISO 15765-4 (CAN-bus) on pins 6 and 14 as the primary OBD-II protocol.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of OBD-II System

The OBD-II architecture follows a structured, layered logic:

- **Layer 1 — Sensor Input Stage:** Sensors (O2, MAF, MAP, TPS, CKP, CMP) feed real-time analog/digital signals to the ECU.
- **Layer 2 — ECU Processing Stage:** The ECU compares live readings against pre-calibrated **expected values** stored in lookup tables.
- **Layer 3 — Diagnostic Manager Stage:** A dedicated diagnostic routine runs in the background, executing:
  - *Continuous Monitoring* (fuel system, misfire, comprehensive components).
  - *Non-Continuous Monitoring* (catalyst efficiency, EVAP system, O2 sensor response).
- **Layer 4 — Fault Decision Stage:** If a fault is detected for a defined duration (e.g., **2 consecutive drive cycles**), a DTC is set, freeze-frame data is captured, and the MIL is commanded ON.
- **Layer 5 — External Communication Stage:** A scan tool queries the ECU via the standardized DLC using **Diagnostic Test Modes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two drive cycles?** The "2-trip" logic prevents false MIL illumination from transient sensor noise. A fault must be confirmed on a *separate, subsequent* drive cycle after the cooling-off.

## 2.2 The Five OBD-II Communication Protocols

A vehicle uses **one** of the following five protocols to communicate with a scan tool:

1. **SAE J1850 VPW (Variable Pulse Width)** — 10.4 kbps, used by GM.
2. **SAE J1850 PWM (Pulse Width Modulated)** — 41.6 kbps, used by Ford.
3. **ISO 9141-2** — 10.4 kbps, asynchronous, used by many European and Asian cars.
4. **ISO 14230-4 (KWP2000)** — 1.2 to 250 kbps, Keyword Protocol 2000.
5. **ISO 15765-4 (CAN)** — 250 kbps (high-speed) or 500 kbps, mandatory in modern cars.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The OBD-II standard defines the **transport layer and application layer** but lets the **physical layer** vary — this is why a single connector supports all five protocols.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Signal Specs

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Standard | Unit |
|-----------|--------|------------------|------|
| DLC Supply Voltage | $V_{batt}$ | $12$ (car) / $24$ (truck) | $\text{V}$ |
| CAN Bus High Voltage (dominant) | $V_{CAN-H}$ | $\approx 3.5$ | $\text{V}$ |
| CAN Bus Low Voltage (dominant) | $V_{CAN-L}$ | $\approx 1.5$ | $\text{V}$ |
| CAN Recessive Bus Voltage | $V_{CAN}$ | $\approx 2.5$ | $\text{V}$ |
| J1850 VPW Bit Rate | $f_{VPW}$ | $10.4$ | kbps |
| J1850 PWM Bit Rate | $f_{PWM}$ | $41.6$ | kbps |
| KWP2000 Fast Init Pulse | $T_{wakeup}$ | $50 \pm 5$ | ms |
| Diagnostic Data Rate (CAN) | $f_{CAN}$ | $250$ or $500$ | kbps |
| MIL Confirmation Drive Cycles | $N_{trip}$ | $2$ | cycles |

### 2.3.1 DTC Format Decoding

A standard OBD-II DTC follows the format `XYYYY`:

$$
\text{DTC} = \underbrace{X}_{\text{System}} \; \underbrace{Y_1}_{\text{Code Type}} \; \underbrace{Y_2 Y_3 Y_4}_{\text{Fault Index}}
$$

- $X$ — System identifier:
  - `P` = Powertrain (engine, transmission)
  - `B` = Body (airbags, lighting, central locking)
  - `C` = Chassis (ABS, suspension, steering)
  - `U` = Network/Communication (CAN bus, LIN bus faults)
- $Y_1$ — Code type:
  - `0` = Generic (SAE-defined, applies to all manufacturers)
  - `1` = Manufacturer-specific
  - `2,3` = SAE-defined (powertrain only)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> `P0301` = **P**owertrain, **0** generic, **301** = Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected.
> `U0100` = **U**nrecoverable communication loss with ECM/PCM.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

OBD-II is the **backbone of modern emission control, telematics, fleet management, and insurance telematics**. It enables:
- Emission testing centers to instantly read readiness monitors and stored codes.
- Workshops to perform remote diagnosis via telematics dongles (e.g., OBD-II Bluetooth adapters).
- Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) to gather over-the-air diagnostic telemetry.
- Insurance companies to assess driving behavior (UBI — Usage-Based Insurance).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CAN Bus Differential Voltage

Modern OBD-II communication over ISO 15765-4 relies on the **Controller Area Network (CAN)** physical layer, which uses a differential signaling scheme for noise immunity.

**Step 1 — Define the single-ended voltages.**
The bus has two wires, CAN-H and CAN-L, with voltages $V_H$ and $V_L$ measured with respect to signal ground.

**Step 2 — Define the differential voltage.**
The receiver interprets the bus state from the differential voltage:

$$
V_{diff} = V_H - V_L
$$

**Step 3 — Apply dominant state (recessive = 0, dominant = 1).**
During the **dominant** state (logic '0' in CAN), the bus is actively driven:
- $V_H \approx 3.5$ V (high output driver)
- $V_L \approx 1.5$ V (low output driver)

Substituting these values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{diff, dom} &= V_H - V_L \\
&= 3.5 \text{ V} - 1.5 \text{ V} \\
&= 2.0 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply recessive state.**
During the **recessive** state (logic '1'), neither driver is active, and the bus sits near the common-mode voltage:
- $V_H \approx 2.5$ V
- $V_L \approx 2.5$ V

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{diff, rec} &= V_H - V_L \\
&= 2.5 \text{ V} - 2.5 \text{ V} \\
&= 0.0 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Receiver threshold.**
The CAN receiver triggers on $|V_{diff}| \geq 0.9$ V (typical). Therefore the dominant bit is reliably distinguishable from the recessive bit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why differential signaling?** In an automotive environment with high EMI from ignition coils and alternators, a single-ended signal would be corrupted. The differential scheme ensures that any common-mode noise (e.g., from ground bounce) is rejected, since it appears equally on both wires and cancels out in $V_{diff}$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — DTC Code Family Reference

| DTC Range | Subsystem | Example | Meaning |
|-----------|-----------|---------|---------|
| P00xx | Fuel & Air Metering | P0171 | System too lean (Bank 1) |
| P01xx–P02xx | Fuel/Air/Emissions | P0128 | Coolant temp below thermostat regulating temp |
| P03xx | Ignition System | P0301 | Cylinder 1 misfire detected |
| P04xx | Auxiliary Emissions | P0420 | Catalyst system efficiency below threshold (Bank 1) |
| P05xx | Vehicle Speed, Idle, Engine | P0500 | Vehicle speed sensor malfunction |
| P06xx | Computer/Output Circuit | P0606 | ECM/PCM processor fault |
| P07xx | Transmission | P0700 | Transmission control system malfunction |
| B00xx–B03xx | Airbag / Restraints | B0028 | Side airbag deployment loop short |
| C00xx–C03xx | ABS / Chassis | C0040 | Right front wheel speed circuit malfunction |
| U00xx–U03xx | Network Communication | U0100 | Lost communication with ECM/PCM |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently test the decoding logic of `XYYYY`. Memorize the four system letters (P, B, C, U) and the first-digit convention (0 = generic, 1 = manufacturer).

## 3.3 Worked Example — DTC Decoding and Likely Cause

**Question:** A customer's OBD-II scan tool displays code `P0420`. Identify the system, code type, subsystem, and the most likely component involved.

**Solution:**

- **Step 1 — Decode 'P'**: System = **Powertrain** (engine, transmission, drivetrain).
- **Step 2 — Decode '0'**: Code type = **Generic SAE** (applies to all manufacturers).
- **Step 3 — Decode '4'**: Subsystem family = **Auxiliary Emissions Controls** (third digit of P04xx family).
- **Step 4 — Decode '20'**: Specific fault = **Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)**.

$$
\text{P0420} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Powertrain},\;\text{Generic},\;\text{Catalyst Bank 1 efficiency low}
$$

**Likely components to inspect:**
- Worn-out three-way catalytic converter.
- Downstream oxygen sensor degraded / slow response.
- Exhaust leak upstream of the rear O2 sensor.
- Engine running rich/lean (causing catalyst overstress).

**Recommended follow-up:** Read Mode $05$ (O2 sensor test) and Mode $06$ (catalyst monitor test results) to quantify the deviation from threshold.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — OBD-II Diagnostic Test Modes (SAE J1979)

| Mode (Hex) | Name | Function |
|------------|------|----------|
| `$01` | Live Data | Read current Parameter IDs (PIDs) — RPM, coolant temp, MAF, etc. |
| `$02` | Freeze Frame | Snapshot of live data at the moment a DTC triggered |
| `$03` | Read DTCs | Retrieve all stored emission-related DTCs |
| `$04` | Clear DTCs | Clear DTCs, freeze-frame, readiness monitors, and turn MIL OFF |
| `$05` | O2 Sensor Test | Test results for oxygen sensor monitoring |
| `$06` | Test Results | Non-continuous monitored system test results (mode $06$) |
| `$07` | Pending DTCs | DTCs from the *current* (first) trip — not yet MIL-confirmed |
| `$08` | Control | Bidirectional control of evaporative system, EGR, etc. |
| `$09` | Vehicle Info | VIN, calibration ID, ECU name |
| `$0A` | Permanent DTCs | Confirmed DTCs that cannot be cleared by Mode $04$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permanent DTCs (Mode $0A$)** were introduced in 2010 to prevent users from clearing codes just before emission tests. They clear only after the OBD-II system itself verifies the repair over successive drive cycles.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OBD-II System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Engine Sensors] --> B1[Engine ECU]
    A2[Transmission Sensors] --> B2[TCU]
    A3[ABS Wheel Speed Sensors] --> B3[ABS ECU]
    A4[Catalyst and O2 Sensors] --> B1
    A5[EVAP and MAF Sensors] --> B1

    B1 -->|CAN Bus| H1[Central Gateway ECU]
    B2 -->|CAN Bus| H1
    B3 -->|CAN Bus| H1
    C1[Body Control Module] -->|CAN Bus| H1

    H1 -->|ISO 15765-4| D1[SAE J1962 DLC Connector]
    D1 -->|OBD-II Cable| E1[External Scan Tool]
    D1 -->|Bluetooth or WiFi| E2[Telematics Dongle]

    B1 -->|MIL Command| F1[Instrument Cluster MIL Lamp]
    B1 -->|Store DTC| G1[Non Volatile Memory]
    B1 -->|Freeze Frame Snapshot| G1

    E1 -->|Mode 03 Read| H1
    E1 -->|Mode 04 Clear| H1
    E1 -->|Mode 01 Live PID| H1
```

## 4.2 OBD-II Fault Detection and MIL Illumination Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Sensor Input to ECU] --> stepB[Diagnostic Manager Compares with Expected Value]
    stepB --> stepC{Fault Detected This Drive Cycle?}
    stepC -->|No| stepA
    stepC -->|Yes| stepD[Set Pending DTC in Memory]
    stepD --> stepE[Wait for Next Cold Start Drive Cycle]
    stepE --> stepF{Fault Recurred on Next Cycle?}
    stepF -->|No| stepG[Pending DTC Cleared - Pass]
    stepF -->|Yes| stepH[Store Confirmed DTC and Freeze Frame]
    stepH --> stepI[Command MIL ON]
    stepI --> stepJ[Continue Monitoring - 3 Drive Cycles for Catalyst]

    stepI --> stepK{Two Consecutive Cycles Without Fault?}
    stepK -->|Yes| stepL[MIL OFF - DTC Stored as History]
    stepK -->|No| stepI
```

## 4.3 OBD-II Data Request — Scan Tool to ECU Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Scan as Scan Tool
    participant DLC as SAE J1962 DLC
    participant ECU as Vehicle ECU

    Scan->>DLC: Send Service Request (e.g., Mode 01 PID 0C = Engine RPM)
    DLC->>ECU: Transmit Request Frame on CAN or K-Line
    ECU->>ECU: Validate Request and Lookup PID Value
    ECU->>DLC: Send Positive Response with Data Bytes
    DLC->>Scan: Receive Response (e.g., 41 0C 12 34 = Engine RPM 0x1234)
    Note over Scan,ECU: Mode 03 used to read DTCs, Mode 04 to clear them
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above sequence illustrates the **request-response** model of OBD-II: every scan tool command triggers a deterministic, structured reply governed by SAE J1979.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between OBD-I and OBD-II systems in a passenger car.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | OBD-I | OBD-II |
|---------|-------|--------|
| Standardization | Proprietary, manufacturer-specific | Universal (SAE/ISO standards) |
| Connector | Varies by manufacturer | Standardized 16-pin **SAE J1962** DLC |
| Diagnostic Codes | Manufacturer-specific | Standardized **SAE J2012** DTCs (P/B/C/U codes) |
| Protocol | No standard | Five protocols (J1850 VPW/PWM, ISO 9141-2, ISO 14230-4, ISO 15765-4) |
| Year | 1980s | USA: 1996, EU: 2001/2004, India: 2013/2017 |
| Capability | Basic engine codes only | Engine, transmission, ABS, body, network; live PIDs; freeze frame; bidirectional control |

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six standardized Diagnostic Test Modes defined by SAE J1979.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Mode $01$** — Request current powertrain diagnostic data (live PIDs).
2. **Mode $02$** — Request freeze-frame data captured at the time of the fault.
3. **Mode $03$** — Request emission-related Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs).
4. **Mode $04$** — Clear/reset emission-related diagnostic information and MIL.
5. **Mode $05$** — Request oxygen sensor monitoring test results.
6. **Mode $06$** — Request on-board monitoring test results for non-continuous systems.
*(Other valid modes: $07$ Pending DTCs, $08$ Bidirectional control, $09$ Vehicle information, $0A$ Permanent DTCs.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **DTC system letter** (P/B/C/U) with the **protocol** used (CAN/K-Line). A common mistake is writing *"U-codes mean CAN-bus codes"* — they actually mean **Network/Communication** faults (which *may* involve CAN but are not limited to it). Always write the full decoded meaning.

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an OBD-II system. List the major SAE and ISO standards that govern OBD-II. (7 marks)

**(b)** Decode the following OBD-II DTCs and explain the most likely component failure associated with each: `P0171`, `P0302`, `P0420`, `B0020`, `U0100`. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — OBD-II Architecture (7 marks):**

The OBD-II architecture consists of four functional layers:

1. **Sensor Layer:** Provides raw inputs to the ECUs — oxygen sensor, MAF, MAP, TPS, CKP, CMP, wheel-speed sensors, etc.
2. **ECU/Control Module Layer:** Each subsystem has its own ECU (ECM, TCM, ABS, BCM) running diagnostic routines and DTC management in non-volatile memory (KAM/NVRAM).
3. **Communication Layer:** ECUs communicate over **CAN bus** (ISO 11898) and present a standardized gateway to the **SAE J1962 DLC** (16-pin connector). Standardized protocols:
   - **SAE J1962** — Connector
   - **SAE J1979** — Diagnostic Test Modes
   - **SAE J2012** — DTC definition and message structure
   - **SAE J1850** (VPW/PWM) — Legacy protocol
   - **ISO 9141-2 / ISO 14230-4 (KWP2000)** — Legacy European/Asian
   - **ISO 15765-4** — Mandatory CAN protocol
4. **External Interface Layer:** Scan tool or telematics dongle connected at the DLC, querying the ECUs in standardized Mode formats and decoding responses into human-readable form.

The MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp) on the instrument cluster is driven by the ECM whenever a confirmed DTC is set.

**Part (b) — DTC Decoding (7 marks):**

| DTC | System | Type | Subsystem | Likely Fault |
|-----|--------|------|-----------|--------------|
| `P0171` | Powertrain | Generic | Fuel/Air metering | System too **lean, Bank 1** — likely vacuum leak, weak fuel pump, dirty MAF, or stuck-open PCV. |
| `P0302` | Powertrain | Generic | Ignition | **Cylinder 2 Misfire Detected** — faulty spark plug, ignition coil, fuel injector, or low compression. |
| `P0420` | Powertrain | Generic | Auxiliary emissions | **Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold, Bank 1** — degraded catalytic converter, downstream O2 sensor failure, or exhaust leak. |
| `B0020` | Body | Generic | Airbag/Restraint | **Left Side Deployment Loop Open** — broken airbag squib wire, corroded connector, or failed airbag module. |
| `U0100` | Network | Generic | Communication | **Lost Communication with ECM/PCM** — broken CAN bus wire, faulty gateway, defective ECM, or terminator resistor failure. |

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the SAE J1962 DLC connector pin-out and the five OBD-II communication protocols. Mention which protocol became mandatory and when. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the OBD-II DTC format `XYYYY` with examples. Explain the difference between *Pending*, *Confirmed*, and *Permanent* DTCs. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — DLC Connector and Protocols (7 marks):**

The **SAE J1962** connector is a 16-pin trapezoidal connector located within reach of the driver's seat. Critical pin assignments:

- **Pin 4** — Chassis ground
- **Pin 5** — Signal ground
- **Pin 6** — **CAN High** (ISO 15765-4)
- **Pin 14** — **CAN Low** (ISO 15765-4)
- **Pin 7** — **K-Line** (ISO 9141-2 / ISO 14230-4)
- **Pin 15** — **L-Line** (used for ISO 9141-2 initialization)
- **Pin 16** — **Battery Power** ($+12$ V for cars, $+24$ V for heavy trucks)
- **Pins 2 and 10** — J1850 Bus+ and Bus− (for VPW/PWM)

**Five Communication Protocols:**

1. **SAE J1850 VPW (Variable Pulse Width):** 10.4 kbps; primarily used by **General Motors**.
2. **SAE J1850 PWM (Pulse Width Modulation):** 41.6 kbps; used by **Ford**.
3. **ISO 9141-2:** 10.4 kbps, asynchronous; popular in **European and Asian** manufacturers.
4. **ISO 14230-4 (KWP2000):** 1.2 to 250 kbps; extends ISO 9141-2 with **fast initialization** and richer services.
5. **ISO 15765-4 (CAN-based diagnostics):** 250/500 kbps; **mandatory on all US vehicles since 2008** and adopted globally. Uses CAN frames with ISO 15765-2 transport protocol for multi-frame messages.

**Part (b) — DTC Format and Lifecycle (7 marks):**

**Format:** `XYYYY`

- **X** = System: `P` (Powertrain), `B` (Body), `C` (Chassis), `U` (Network).
- **Y1** = Code type: `0` = Generic (SAE), `1` = Manufacturer-specific, `2`/`3` = SAE (powertrain only).
- **Y2 Y3 Y4** = Specific fault index (0000–FFFF for each combination).

**DTC Lifecycle States:**

- **Pending DTC:** Set when a fault is detected on a **single drive cycle** but not yet confirmed. Visible via **Mode $07$**.
- **Confirmed (Stored) DTC:** Set when the fault **reoccurs on the next consecutive drive cycle**. The MIL illuminates. Visible via **Mode $03$**. Can be cleared by Mode $04$ or by a successful completion of the relevant monitor.
- **Permanent DTC:** Introduced in **2010**; cannot be cleared by Mode $04$ or a battery disconnect. It clears **only when the OBD-II system itself verifies the repair** over subsequent drive cycles. Visible via **Mode $0A$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note (Mark Recovery):** If the examiner asks *why Permanent DTCs were introduced*, answer: **to prevent customers from manually erasing codes immediately before an emission inspection (I/M test)**. This is a frequent 1-mark follow-up sub-question.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **OBD-I** = proprietary (1980s). **OBD-II** = standardized (USA 1996; EU 2001/2004; India 2013/2017).
- The **SAE J1962** connector is the universal **16-pin DLC**, with **Pin 16 = +12 V/+24 V** and **Pins 6 & 14 = CAN-H / CAN-L**.
- **Five OBD-II protocols:** J1850 VPW, J1850 PWM, ISO 9141-2, ISO 14230-4 (KWP2000), ISO 15765-4 (CAN).
- **ISO 15765-4 (CAN) is mandatory on all new US vehicles since 2008.**
- **DTC format `XYYYY`:** `P` = Powertrain, `B` = Body, `C` = Chassis, `U` = Network. Second digit `0` = Generic, `1` = Manufacturer.
- **MIL illumination requires two consecutive confirmed drive cycles** for most emissions faults; **one cycle** for catalytic converter damage causing excess emissions.
- **Diagnostic Test Modes (SAE J1979):** Mode $01$ Live Data, $02$ Freeze Frame, $03$ Read DTCs, $04$ Clear, $05$ O2 test, $06$ Test results, $07$ Pending, $08$ Control, $09$ Vehicle Info, $0A$ Permanent.
- **Permanent DTCs (Mode $0A$) introduced in 2010** — cannot be cleared by scan tool.
- **OBD-III (concept stage):** wireless, real-time remote emission reporting to authorities.
- **KWP2000 fast-init** uses a $50 \pm 5$ ms wake-up pulse on the K-Line.
- **CAN bus dominant-state differential voltage** is exactly $V_{diff} = V_H - V_L = 3.5 - 1.5 = 2.0$ V.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT501 - AUTOMOBILE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Pressure Sensors ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > A **Pressure Sensor** is an electromechanical transducer that converts an applied mechanical pressure — the force per unit area exerted by a fluid (gas or liquid) — into a measurable, standardized electrical signal (typically voltage, current, frequency, or a digital pulse-width-modulated output). In modern automotive electronic control units (ECUs), pressure sensors form a critical sub-system of the sensor cluster, providing real-time pressure feedback to the Engine Control Module (ECM), Transmission Control Module (TCM), and the Body Control Module (BCM). The SI unit of pressure is the **Pascal (Pa)**, which equals **1 N/m²**. However, automotive engineering commonly uses derived units such as **kPa**, **bar**, **psi (lbf/in²)**, and **mmHg**. Standard atmospheric pressure at sea level is defined as **101.325 kPa (≈ 1.01325 bar)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Balloon-in-a-Box" Analogy:** > Imagine placing a partially inflated balloon inside a sealed cardboard box. If you press down on the top of the box with your hand, the balloon gets squeezed. The harder you push, the more the balloon deforms. Now, glue a tiny ruler to the side of the balloon and a marker on the box wall — as the balloon changes shape, the marker draws a different line on the ruler. That "drawn line" is exactly what a pressure sensor does — it transforms an invisible force (pressure) into a measurable, readable signal. In an automobile, instead of a balloon, there is a thin **silicon diaphragm** with embedded strain-sensitive resistors. Instead of a ruler, there are electrical wires carrying a voltage back to the ECU. The ECU's microprocessor then interprets that voltage as a specific pressure value. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold) - Standard Atmospheric Pressure: **101.325 kPa** - Typical MAP Sensor Range (Turbocharged Engines): **10 kPa to 400 kPa** (vacuum to boost) - Typical Oil Pressure Sensor Range (passenger car): **0 kPa to 1000 kPa** (≈ 0–145 psi) - Operating Voltage (automotive sensors): **5 V DC** (regulated reference from ECU) or **12 V** (system voltage) - Sensor Output (analog): **0.5 V to 4.5 V** (ratiometric to 5 V supply) - Response Time (typical MAP sensor): **≤ 1 ms** - Operating Temperature Range: **−40 °C to +125 °C** (under-hood automotive spec) > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — Pressure Sensors):** > The KTU 2024 Scheme PCAUT501 Module 4 emphasizes the **classification**, **operating principles**, and **automotive applications** of pressure sensors, with a particular focus on **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP)**, **Barometric Air Pressure (BAP)**, **Oil Pressure**, **Fuel Rail Pressure**, and **Tire Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS)** sensors. Students are expected to draw constructional diagrams, explain the piezoresistive effect, and compare sensor technologies. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Automotive Pressure Sensors Pressure sensors in vehicles are classified based on the **measured medium**, **measurement principle**, and **output signal type**. **A. Based on Measured Medium / Application:** | Sensor Type | Location in Vehicle | Typical Pressure Range | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) | Intake manifold | 10–400 kPa | Determines engine load; controls fuel injection duration | | BAP (Barometric Air Pressure) | Inside ECU or air-filter housing | 60–110 kPa | Altitude compensation for fuel map | | Oil Pressure | Engine block / oil gallery | 0–1000 kPa | Oil-pressure warning lamp; engine protection | | Fuel Rail Pressure | Common-rail diesel / GDI petrol | 0–200,000 kPa (2000 bar) | Precision fuel injection control | | Tire Pressure (TPMS) | Inside each tire valve | 0–500 kPa | Safety warning; fuel economy | | Brake Fluid Pressure | Master cylinder / ABS modulator | 0–20,000 kPa | ABS / EBD control logic | | AC Refrigerant Pressure | AC line | 0–3000 kPa | Compressor clutch cut-off control | **B. Based on Operating Principle:** | Principle | Mechanism | Key Material | Common Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Piezoresistive** | Resistance change of doped silicon under stress | Silicon diaphragm with diffused resistors | MAP, BAP, oil pressure | | **Capacitive** | Gap change between two plates alters capacitance | MEMS silicon plates | Low-pressure MAP, barometric | | **Piezoelectric** | Stress generates charge across crystal | Quartz, PZT ceramic | Knock detection, dynamic pressure | | **Strain-Gauge (Bonded)** | Foil gauge resistance changes with elongation | Constantan foil on steel diaphragm | Oil pressure, hydraulic pressure | | **LVDT-based** | Core displacement in a transformer coil | Ferromagnetic core | Heavy-duty brake / hydraulic test rigs | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Piezoresistive Effect — Core Principle:** > When a semiconductor crystal (typically silicon) is mechanically stressed, its electrical resistivity changes anisotropically. This phenomenon, discovered by Lord Kelvin (1856), is the **piezoresistive effect**. Modern MAP sensors exploit this by diffusing four doped resistors onto a thin silicon diaphragm in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration. Under pressure, two resistors experience compressive strain (resistance decreases) while two experience tensile strain (resistance increases), unbalancing the bridge and producing a differential voltage proportional to pressure. ### 2.2 The Wheatstone Bridge — Mathematical Heart of a Pressure Sensor The piezoresistive sensor die is a **Wheatstone bridge** — a diamond of four resistors, two of which change with pressure. The bridge output voltage is given by: $$ V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}\right) $$ Where $V_{ex}$ is the excitation voltage supplied by the ECU (typically **5.0 V**), and $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ are the four bridge resistors. At rest (zero applied pressure), the bridge is balanced, so $V_{out} = 0$ V. When pressure is applied, the differential output becomes non-zero and is linearly proportional to pressure. ### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic Flow (Why and How) 1. **Pressure is applied** to the silicon diaphragm via a port connected to the fluid (e.g., intake manifold). 2. **The diaphragm deflects** elastically — its centre moves by a few micrometres while the edges remain fixed. 3. **Mechanical strain is induced** in the diaphragm material along defined crystal axes. 4. **Resistivity changes** in the diffused piezoresistors occur due to the piezoresistive effect. 5. **The Wheatstone bridge becomes unbalanced** — output voltage $V_{out}$ shifts away from zero. 6. **Signal conditioning circuitry** (inside the sensor housing) amplifies, linearizes, and temperature-compensates the signal. 7. **A 0.5 V – 4.5 V analog signal** is sent to the ECU's Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). 8. **The ECU's software map** (calibration lookup table) converts the voltage reading into a pressure value in kPa. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Equation / Value | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Definition of Pressure | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa (N/m²) | Force per unit area | | 2 | Gauge vs Absolute | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Pa | MAP sensor measures $P_{abs}$ | | 3 | Bridge Output (general) | $V_{out} = V_{ex}\!\left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}-\dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)$ | V | Wheatstone bridge | | 4 | Bridge Sensitivity (4 active) | $V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | V | All 4 resistors active | | 5 | Piezoresistive Gauge Factor | $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | dimensionless | Silicon GF ≈ 100–170; metal ≈ 2 | | 6 | Capacitive Sensor | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | F | Gap $d$ changes with pressure | | 7 | Output Linearity | $V_{out} = m \cdot P + b$ | V | $m$ = slope, $b$ = offset | | 8 | Ratiometric Output | $\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{ex}} = f(P)$ | dimensionless | Independent of supply drift | | 9 | Atmospheric Std. | $P_{atm} = 101.325$ | kPa | Sea level reference | | 10 | Barometric Formula | $P = P_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{Lh}{T_0}\right)^{\dfrac{gM}{RL}}$ | Pa | $L \approx 0.0065$ K/m | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world engineering utility:** The piezoresistive MAP sensor is the **backbone of speed-density air-charge estimation** in port-fuel-injected engines. Without it, the ECU would have no way to infer engine load, and the air-fuel ratio could not be accurately controlled. Modern turbocharged engines rely on MAP sensing for closed-loop boost control, where a deviation of even **±5 kPa** from the target boost pressure triggers diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs) such as P0299 (Turbocharger Underboost). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Piezoresistive Sensor Transfer Function **Objective:** Derive the bridge output voltage $V_{out}$ as a function of applied differential pressure $\Delta P$ on a square silicon diaphragm. #### Step 1 — Geometry and Boundary Conditions Consider a square silicon diaphragm of side length $a$ and thickness $h$, clamped at all four edges. A uniform differential pressure $\Delta P$ acts on the top surface. The maximum centre deflection $w_0$ of a clamped square plate under uniform load is given by classical plate theory: $$ w_0 = \alpha \cdot \frac{\Delta P \cdot a^4}{E \cdot h^3} $$ Where: - $\alpha$ is a dimensionless geometric coefficient ($\alpha \approx 0.00126$ for a square clamped plate with Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.25$). - $E$ is Young's modulus of silicon ($E \approx 190$ GPa along the $\langle 100 \rangle$ crystal direction). - $a$ is the side length (typically 1–3 mm). - $h$ is the diaphragm thickness (typically 20–50 µm). #### Step 2 — Strain at Diaphragm Centre The maximum radial strain at the centre of the diaphragm (where piezoresistors are usually placed) is: $$ \varepsilon_r = \beta \cdot \frac{\Delta P \cdot a^2}{E \cdot h^2} $$ Where $\beta$ is another geometric coefficient ($\beta \approx 0.00138$ for clamped square plate). #### Step 3 — Resistance Change via Gauge Factor By definition of the gauge factor $GF$: $$ \frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon_r $$ For p-type silicon diffused along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction, $GF \approx 100$ to $170$. #### Step 4 — Substitute Strain into the Gauge Equation $$ \frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \beta \cdot \frac{\Delta P \cdot a^2}{E \cdot h^2} $$ #### Step 5 — Bridge Output Voltage For a fully active Wheatstone bridge where all four resistors experience equal magnitude but alternating-sign strain (two in compression, two in tension), the differential output simplifies to: $$ V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} $$ Substituting Step 4: $$ V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \beta \cdot \frac{\Delta P \cdot a^2}{E \cdot h^2} $$ $$ \boxed{\,V_{out} = \underbrace{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \beta \cdot \frac{a^2}{E \cdot h^2}}_{\text{Sensitivity constant } S} \cdot \Delta P\,} $$ Thus, $V_{out} = S \cdot \Delta P$, where $S$ is the **sensitivity** in **V/Pa**. #### Step 6 — Numerical Example (Worked-Out) **Given:** - $V_{ex} = 5.0$ V - $GF = 120$ - $\beta = 0.00138$ - $a = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m - $E = 190 \times 10^9$ Pa - $h = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ m - Applied $\Delta P = 100$ kPa $= 100{,}000$ Pa **Compute $a^2$:** $$ a^2 = (2 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 $$ **Compute $E \cdot h^2$:** $$ E \cdot h^2 = 190 \times 10^9 \times (25 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 190 \times 10^9 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-10} $$ $$ E \cdot h^2 = 118.75 \text{ N·m} $$ **Compute the ratio $a^2 / (E \cdot h^2)$:** $$ \frac{a^2}{E \cdot h^2} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-6}}{118.75} = 3.368 \times 10^{-8} \text{ Pa}^{-1} $$ **Compute the sensitivity constant $S$:** $$ S = 5.0 \times 120 \times 0.00138 \times 3.368 \times 10^{-8} $$ $$ S = 5.0 \times 120 \times 4.648 \times 10^{-11} $$ $$ S = 2.789 \times 10^{-8} \text{ V/Pa} $$ **Compute $V_{out}$ at $\Delta P = 100$ kPa:** $$ V_{out} = 2.789 \times 10^{-8} \times 100{,}000 = 2.789 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \approx 2.79 \text{ mV} $$ **Comment:** A raw output of only **2.79 mV** is far too small to be directly digitized by the ECU's 0–5 V ADC with acceptable resolution. This is precisely why **on-chip CMOS signal-conditioning ASICs** are integrated within modern sensor packages — they provide a gain of approximately **1000×**, raising the full-scale output to **≈ 4.5 V** at maximum rated pressure. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Pressure Sensor Trade-Off Table | Parameter | Piezoresistive | Capacitive | Piezoelectric | Bonded Strain Gauge | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Sensitivity** | High | Medium | Very High | Medium | | **Frequency Response** | DC–10 kHz | DC–1 kHz | > 100 kHz (AC only) | DC–5 kHz | | **Static (DC) Measurement** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (AC-coupled) | ✅ Yes | | **Temperature Sensitivity** | High (needs compensation) | Low | Medium | Medium | | **Cost** | Low (MEMS batch fab) | Low | Medium | High | | **Typical Use in Car** | MAP, BAP, Oil, TPMS | Barometric, low-pressure MAP | Knock sensor (also a vibration sensor), dynamic combustion | Hydraulic brake / off-road | | **Output Type** | Analog voltage | Analog voltage / capacitance | Charge / voltage | Analog voltage (bridge) | ### 3.3 Closed-Loop Signal Chain Diagram (Analytical Mapping) The complete signal path from physical pressure to a digital number inside the ECU is: $$ P_{fluid} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Diaphragm deflection} \;\longrightarrow\; \Delta R \;\longrightarrow\; V_{out}(\text{bridge}) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ASIC (amp + T-comp)} \;\longrightarrow\; V_{analog}(0.5\text{–}4.5\text{ V}) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ADC} \;\longrightarrow\; N_{digital} \;\longrightarrow\; P_{digital} $$ The reverse mapping (digital → physical) is performed by the ECU using a calibration lookup table: $$ P_{digital} = f(N_{digital}) = m \cdot N_{digital} + c $$ Where $m$ (slope, in **kPa/bit**) and $c$ (offset, in **kPa**) are stored in the ECU's non-volatile memory (EEPROM/Flash). --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Automotive MAP Sensor Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB node1[Intake Manifold
Vacuum Boost Pressure] node2[Pressure Port
Sealed Nipple with O-Ring] node3[Silicon Diaphragm
MEMS Piezoresistive] node4[Wheatstone Bridge
4 Diffused Resistors] node5[Reference Voltage Vex
5.0 V from ECU] node6[Differential Amplifier
On-Chip ASIC] node7[Temperature Compensation
Network] node8[Output Buffer
0.5 V to 4.5 V Analog] node9[ECU ADC Input Pin
10 or 12 bit Resolution] node10[Microprocessor
Lookup Table Conversion] node11[Engine Control Action
Fuel Injection Pulse Width] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node5 --> node4 node4 --> node6 node6 --> node7 node7 --> node8 node8 --> node9 node9 --> node10 node10 --> node11 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Wheatstone Bridge Under Pressure ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Vex 5V] nodeB[Node A Top] nodeC[Node B Right] nodeD[Node D Bottom] nodeE[GND 0V] nodeF[R1 Piezoresistor] nodeG[R2 Piezoresistor] nodeH[R3 Piezoresistor] nodeI[R4 Piezoresistor] nodeJ[Vout to ASIC] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeF nodeB --> nodeH nodeF --> nodeC nodeH --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeI nodeD --> nodeG nodeI --> nodeE nodeG --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeJ nodeD --> nodeJ ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete TPMS (Tire Pressure Monitoring System) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeT1[Tire 1 Sensor
Piezoresistive plus RF Transmitter 433 MHz] nodeT2[Tire 2 Sensor
Piezoresistive plus RF Transmitter] nodeT3[Tire 3 Sensor
Piezoresistive plus RF Transmitter] nodeT4[Tire 4 Sensor
Piezoresistive plus RF Transmitter] nodeRX[RF Receiver Antenna
Mounted Near Wheel Well] nodeDEC[Decoder Module
Inside BCM] nodeECU[Body Control Module BCM] nodeDISP[Driver Dashboard Display
Tire Pressure Readout] nodeDTC[OBD II Diagnostic Trouble Code
DTC C0750 Family] nodeT1 --> nodeRX nodeT2 --> nodeRX nodeT3 --> nodeRX nodeT4 --> nodeRX nodeRX --> nodeDEC nodeDEC --> nodeECU nodeECU --> nodeDISP nodeECU --> nodeDTC ``` ### 4.4 Constructional Sketch — MAP Sensor Cross-Section (Block Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeL1[Outer Plastic Housing
with Electrical Connector] nodeL2[Sealed Reference Cavity
Vacuum Reference approximately 0.1 Pa] nodeL3[Silicon Diaphragm with Diffused Piezoresistors
Approximately 20 to 50 micrometres thick] nodeL4[Glass Bonding Frit
Anodic Bond] nodeL5[Ceramic Substrate with ASIC and Passive Components] nodeL6[Three Pins
Vex, GND, Vout] nodeL7[Intake Manifold Pressure Port
O-Ring Seal] nodeL7 --> nodeL3 nodeL3 --> nodeL2 nodeL3 --> nodeL4 nodeL4 --> nodeL5 nodeL5 --> nodeL1 nodeL5 --> nodeL6 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-Diagram Reading Tip for Exam:** Always draw the sensor with the **reference vacuum cavity on the back side of the diaphragm** and the **process pressure (manifold) on the front side**. The differential pressure between the two sides deflects the diaphragm. This sealed-reference arrangement makes the MAP sensor a true **absolute pressure** device, which is why it can read pressures below atmospheric (during engine vacuum, e.g., 30 kPa at idle). --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define a pressure sensor and explain the piezoresistive effect. > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A **pressure sensor** is a transducer that converts an applied fluid pressure into a proportional electrical signal. **(1 mark)** > The **piezoresistive effect** is the phenomenon in which the electrical resistivity of a semiconductor (such as silicon) changes when subjected to mechanical stress or strain. **(1 mark)** > Mathematically, $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$, where $GF$ is the gauge factor (typically 100–170 for silicon) and $\varepsilon$ is the strain. **(1 mark)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** List any three automotive applications of pressure sensors and state the typical pressure range for each. > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > 1. **MAP Sensor (Manifold Absolute Pressure):** Located on the intake manifold; range ≈ **10 kPa to 400 kPa** (vacuum to boost). **(1 mark)** > 2. **Oil Pressure Sensor:** Mounted on the engine block; range ≈ **0 kPa to 1000 kPa** (≈ 0–145 psi). **(1 mark)** > 3. **Tire Pressure Sensor (TPMS):** Inside each wheel; range ≈ **0 kPa to 500 kPa**. **(1 mark)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A — Part (a) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) sensor** used in a port-fuel-injected petrol engine. **Model Solution:** **1. Function & Location (2 marks):** The MAP sensor measures the absolute pressure inside the intake manifold downstream of the throttle body. Its signal is one of the primary inputs to the ECU for calculating air mass (via the speed-density method) and hence for determining the required injector pulse width. **2. Construction (3 marks):** - **Pressure port** connects to the intake manifold via a vacuum hose. - **Sealed reference vacuum cavity** on the back side of the diaphragm provides a stable 0 Pa reference. - **Silicon diaphragm** (20–50 µm thick) with four diffused piezoresistors arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge**. - **On-chip ASIC** for amplification, temperature compensation, and output buffering. - **Three-pin connector:** Vex (+5 V), GND, and Vout (0.5–4.5 V analog). - **Plastic housing** with integrated connector and O-ring seal. **3. Working (2 marks):** When manifold pressure rises (engine under load / turbo boost), the diaphragm deflects, two piezoresistors experience tensile strain (R↑) while two experience compressive strain (R↓). The Wheatstone bridge becomes unbalanced, producing a differential voltage proportional to the applied pressure. This millivolt-level signal is amplified by the ASIC to a 0.5–4.5 V analog output, which the ECU's ADC digitizes and converts to a pressure value via a calibration lookup table. --- #### **Question A — Part (b) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** Derive the transfer function of a piezoresistive pressure sensor and show that the bridge output voltage is directly proportional to the applied differential pressure. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** The maximum centre deflection of a clamped square diaphragm is: $$ w_0 = \alpha \cdot \frac{\Delta P \cdot a^4}{E \cdot h^3} $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** The radial strain at the diaphragm centre is: $$ \varepsilon_r = \beta \cdot \frac{\Delta P \cdot a^2}{E \cdot h^2} $$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** By definition of the gauge factor: $$ \frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon_r = GF \cdot \beta \cdot \frac{\Delta P \cdot a^2}{E \cdot h^2} $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** For a fully active Wheatstone bridge: $$ V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} $$ Substituting: $$ V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \beta \cdot \frac{a^2}{E \cdot h^2} \cdot \Delta P $$ **Step 5 (2 marks):** Defining the sensitivity constant $S$: $$ S = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \beta \cdot \frac{a^2}{E \cdot h^2} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\,V_{out} = S \cdot \Delta P\,} $$ This confirms that the bridge output voltage is **linearly proportional** to the applied differential pressure, provided that the diaphragm operates in its elastic (small-deflection) regime. --- #### **Question B — Part (a) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Compare **piezoresistive**, **capacitive**, and **piezoelectric** pressure sensors on the basis of (i) measurement type (static or dynamic), (ii) sensitivity, (iii) typical automotive application. **Model Solution:** | Parameter | Piezoresistive | Capacitive | Piezoelectric | |---|---|---|---| | **(i) Measurement type** | Static (DC) and dynamic | Static (DC) and dynamic | Dynamic only (AC-coupled) | | **(ii) Sensitivity** | High (GF ≈ 100–170) | Medium | Very high | | **(iii) Automotive application** | MAP, BAP, Oil Pressure, TPMS | Low-pressure MAP, Barometric | Knock sensor, in-cylinder dynamic pressure | **(i) Measurement type (2 marks):** Piezoresistive and capacitive sensors can measure both steady (DC) pressures like manifold vacuum at idle and rapid transients. Piezoelectric sensors, however, only respond to *changing* pressure because their output is a charge that decays with time — they cannot hold a steady DC output. **(ii) Sensitivity (2 marks):** Silicon piezoresistors offer extremely high gauge factors (100–170) versus metal foil (≈ 2), making piezoresistive sensors very sensitive. Capacitive sensors have moderate sensitivity since capacitance depends inversely on the gap (large signals require very small gaps). Piezoelectric sensors generate charge directly and have the highest sensitivity of all three. **(iii) Automotive application (3 marks):** Piezoresistive sensors dominate **steady-state pressure measurement** (MAP, BAP, oil, fuel rail). Capacitive sensors are used for **low-pressure barometric** and **low-vacuum MAP** applications. Piezoelectric sensors are used for **engine knock detection** and **in-cylinder combustion pressure research**. --- #### **Question B — Part (b) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Tire Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS)**. List any two advantages and one limitation. **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (4 marks):** ``` [Tire Sensor + RF Tx] → [RF Antenna Receiver] → [Decoder Module] → [BCM] → [Dashboard Display + DTC] ``` - Each of the **four wheels** contains a **piezoresistive (or capacitive) pressure sensor** bonded to an **ASIC + RF transmitter** on a single 3 V lithium coin cell. - The sensor wakes up periodically (every few seconds while driving) and **transmits** the pressure, temperature, and unique sensor ID via **433 MHz** (Europe/Asia) or **315 MHz** (North America) RF. - A **central receiver antenna** mounted near each wheel well picks up the RF signal and forwards it to a **decoder module**. - The **Body Control Module (BCM)** decodes the sensor ID, matches it to a known wheel position, compares the pressure against a threshold (typically **180–220 kPa** warning level), and triggers a **dashboard warning lamp** if under-inflation is detected. - A **DTC (e.g., C0755, C0760)** is also stored in non-volatile memory for retrieval via OBD-II scan tools. **Advantages (2 marks):** 1. **Improved safety** — early detection of slow leaks prevents blowouts and accidents. 2. **Better fuel economy and tire life** — correct pressure reduces rolling resistance and uneven tread wear. **Limitation (1 mark):** 1. The non-replaceable lithium battery has a limited life of **≈ 7–10 years**; once depleted, the entire sensor module must be replaced. Also, RF link reliability can degrade under heavy EMI environments. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure:** Many students write "MAP sensor measures manifold vacuum" — this is wrong terminology. It measures **absolute pressure**; vacuum is the *difference* between atmospheric and absolute pressure. Saying $P_{vacuum} = P_{atm} - P_{manifold}$ earns full credit. > 2. **Forgetting to draw the sealed vacuum reference cavity** in the MAP sensor sketch costs at least 1 mark. The reference cavity is what makes it an *absolute* (not gauge) sensor. > 3. **Skipping the gauge factor in derivations:** When deriving the transfer function, students who omit $GF$ in Step 3 lose 1 mark. Always define $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ before substituting. > 4. **Mixing up MAP and MAF:** MAP = Manifold Absolute Pressure (a pressure sensor). MAF = Mass Air Flow (a different sensor using a hot-wire or hot-film anemometer). Examiners will deduct for interchange. > 5. **TPMS frequency confusion:** India and Europe use **433 MHz**; North America uses **315 MHz**. Mentioning the wrong band costs a mark if the question specifies the region. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Definition:** A pressure sensor is a transducer that converts fluid pressure (force/area, units of Pa or kPa) into an electrical signal. - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** **101.325 kPa** at sea level. - **MAP sensor range:** ~10 kPa (deep vacuum at idle) to ~400 kPa (turbo boost). - **Operating principle (most common):** **Piezoresistive** — silicon diaphragm + Wheatstone bridge + ASIC. - **Wheatstone bridge output:** $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)$ - **Gauge factor:** $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$; silicon ≈ 100–170, metal ≈ 2. - **Transfer function:** $V_{out} = S \cdot \Delta P$, where $S$ depends on $V_{ex}, GF, a, h, E$. - **Output voltage range:** Typically **0.5 V to 4.5 V** ratiometric to a **5 V** excitation — the 0.5 V offset is reserved for diagnostic short/open detection by the ECU. - **Absolute vs Gauge:** MAP = absolute (has vacuum reference); Oil pressure = gauge (vented to atmosphere). - **TPMS frequency:** 433 MHz in India/Europe; 315 MHz in North America. - **Signal chain:** Pressure → Diaphragm deflection → ΔR → Bridge Vout (mV) → ASIC amp → 0.5–4.5 V → ECU ADC → Digital → Lookup table → Pressure value. - **DTC examples:** P0105–P0109 (MAP sensor circuit), P0521–P0524 (oil pressure), C0750–C0760 (TPMS). - **Three-pin wiring:** Vex (5 V), GND, Vout (analog). Never short Vex to Vout. - **Exam mantra:** Always draw the **reference vacuum cavity**, label the **diaphragm thickness** (20–50 µm), state the **gauge factor**, and end with a **calibrated linear equation** $V_{out} = mP + b$. ---

topics.title: Temperature Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT501 - AUTOMOBILE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Temperature Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌡️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Official Terminology)**
> A **Temperature Sensor** in an automobile is a transducer that converts the thermal energy (temperature) of a physical medium into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, resistance, or current), which is subsequently fed to the **ECU (Engine Control Unit)** for real-time engine management, emissions control, and driver information display.

In modern vehicles, temperature sensors are the *nervous system* of the powertrain. Without accurate thermal feedback, the ECU would have no idea how to adjust fuel injection quantity, ignition timing, or transmission shift points.

---

## 🔑 Real-World Analogy: The Human Body Thermoreceptor

Imagine how your body reacts when you touch a hot cup of coffee or step into a freezing room:

1. Your skin's **thermoreceptors** detect the change in temperature.
2. This thermal information is converted into **electrical nerve impulses**.
3. Your **brain (ECU equivalent)** processes the signal and instructs your muscles (actuators) to withdraw your hand or shiver to generate warmth.

A **temperature sensor in a car works identically**:
- The **sensor element** acts as the *thermoreceptor*.
- The **wiring harness** acts as the *nerves*.
- The **ECU** acts as the *brain*.
- The **fuel injectors, radiator fan, dashboard gauge** act as the *muscles*.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board High-Yield Highlights**
> - The most commonly used temperature sensor in automobiles is the **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) Thermistor**.
> - Temperature sensors fall under the classification of **Variable Resistance Sensors**.
> - Primary automotive applications include: **ECT (Engine Coolant Temperature), IAT (Intake Air Temperature), EGT (Exhaust Gas Temperature), Oil Temperature, and Ambient Air Temperature**.
> - Operating range in vehicles typically spans from **−40 °C to +1000 °C**, depending on sensor location.

---

## 📋 Classification of Automotive Temperature Sensors

Automobile temperature sensors can be broadly classified based on their **operating principle**:

| Classification | Sensor Type | Primary Automotive Use |
|----------------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Resistive** | NTC Thermistor | Coolant, Intake Air, Oil |
| **Resistive** | PTC Thermistor | Over-temperature protection |
| **Resistive** | RTD (Pt100/Pt1000) | High-precision measurements |
| **Voltage-Generating** | Thermocouple (Type K) | Exhaust Gas Temperature |
| **Mechanical** | Bimetallic Strip | Dashboard temperature gauge |
| **Semiconductor** | IC Bandgap Sensor | Climate control modules |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 🧠 2.1 The NTC Thermistor — The Heart of Automotive Temperature Sensing

The **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) Thermistor** is a semiconductor device whose electrical resistance **decreases** as the temperature **increases**. It is the dominant sensor in modern ECUs.

### Working Principle (Step-by-Step)
1. The thermistor is manufactured from **sintered metal oxides** (typically nickel, cobalt, manganese, or iron oxides).
2. As temperature rises, the **charge carriers (electrons and holes)** gain thermal energy.
3. This results in a higher number of free charge carriers available for conduction.
4. Consequently, **resistance drops** as temperature rises — hence the "Negative" Coefficient.

---

## 🧠 2.2 The PTC Thermistor

In contrast, a **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient) Thermistor** exhibits an **increase** in resistance with rising temperature. It is commonly used as a **safety switch** (e.g., for over-temperature protection in electric vehicle battery packs and radiator fan circuits).

---

## 🧠 2.3 The Thermocouple

A **thermocouple** operates on the **Seebeck Effect**:
- Two dissimilar metals are joined at a junction (the *hot junction*).
- When heated, a small EMF (typically in millivolts) is generated proportional to the temperature difference between the hot and cold (reference) junctions.
- **Type K Thermocouple (Chromel–Alumel)** is the industry standard for **EGT sensors** in diesel vehicles due to its wide range and ruggedness.

---

## 🧠 2.4 The RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector)

An **RTD** uses a pure metal (typically platinum) whose resistance increases linearly with temperature. **Pt100 (100 Ω at 0 °C)** and **Pt1000 (1000 Ω at 0 °C)** are common automotive variants.

---

## 🧠 2.5 The Bimetallic Strip

A **bimetallic strip** consists of two metals with different coefficients of thermal expansion bonded together. As temperature changes, the strip bends, mechanically moving a needle on an analog gauge. This is found in older vehicles.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Mandatory for Board Exams**

$$
\boxed{R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\,B\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)}}
$$

The above is the **Steinhart–Hart Approximation (Single-Term Beta Model)** for NTC thermistors.

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
|--------|----------|------|
| $R_T$ | Resistance of thermistor at temperature $T$ | $\Omega$ |
| $R_0$ | Resistance of thermistor at reference temperature $T_0$ (usually 25 °C = 298.15 K) | $\Omega$ |
| $B$ | Beta constant (material-specific, typically 3000–5000 K) | K |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature (Kelvin) | K |
| $T_0$ | Reference absolute temperature | K |
| $\alpha$ | Temperature coefficient of resistance | /°C |

---

### 🔧 KTU Voltage Divider Equation (Most Frequently Asked!)

In an automotive ECU, the NTC sensor is connected in a **voltage divider** with a fixed reference resistor $R_{ref}$.

$$
\boxed{V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_{sensor}}{R_{sensor} + R_{ref}}}
$$

The ECU applies a regulated **5 V reference voltage** and reads back $V_{out}$ through its ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter).

---

### 📐 Linearized NTC Voltage Output Derivation (Simplified)

For small temperature ranges, the NTC characteristic can be **linearized**:

$$
\boxed{V_{out}(T) \approx V_{ref}\!\left[\,\tfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{B}{4 T_0^2}\,(T - T_0)\,\right]}
$$

This linearization allows the ECU's lookup table to be more compact.

---

### 🌡️ Thermocouple EMF Equation (Seebeck)

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{E} = S(T) \cdot \Delta T}
$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{E}$ = generated Seebeck voltage (Volts)
- $S(T)$ = Seebeck coefficient (V/°C) — for Type K, ≈ **41 μV/°C**
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference between hot and cold junctions

---

### 🔩 RTD Resistance Equation (Callendar–Van Dusen)

$$
\boxed{R_T = R_0\!\left[\,1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2\,\right]}
$$

For platinum RTDs: $\alpha \approx \mathbf{3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,/°C}$, $\beta \approx -5.8 \times 10^{-7}\,/°C^2$.

---

## 🏎️ Real-World Automotive Engineering Utility

| Sensor | Location | ECU Function |
|--------|----------|--------------|
| **ECT Sensor** | Thermostat housing / cylinder head | Controls fuel enrichment during cold start, ON/OFF of radiator fan, dashboard gauge |
| **IAT Sensor** | Intake manifold / air filter housing | Adjusts air-fuel ratio, ignition timing density compensation |
| **EGT Sensor** | Exhaust manifold / turbocharger outlet | Protects DPF and turbocharger from overheating |
| **Oil Temp Sensor** | Oil pan / oil gallery | Activates warning lamp, adjusts VVT timing |
| **Ambient Air Sensor** | Front grille / bumper | Automatic HVAC control, automatic headlamp activation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are written in exhaustive detail. **Every algebraic step** is preserved for KTU board examination purposes. No shortcuts have been taken.

---

## 🧮 3.1 Derivation: NTC Thermistor Resistance from First Principles

### Step 1 — Semiconductor Conductivity Foundation
The electrical conductivity of a semiconductor depends on charge carrier concentration:

$$
\sigma = n \cdot q \cdot \mu_n + p \cdot q \cdot \mu_p
$$

Where $n, p$ are electron and hole concentrations, $q$ is electron charge, $\mu$ are mobilities.

### Step 2 — Intrinsic Carrier Concentration
For an intrinsic semiconductor (like a sintered metal oxide thermistor), carrier concentration follows the Boltzmann exponential:

$$
n_i \propto T^{\frac{3}{2}} \cdot e^{-\dfrac{E_g}{2 k_B T}}
$$

Where:
- $E_g$ = bandgap energy (eV)
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant = $\mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,J/K}$
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### Step 3 — Resistance Inversion
Since $R \propto 1/\sigma$, and $\sigma \propto n_i$, we obtain:

$$
R(T) \propto \dfrac{1}{T^{\frac{3}{2}}} \cdot e^{+\dfrac{E_g}{2 k_B T}}
$$

### Step 4 — Empirical Beta-Model Simplification
For engineering use, this is empirically compressed into the **Beta equation**:

$$
R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\,B\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)}
$$

This is the **industry-standard KTU formula** for NTC behavior.

---

## 🧮 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — ECT Sensor at 80 °C

**Given:**
- Sensor: NTC thermistor with $R_0 = 2.5\,\text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 25\,°C$
- Beta constant $B = 3950\,K$
- Operating temperature $T = 80\,°C$
- Reference resistor $R_{ref} = 2.5\,\text{k}\Omega$
- Supply voltage $V_{ref} = 5\,V$

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$
T_0 = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15\,K
$$

$$
T = 80 + 273.15 = 353.15\,K
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponent:**

$$
B \left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right) = 3950 \left(\dfrac{1}{353.15} - \dfrac{1}{298.15}\right)
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{353.15} = 2.8317 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{298.15} = 3.3540 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\Delta = (2.8317 - 3.3540) \times 10^{-3} = -0.5223 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
B \cdot \Delta = 3950 \times (-0.5223 \times 10^{-3}) = -2.0631
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the resistance:**

$$
R_T = 2500 \cdot e^{-2.0631}
$$

$$
e^{-2.0631} = 0.1269
$$

$$
\boxed{R_T \approx 317.3\,\Omega}
$$

This shows the thermistor drops from **2.5 kΩ at 25 °C** to about **317 Ω at 80 °C** — a strong, usable signal.

**Step 4 — Compute the voltage divider output:**

$$
V_{out} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{317.3}{317.3 + 2500} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{317.3}{2817.3}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 5 \cdot 0.1126
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{out} \approx 0.563\,V}
$$

The ECU reads **0.563 V at 80 °C** — a clear, measurable signal well within the ADC's 0–5 V range.

---

## 🧮 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparative matrix is the most frequently expected 7-mark question in KTU Module 4.**

| Parameter | NTC Thermistor | PTC Thermistor | Thermocouple (Type K) | RTD (Pt100) | Bimetallic |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|------------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Output Type** | Resistance change | Resistance change | EMF (mV) | Resistance change | Mechanical displacement |
| **Sensitivity** | High | Moderate | Low | Moderate | N/A |
| **Range** | −55 to +300 °C | −55 to +150 °C | −200 to +1370 °C | −200 to +850 °C | 0 to +150 °C |
| **Linearity** | Exponential (needs LUT) | Non-linear | Approximately linear | Highly linear | Non-linear |
| **Response Time** | Fast (1–5 s) | Fast | Very fast (ms) | Moderate (3–10 s) | Slow |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | Moderate | High | Very low |
| **ECU Interface** | Voltage divider + ADC | Voltage divider + ADC | Amplifier + ADC | Wheatstone bridge | Direct gauge |
| **Primary Auto Use** | ECT, IAT, Oil Temp | Fan switch, protection | EGT | High-precision labs | Analog dashboard |

---

## 🧮 3.4 Derivation: Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR)

For any resistive temperature sensor, the **TCR (α)** is defined as the fractional change in resistance per degree Celsius:

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{1}{R_0}\cdot\dfrac{dR}{dT}
$$

For the NTC Beta model, differentiating with respect to $T$:

$$
\dfrac{dR_T}{dT} = R_0 \cdot e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)} \cdot \left(-\dfrac{B}{T^2}\right) = -\dfrac{B}{T^2} \cdot R_T
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\alpha = -\dfrac{B}{T^2}}
$$

The **negative sign** confirms the NTC nature (resistance decreases with temperature rise). At $T = 298.15\,K$ and $B = 3950$:

$$
\alpha = -\dfrac{3950}{(298.15)^2} \approx -0.0444\,/°C = \mathbf{-4.44\%/°C}
$$

This means the sensor's resistance changes by **~4.44% per degree Celsius** at room temperature — extremely sensitive.

## 📊 4.3 NTC Thermistor Voltage Divider Circuit (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["5 VOLT REGULATED SUPPLY FROM ECU"]
    nodeB["REFERENCE RESISTOR RREF TYPICALLY 2.5 KILOHM"]
    nodeC["NTC THERMISTOR RSENSOR LOCATED IN ENGINE"]
    nodeD["VOUT NODE BETWEEN RREF AND RSENSOR"]
    nodeE["ECU ADC INPUT PIN"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeC
    nodeD --> nodeE
```

**Operating Logic:**
- When temperature **rises** → $R_{sensor}$ **falls** → $V_{out}$ **falls** → ECU interprets as **hot**.
- When temperature **falls** → $R_{sensor}$ **rises** → $V_{out}$ **rises** → ECU interprets as **cold**.
